document_information.title: Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council
document_information.long_title: Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 13 June 2024 laying down harmonised rules on artificial intelligence and amending Regulations (EC) No 300/2008, (EU) No 167/2013, (EU) No 168/2013, (EU) 2018/858, (EU) 2018/1139 and (EU) 2019/2144 and Directives 2014/90/EU, (EU) 2016/797 and (EU) 2020/1828 (Artificial Intelligence Act)

I.summary_1: The items together establish a comprehensive EU framework to promote human-centric, trustworthy AI while protecting health, safety, fundamental rights, democracy, the rule of law, and the environment. They set harmonized requirements for bringing AI to market, putting it into service, and use, including prohibitions on certain practices, risk-based obligations for high-risk systems, transparency, governance, enforcement, and rules for general-purpose AI, with measures to support innovation, especially for SMEs and startups. The scope covers providers, deployers, and related stakeholders in the EU, clarifies ties to existing product legislation, data protection, and worker protections, and excludes military or national security activities, pre-market research, and non-EU law areas, with open-source licenses treated unless high-risk or governed by specific provisions. Core definitions establish the terminology and roles (such as provider, deployer, importer, distributor, operator) and outline concepts like placing on the market, making available, putting into service, and intended use, along with mechanisms for conformity assessment, real-world testing, and post-market monitoring. Finally, providers and deployers must ensure adequate AI literacy among staff and users, tailoring training to the technical knowledge and context of those involved.
I.summary_2:
The material describes a comprehensive EU Regulation that harmonises rules for artificial intelligence across the Union to promote trustworthy, human-centric AI while safeguarding health, safety, fundamental rights (including democracy and the rule of law) and the environment, and to support innovation. It establishes a framework for the placement on the market, putting into service, and use of AI systems, including prohibitions on certain practices, specific obligations for high-risk AI, and harmonised transparency requirements for certain systems and general-purpose AI models, with governance, market monitoring, and enforcement provisions. It aims to foster innovation, with particular emphasis on SMEs and startups.
The Regulation has a broad cross-border scope, applying to EU and non-EU providers and deployers when outputs are used in the Union, as well as importers, distributors, product manufacturers, authorized representatives, and individuals in the Union. When AI systems are classified as high-risk, core high-risk provisions and related enforcement apply, with the regulatory sandbox concept limited to what those harmonised rules permit; sector-specific legislation may address some high-risk requirements. Important exclusions and regime specifics shape the scope: it does not cover activities outside EU law, military, defence, or national security purposes; outputs used exclusively for military purposes are excluded; public authorities of third countries are exempt in certain international cooperation contexts; it does not alter intermediary-liability rules or completely exclude open-source AI unless it is placed on the market or put into service as high-risk; personal data protection remains governed by EU data-protection rules. It interacts with other EU laws and does not override consumer protection or general product safety rules, and allows Member States or the Union to provide stronger worker protections; it does not apply to purely personal, non-professional use or to OSS unless placed on the market or deemed high-risk.
Definitions establish the core concepts and governance for the AI lifecycle: an AI system is a machine-based, (partially) autonomous system that may adapt after deployment and produces outputs that can affect real or virtual environments; risk combines probability of harm and severity; actors include providers, deployers, authorised representatives, importers, distributors, and operators; placing on the market, making available, and putting into service tie to first access, commercial supply, and initial use in the Union. The framework relies on conformity assessments and designated bodies, CE marking, post-market monitoring, market surveillance, and harmonised standards to ensure ongoing compliance. The terminology also covers data, biometric processing, and privacy considerations, with distinctions among training, validation, and testing data, and discussions of personal data, biometric data, sensitive categories, emotion recognition, and real-time identification. It introduces governance and risk concepts, the AI Office and national authorities, high-risk and general-purpose AI models, downstream providers, and technical aspects like testing data and floating-point operations, forming a comprehensive vocabulary for safety, compliance, data handling, and risk management.
Finally, providers and deployers must implement measures to ensure AI literacy among staff and users, tailored to individuals' knowledge, experience, training context, and user characteristics.

II.summary_1: The provisions collectively ban AI practices that manipulate or deceive individuals, exploit vulnerabilities, or rely on social scoring and criminal-risk profiling, including the untargeted scraping of facial images to build recognition databases and the inference of emotions in workplace or educational settings (except where medically or safety justified). They severely restrict real-time remote biometric identification by law enforcement in public spaces, requiring prior authorization by a judicial or independent authority, a rights impact assessment, narrowly tailored scope, and registration in an EU database, with time-limited urgent-case allowances and mandatory data deletion if not authorized. They also impose national rulemaking and reporting duties--notification to market surveillance and data protection authorities and annual reporting to the Commission--while preserving other EU prohibitions.
II.summary_2:
The set of provisions establishes a comprehensive framework to curb AI practices that could undermine individual autonomy, safety, or nondiscrimination. It bans subliminal or deceptive techniques designed to distort behavior and cause significant harm, and it prohibits AI that exploits vulnerabilities based on age, disability, or socio-economic status. It further restricts attempts to evaluate or classify people over time based on social behavior or inferred characteristics in ways that could lead to harmful or unjustified treatment. Where risk assessments rely on profiling or personality traits, such use is generally excluded unless it supports human, fact-based assessments of criminal activity. The rules also bar creating or expanding facial recognition databases from untargeted scraping, inferring emotions in most work or education contexts (except for medical or safety reasons), and biometric categorization that reveals sensitive attributes, with limited exceptions for law enforcement labeling or filtering of lawfully obtained biometric data.
Real-time remote biometric identification in publicly accessible spaces for law enforcement is broadly prohibited, with three narrowly defined exceptions: targeted searches for victims of abduction, trafficking, sexual exploitation, or missing persons; prevention of a specific, substantial and imminent threat to life or safety or a current/foreseeable terrorist threat; and locating or identifying a person suspected of a crime punishable by at least four years in prison. Even within these exceptions, the broader prohibition remains subject to data-protection safeguards and does not override GDPR provisions for biometric data processing in non-law-enforcement contexts. When such identification is used by law enforcement, it must be limited to confirming the identity of the targeted individual and must balance the seriousness and likelihood of harm if not used against the rights and freedoms of all involved, with strict adherence to safeguards, national law, and temporal, geographic, and personal limits, plus prior assessment and registration.
Deployment of these technologies requires notification to both the national market surveillance authority and the national data protection authority, including specified information while excluding sensitive operational data. Member States may enact more restrictive national rules for requests, issuances, supervision, and reporting of authorizations and must inform the European Commission within 30 days. Annual reporting on these uses is required from national authorities to the Commission, which will provide templates and publish aggregated annual reports that exclude sensitive operational data. The Commission's reporting will summarize aggregated data, and nothing in these provisions diminishes prohibitions under other Union law.

1.unit_title: Classification of AI systems as high-risk
1.summary_1:
The set establishes a framework to identify high-risk AI systems, focusing on those that function as safety components or products requiring third-party conformity assessment, and it adds an expanded high-risk category with derogations for certain non-significant uses (procedural tasks, improving prior results, detecting decision patterns without replacing human review, or preparatory tasks); however profiling of natural persons remains always high-risk. Providers must document assessments for these high-risk uses, and the Commission may issue guidelines by a set date and amend the rules through delegated acts, while ensuring that protection is not diminished.
Delegated acts may add or modify high-risk uses in the annex when they pose health or safety risks or adverse effects on fundamental rights, using criteria such as intended purpose, extent of use, data processed (including special categories), autonomy and human override, prior harms or concerns, potential magnitude of harm, feasibility of opting out, vulnerability or power imbalances, reversibility, overall benefits, and alignment with EU redress law. The Commission may also remove high-risk designations if the risks are no longer significant and such removal does not reduce overall protection under Union law.
1.summary_2:
The material describes a risk-based framework for AI under the Union harmonisation scheme, linking high-risk status to the product safety regime and conformity requirements. High-risk classification applies when an AI system is used as a safety component of a product or is itself a product covered by applicable harmonisation legislation, requiring a third-party conformity assessment before market or service. In addition, certain AI systems listed in a defined set of use-cases are treated as high-risk, but a derogation allows some Annex III systems to escape high-risk status if they do not pose significant risks to health, safety, or fundamental rights or if they merely support or augment human activity rather than replace it. Examples of such non-high-risk tasks include narrow procedural functions, enhancing outcomes of completed human activity with suitable human review, detecting decision-making patterns without supplanting human assessment, or performing preparatory tasks for assessments; however, profiling of natural persons remains always high-risk. If a provider considers an Annex III system not high-risk, they must document the assessment prior to market and comply with a registration obligation, with the documentation available to national authorities on request. Practical guidance is to be issued by the Commission by 2 February 2026 after consulting the European AI Board, including examples distinguishing high-risk and non-high-risk use cases. The Commission may adopt delegated acts to add, modify, or delete the conditions for the derogation, provided there is concrete, reliable evidence, protections are not weakened, consistency with other delegated acts is maintained, and the changes reflect market and technological developments.
Separately, the framework empowers the Commission to amend Annex III through delegated acts to add or modify high-risk use-cases when two conditions are met: the AI is intended for use in areas already listed in Annex III, and the AI poses health and safety risks or adverse effects on fundamental rights at a level equal to or greater than existing high-risk systems. A non-exhaustive set of risk criteria guides the assessment, including the AI's intended purpose, breadth of use, data processed (including special categories), autonomy and human override, prior harms or rights concerns, the magnitude and distribution of harm, dependence on the outcome, power imbalances or vulnerabilities, ease of correction or reversal, the potential societal benefit, and whether current Union law provides effective redress and risk prevention measures. The Commission may also remove high-risk designation if the system no longer poses significant risks under these criteria, ensuring that deletion does not undermine overall protections. This framework thus contemplates both expansion and contraction of high-risk coverage based on rights-protective assessment.

2.summary_1: Together, these provisions create a comprehensive regulatory framework for high-risk AI systems aimed at protecting health, safety, and fundamental rights while ensuring compliance with EU harmonisation rules. They require a continuous lifecycle risk-management approach that identifies, analyzes, and mitigates known and foreseeable risks, integrates post-market monitoring, and mandates pre-market testing, including real-world testing. They impose rigorous data governance for training, validation, and testing data--ensuring relevance, representativeness, bias detection and mitigation, data-origin transparency, and privacy protections--with stricter safeguards for processing special categories of data; if no training data is used, governance focuses on testing data. They demand robust technical documentation, automatic event logging for traceability, and clear, accessible instructions to deployers, including system characteristics, limitations, and interpretation guidance. They also mandate effective human oversight and, where required, independent verification, along with explicit, benchmarked requirements for accuracy, robustness, and cybersecurity to defend against faults, manipulation, and attacks on data or models.
2.summary_2:
The material lays out a comprehensive EU regime for high-risk AI systems focused on safety, fundamental rights, and accountability throughout the system's lifecycle. It centers on a tightly integrated framework of risk management, data governance, documentation, traceability, transparency, human oversight, and robust performance and security requirements, all designed to ensure conformity with harmonised rules and to facilitate reliable deployment in the market.
Key elements include a continuous, iterative risk management process that identifies health, safety, and rights risks for the intended use, estimates and mitigates risks (including foreseeable misuse), and uses post-market monitoring data to adapt measures. Risk controls must be practicable through design and information provision, with attention to interaction among regulatory requirements and to protecting vulnerable groups; testing is required at various development stages and, when allowed, in real-world settings, before market entry or deployment.
When training data are involved, data governance and management must ensure high data quality and representativeness, with explicit attention to biases that could affect health, safety, or rights. This includes careful handling of data collection, preparation, annotation, and labeling, along with assessing data availability, quantity, suitability, and bias mitigation. Exceptional processing of special categories of personal data is allowed only for bias detection and correction under strict safeguards. For non-training systems, the related data testing provisions apply to testing data sets.
Technical documentation must be prepared before market or service introduction and kept up to date, enabling authorities and notified bodies to assess conformity. A core set of elements must be provided (with simplified forms available for SMEs) and, for products covered by harmonisation acts, a single, integrated documentation package must be created. The Commission can update the documentation framework through delegated acts to reflect technical progress.
High-risk AI systems must automatically record logs across their operational life to support traceability, post-market monitoring, and ongoing operation oversight. Logging requirements are tailored to the system's subset of applicability, including details such as use durations, input data references, and implicated individuals where relevant.
Transparency and usable instruction are mandatory, with systems designed so their operation is interpretable and deployers can understand outputs. Instructions must be concise, complete, and accessible, covering provider identity, system capabilities and limitations, tested performance, known risks, data set information, methods to explain outputs, and any predetermined changes identified during conformity assessment. They must also describe human-oversight measures, required technical and hardware resources, expected lifetime, maintenance needs, and any mechanisms for logs and monitoring in line with the other requirements.
Human oversight is a core design principle, ensuring that natural persons can effectively supervise system use, detect anomalies, avoid automation bias, interpret outputs, and intervene to ensure safety. In biometric or remote identification contexts, independent verification by at least two competent natural persons is required for actions or decisions, except where law or policy explicitly disallows such verification.
Performance, accuracy, robustness, and cybersecurity are mandatory design objectives sustained throughout the lifecycle. Providers must work with stakeholders to develop benchmarks and measurement methodologies appropriate to context, and disclose explicit performance levels and metrics to users. Systems should be as resilient as possible to errors, with safeguards, redundancy, and fail-safe mechanisms. For systems that continue to learn after deployment, designers must minimize bias in feedback loops and implement mitigation against data or model poisoning, adversarial inputs, confidentiality attacks, and other vulnerabilities.
Overall, the regime envisions a tightly coupled set of governance, technical, and organizational measures that collectively ensure high-risk AI systems are safe, trustworthy, auditable, and aligned with fundamental rights, while enabling compliance, market access, and ongoing accountability through lifecycle management and regular updating of requirements.

3.unit_title: Obligations of providers and deployers of high-risk AI systems and other parties
3.summary_1: These provisions establish a comprehensive EU framework for high-risk AI systems, governing the entire lifecycle and all actors in the supply chain. They require providers to implement a documented quality management system, align design, development, data management, risk management, post-market monitoring, incident reporting, governance, and communications with authorities, and to carry out conformity assessments and obtain CE marking where feasible. They mandate extensive documentation, including technical documentation and quality management system records, as well as automatically generated logs, to be retained for long periods. Providers must take immediate corrective actions for non-conforming systems and cooperate with authorities, with clear disclosure to distributors, deployers, and importers. Non-EU providers must appoint an EU-based authorized representative, and importers and distributors have explicit duties to verify conformity, keep documentation for a decade, display importer details, and inform authorities of risks. Deployers must use high-risk AI systems under appropriate human oversight, monitor performance, maintain logs, inform workers, and conduct fundamental rights impact assessments prior to use, while considering data protection requirements. The regime also covers changes in responsibility when third parties become providers, cooperation requirements with authorities, post-market surveillance and incident reporting, and special provisions for financial institutions and for safety components of products.
3.summary_2:
The material outlines a comprehensive, lifecycle-oriented regime for high-risk AI systems that places broad obligations on providers, including those outside the EU, as well as on importers, distributors, and deployers. It emphasizes governance, accountability, transparency, and ongoing conformity, with strong coordination among regulators, notified bodies, and market surveillance authorities.
Key themes and obligations include:
- Governance and conformity at the system level: Providers must implement a robust quality management system and ensure overall regulatory compliance. They are required to subject high-risk AI systems to appropriate conformity assessment before market entry, draft an EU declaration of conformity, and affix the CE marking. They must maintain technical and governance documents, participate in registration regimes, and implement prompt corrective actions for non-conformities, including informing distributors and authorities and demonstrating conformity to national authorities upon request. They must clearly identify themselves and provide contact details on the product or its packaging/documentation, and ensure accessibility in line with EU directives.
- Documentation, logs, and data retention: Providers must retain core technical documentation, QMS documentation, changes approved by notified bodies, and the EU declaration of conformity for a 10-year period, making them available to competent authorities. They must also retain automatically generated logs for a minimum period (six months, extendable by law) and ensure documentation supports ongoing conformity. Special considerations apply to financial institutions under Union financial services law. Importantly, there are safeguards for bankruptcy or cessation of activity, and confidentiality and data protection considerations accompany access to documentation and logs.
- Roles and responsibilities across the supply chain: The regime assigns clear duties to EU-based authorized representatives for non-EU providers, who must verify conformity, keep documentation available, and cooperate with authorities for a 10-year window, including access to logs. Importers must verify conformity before market, ensure CE marking and the EU declaration of conformity, label systems with their contact details, maintain documentation for 10 years, and cooperate with authorities. Distributors must verify CE marking and documentation, take corrective action if non-conformities are found, and inform authorities of risks. A mechanism for collaboration among providers, importers, distributors, and deployers is established, with written agreements specifying required information and access to ensure full regulatory compliance; in some cases, the product manufacturer is deemed the provider for safety components.
- Deployment, use, and ongoing monitoring: Deployers must implement appropriate technical and organizational measures to ensure use according to instructions, assign trained human oversight, monitor operations, and suspend use if risks arise. They must retain logs under their control for a defined period and inform workers and authorities about deployments, particularly in workplace settings. Public authorities must comply with registration requirements and refrain from use if not registered. Deployers must address post-market observations, report serious incidents, and supply information to authorities on request. For post-remote biometric identification, there are explicit authorization requirements and strict limits on untargeted law-enforcement use, with mandatory documentation and reporting.
- Risk assessment and rights-based safeguards: Before deploying a high-risk system, certain deployers must conduct a fundamental rights impact assessment, addressing how the system will be used, the time frame and frequency, affected individuals or groups, specific harms or risks, human oversight arrangements, and risk response measures. The assessment is due before first use and must be updated if circumstances change. The AI Office is tasked with developing a template to facilitate this assessment. If aspects are already covered by a data protection impact assessment or similar DPIA, the new assessment supplements those requirements.
- Confidentiality, language, and cross-border coordination: Information obtained by authorities remains subject to confidentiality protections. Access rights and documentation are to be provided in an official EU language designated by the Member State. The regime also contemplates coordination between national authorities, EU-based representatives, and the AI Office to streamline compliance and enforcement.
In sum, the set of provisions creates an integrated, cross-role framework governing the introduction, use, monitoring, and accountability of high-risk AI systems in the EU, emphasizing preventive conformity, rigorous documentation, ongoing oversight, responsible handling of information, and respect for fundamental rights throughout the system's lifecycle.

4.summary_1:
The set establishes a unified EU framework for the designation and oversight of conformity assessment bodies and their authorities in relation to high-risk AI systems. It requires Member States to designate notifying authorities that set up procedures to assess, designate, and monitor conformity assessment bodies, with the possibility to delegate to national accreditation bodies. These authorities must avoid conflicts of interest, ensure impartial decision-making, protect confidential information, and employ skilled staff with expertise in IT, AI, and law, while refraining from providing related services.
Conformity assessment bodies must apply for notification with details about their activities, the conformity assessment modules, the AI system types they assess, and any accreditation. If accreditation is not available, they must supply documentary evidence for verification and ongoing monitoring. Existing designations under other harmonisation rules may be supported by current documentation, with prompt updates to ensure continuous compliance.
Notifying authorities may only notify bodies that meet defined criteria and must inform the Commission and other Member States electronically, sharing full details of activities, modules, AI types, and competence attestations. If accreditation is not present, documentary evidence suffices to verify competence and ongoing monitoring arrangements. Authorization follows after defined objection periods, with the Commission consulting Member States if objections arise.
Notified bodies must be established under national law with legal personality, independent from high-risk AI system providers, and must safeguard impartiality and confidentiality. They must maintain liability insurance or national equivalents, ensure internal expertise and staff, and participate in standardisation and coordination activities. Subcontracting is allowed only if subcontractors meet requirements and the notifying authority is informed, with full responsibility retained by the notifying body and records kept.
Conformity assessment bodies that demonstrate conformity with harmonised standards are presumed to meet the criteria. Notified bodies are responsible for verifying conformity of high-risk AI systems, balancing rigorous protection with minimizing provider burden, and must provide relevant documentation to the notifying authority.
The Commission assigns a unique identification number to each notified body and maintains a public, up-to-date list of all notified bodies and their notified activities. Changes to notification procedures require reporting to the Commission and other Member States, with safeguards for cessation, suspension, or withdrawal of designation and ongoing record-keeping to ensure continued conformity of high-risk AI systems.
The Commission may investigate cases questioning a notified body's competence or ongoing compliance, with confidential handling of sensitive information. If deficiencies are found, corrective measures, including suspension or withdrawal, may be imposed. A sectoral group of notified bodies will coordinate conformity assessment activities for high-risk AI systems, with compulsory participation and knowledge exchange facilitated by the Commission.
Finally, third-country conformity assessment bodies may be authorized to perform notified-body activities if they meet the requirements or under international agreements that recognize or accept such bodies.
4.summary_2:
The set of provisions establishes a comprehensive EU framework for the designation, notification, monitoring, and coordination of entities involved in conformity assessment of high-risk AI systems. It creates a two-tier system in which Member States designate notifying authorities to manage the procedures for assessing, designating, and supervising conformity assessment bodies (CABs), with the option to entrust some tasks to national accreditation bodies, provided independence and objectivity are safeguarded. Notifying authorities must avoid conflicts of interest, safeguard confidentiality, and employ competent personnel with expertise in relevant areas such as information technologies, AI, and law; they are prohibited from offering conformity assessment activities or commercial consultancy to CABs.
CABs seeking designation must submit a detailed notification file describing their conformity assessment activities, the modules and AI types they cover, and their accreditation status or other documentary evidence of compliance; existing designations under other EU harmonisation acts may support the designation, with ongoing updating to reflect changes. Notifications to the Commission and other Member States require robust evidence of competence, and are subject to timely objections; the Commission may engage in consultations and decide on the justification of any designation. Notified bodies must be created under national law, possess independence from high-risk AI system providers and related economic interests, and avoid involvement in the design or marketing of high-risk AI systems, while maintaining confidentiality, professional secrecy, liability insurance, and adequate internal expertise. They are expected to participate in coordination efforts and stay aligned with European standards.
Notified bodies may rely on harmonised standards to presume conformity for the aspects those standards cover, while subcontracting is allowed only with retained responsibility and a publicly accessible list of subsidiaries; subcontractors' qualifications and work must be documented and kept available for years. Notified bodies must verify high-risk AI systems according to designated conformity assessment procedures, balancing due diligence with efforts to minimize administrative burdens for smaller providers, and must provide relevant documentation to the notifying authority to support designation, notification, and monitoring. Each notified body receives a unique identification number, and the Commission publishes a current, cross-regulatory list of notified bodies and their notified activities. Changes to notifications, including extensions or reductions of scope, and cessation, suspension, or withdrawal of designations, trigger specific procedural steps, timelines, and potential transitional arrangements for certificates; the Commission and Member States must be informed of such changes, and corrective actions may be taken if a body fails to meet requirements.
The Commission may investigate suspected failures in a notified body's competence or ongoing compliance, with confidentiality safeguards for sensitive information. A sectoral group of notified bodies is established to coordinate conformity assessment practices for high-risk AI systems, with participation by designated bodies and information exchange facilitated among authorities. Third-country conformity assessment bodies may be authorized to perform notified-body functions if they meet applicable requirements or demonstrate equivalent compliance, ensuring international participation aligns with the Regulation's standards. Overall, the framework emphasizes independence, objectivity, transparency, ongoing monitoring, cross-border cooperation, and risk-based administration to support rigorous yet proportionate conformity assessment of high-risk AI systems.

5.summary_1: These provisions create an integrated EU framework for high-risk AI systems and general-purpose models, tying standards development, conformity assessment, marking, and market operation to safety, cybersecurity, accountability, and fundamental rights. Where harmonised standards exist, conformity is presumed; where they do not, or are insufficient, the Commission may adopt common specifications through implementing acts after consultation, with earlier acts repealed as applicable and state-level flags for amendments possible. Conformity assessment offers two routes: an internal control path for systems aligned with the standards or specifications, and a notified-body assessment for others, covering quality management and technical documentation. Certificates issued by notified bodies must be clear, with defined validity periods, renewal or suspension rules, and an obligation for notifying bodies to report certificates, refusals, and changes and to share relevant information while protecting confidentiality. Temporary or urgent authorisations allow authorities to deploy high-risk AI in exceptional public security or life-saving contexts, subject to post-deployment review and Commission oversight. Providers must produce a written EU declaration of conformity, keep it for a decade, ensure it covers the applicable requirements, and translate it for authorities; a single declaration may suffice if other EU harmonisation laws apply. A digital or traditional CE mark must be visible and traceable, reflecting any overlapping Union rules. Finally, high-risk AI systems and certain others must be registered in an EU database before market use, with special handling for security-relevant deployments and differentiated registration paths for different system categories.
5.summary_2:
The material presents a comprehensive EU regulatory framework for high-risk AI systems and certain general-purpose AI models, built around harmonised standards and a structured conformity regime. The overarching aim is to safeguard fundamental rights while fostering innovation, legal certainty, and EU market competitiveness, and to align EU rules with compatible international standards through inclusive, multi-stakeholder governance. The framework requires standardisation bodies to develop harmonised standards and accompanying deliverables on reporting, documentation, energy/resource efficiency, and responsible development, with the Commission coordinating standardisation requests, consulting an advisory forum, and seeking evidence of best efforts. It also emphasizes keeping standards aligned with existing EU harmonisation laws and international progress.
The regime then lays out the pathways and conditions for conformity and market access. Implementing acts may establish common specifications when harmonised standards are unavailable, insufficient, or fail to address rights concerns; such acts require consultation, and, if a harmonised standard is adopted later, the Commission must assess and possibly repeal related implementing acts. High-risk AI systems that conform to these specifications are presumptively compliant with the corresponding Section 2 requirements or Chapter V obligations. For data governance, data used to train and test high-risk systems reflecting their use context is presumed to meet quality and bias management standards, and cybersecurity conformity can be demonstrated via prior certification under a relevant scheme. Conformity assessment offers two paths for systems covered by harmonised standards: internal control or a third-party route with a notified body; some subcategories require internal control only, while others may be regulated by Union harmonisation acts with designated notified bodies. Substantial modifications trigger a new conformity assessment. Delegated acts may update annexes and extend conformity assessment scopes.
Certificates and declarations of conformity are central: providers must issue a signed EU declaration and maintain it for a long period, with translations and the possibility of consolidation across related harmonisation acts; CE marking, including digital marking for digitally supplied systems, must accompany the product and may include the notified body's identification number. Notified bodies have extensive information-sharing and confidentiality duties and must promptly inform authorities about certification activities, refusals, suspensions, or changes. Market surveillance authorities may grant temporary market access for exceptional public security or safety reasons, with mechanisms for rapid review and potential withdrawal if non-compliant. Providers must register high-risk systems in an EU database before market introduction or deployment, with specific rules for public authorities, law-enforcement and border control contexts, and some categories requiring national-level registration or secure non-public database sections. The regime envisions ongoing updates by delegated acts to reflect technical progress and evolving risk management and fundamental rights protections.

unit_definitions.value: the European Artificial Intelligence Board (the 'Board')

III.summary_1: These provisions establish a unified EU framework for high-risk AI systems that covers their entire lifecycle and all actors in the supply chain, mandating continuous risk management, post-market monitoring, and rigorous pre-market testing (including real-world testing) along with robust data governance for training, validation, and testing data, with stronger safeguards for processing sensitive data. They expand the high-risk category and allow delegated acts to update the list based on risk criteria, while maintaining protections against profiling of natural persons. Providers must maintain comprehensive technical documentation, automatic event logging, and clear deployment instructions, and deployers must oversee systems with appropriate human oversight and rights impact assessments, with independent verification where required. Conformity assessment and CE marking are central, imposing duties on EU-based representatives for non-EU providers, as well as importers and distributors, with long-term documentation retention and mandatory risk communication to authorities. A parallel framework governs conformity assessment bodies through notifying authorities, ensuring impartiality, expertise, confidentiality, and a public registry; conformity can be demonstrated via internal control against harmonized standards or by a notified body, with certificates, renewal rules, and potential urgent authorisations. The regime interoperates with EU standards development, allows for recognition of third-country bodies under international agreements, and requires registration of high-risk systems in an EU database, including sector-specific rules for finance and safety components.
III.summary_2:
The material describes a comprehensive, EU-wide framework for high-risk AI systems that integrates risk-based designation, lifecycle governance, and a structured conformity regime designed to protect fundamental rights while enabling innovation and market access. The regime ties a high-risk classification to safety and product conformity, with a special set of Annex III use cases that can be derogated from high-risk status under strict conditions. Delegated acts enable the Commission to expand or contract high-risk coverage based on systematic, rights-centered criteria, including the AI's intended use, data handling, autonomy, potential harms, and the possibility of redress under Union law. In parallel, a core requirement is that certain AI systems, especially those used as safety components or covered by harmonisation acts, undergo third-party conformity assessments before market entry, while others may rely on internal controls or other pathways if aligned with harmonised standards or implementing acts. The regime contemplates ongoing updates to annexes and criteria as technology and market conditions evolve, with a strong emphasis on protecting health, safety, and fundamental rights.
From a lifecycle perspective, the regime imposes a tightly integrated governance model that spans risk management, data governance, documentation, traceability, transparency, human oversight, and cybersecurity. It requires continuous risk assessment and mitigation, post-market monitoring, and iterative refinement of safeguards based on real-world data. Training data handling is subject to rigorous governance to ensure quality and representativeness, with explicit attention to bias and the protection of sensitive information. Technical documentation must be prepared and maintained to support conformity assessments, and high-risk systems must automatically log operational data to support traceability and oversight. Transparency obligations mandate usable, accessible instructions about system capabilities, limitations, risks, data sources, and human oversight, while preserving left-over protections for vulnerable groups. Human oversight is central, with particular emphasis on meaningful supervision, interpretation of outputs, and intervention capabilities; biometric or remote identification contexts include stringent verification and accountability requirements. The regime also requires robust performance, robustness, and cybersecurity standards, with clear benchmarks and mitigation strategies for errors, adversarial threats, and evolving risks, and it anticipates ongoing updates to standards as part of the risk-management framework.
The provisions place broad governance and liability on providers--including non-EU entities--along with importers, distributors, and deployers, promoting governance, accountability, transparency, and ongoing conformity. Providers must implement quality management systems, conduct conformity assessments for high-risk systems before market entry, prepare EU declarations of conformity, affix CE markings, maintain comprehensive documentation for a decade, and enable authorities to access logs and records. They must ensure clear identification and contact details on packaging and documentation and coordinate closely with distributors and national authorities. Importers and distributors have corresponding duties to verify conformity, retain documentation, and take corrective actions. Deployers bear responsibility for proper use, oversight, monitoring, and timely reporting of incidents, with post-market observations and information-sharing obligations. Before deployment, some actors must conduct fundamental rights impact assessments, and cross-border coordination among national authorities, the AI Office, and industry stakeholders is emphasized to ensure consistent application and enforcement. Confidentiality, language requirements, and accessibility in official EU languages are integrated across the regime.
A dedicated framework governs conformity assessment bodies and their designation. Member States designate notifying authorities to manage assessment procedures and oversee CABs, with independence and objectivity safeguarded. CABs must demonstrate competence, avoid conflicts of interest, and refrain from activities that could bias conformity outcomes. Notified bodies may rely on harmonised standards but may subcontract only with retained responsibility and public disclosure of subcontractors. They face strict documentation and reporting duties to support designation, notification, and ongoing monitoring, and each body is identified by a unique number. The Commission maintains a cross-regulatory list of notified bodies and their scopes, and can investigate competence failures. A sectoral group of notified bodies and cooperation with third-country CABs ensure coordinated conformity practices and international participation where appropriate.
The framework also governs standardization and market access through harmonised standards, common specifications where standards are unavailable or insufficient, and potential updates via delegated acts. Compliance paths include internal control and third-party conformity assessments, with translations, long-term declarations, and CE marking, including digital markings for digitally supplied systems. Certificates, declarations of conformity, and registration in EU databases are central, supported by market surveillance and potential temporary access in exceptional circumstances. The regime envisions ongoing evolution through delegated acts to reflect technological progress and refinements in risk-management and fundamental rights protections, ensuring that high-risk AI systems are safe, trustworthy, auditable, and aligned with rights while maintaining market access and accountability throughout their lifecycle.

IV.unit_title: TRANSPARENCY OBLIGATIONS FOR PROVIDERS AND DEPLOYERS OF CERTAIN AI SYSTEMS
IV.summary_1: This provision establishes a broad transparency framework for AI and synthetic media, requiring that users be informed when interacting with AI and that outputs that are AI-generated or manipulated be clearly disclosed, with lawful exceptions for crime-related uses. It mandates that those deploying emotion recognition or biometric categorisation inform individuals and process data in line with GDPR and related EU law, including exceptions for lawful criminal-offence purposes. Deepfake content and AI-generated text intended for public-interest information must also be disclosed, subject to similar exceptions and contextual limits for artistic works. Disclosures must occur at the first interaction in an accessible manner and do not override other transparency duties. The AI Office is tasked with promoting codes of practice, while the Commission may adopt implementing acts to standardize these obligations.
IV.summary_2: The set of provisions establishes a framework to promote transparency, accountability, and safeguards in the deployment of artificial intelligence and the handling of AI-generated content in the European Union. It requires that individuals know they are interacting with AI, unless the AI use is so obvious from the context that disclosure would be redundant, with narrow exceptions for certain legally authorized criminal-enforcement systems that are not publicly reportable and accompanied by appropriate safeguards. Outputs that generate synthetic audio, image, video, or text must be marked in a machine-readable way and clearly detectable as AI-generated or manipulated, with providers obligated to implement robust, interoperable technical solutions that balance effectiveness with cost and align with state-of-the-art standards; exemptions exist where the system merely assists editing or does not substantially alter input data, or where law authorizes detection or prosecution of offences. When emotion recognition or biometric categorisation systems are deployed, users must be informed about how the system operates and how personal data is processed in line with applicable EU data-protection laws, with exceptions for legally permitted criminal-enforcement uses safeguarded by rights protections and Union law compliance. For content that generates or manipulates media as deepfakes, disclosure that the content is artificially generated or manipulated is required, except where law authorizes detection or investigation of offences; however, artistic, creative, satirical, fictional, or analogous works may only require disclosure of the existence of such content in a manner that does not hinder display or enjoyment. In the case of AI-generated text published to inform the public on matters of public interest, disclosure is required unless the use is legally authorized to detect or prevent offences, or the content has undergone human review or editorial control with editorial responsibility resting in a natural or legal person. Across all these requirements, disclosures must be provided clearly and in a distinguishable manner at the first interaction, with accessibility considerations reflected, and these obligations do not replace or modify other existing transparency duties under Union or national law. The AI Office is tasked with promoting Union-level codes of practice to facilitate effective detection and labelling of artificially generated or manipulated content, and the Commission is empowered to adopt implementing acts to approve such codes or to establish common rules for their implementation through the examination procedure if needed.

1.summary_1: A framework is established for identifying and regulating general-purpose AI models that pose systemic risk, based on high-impact capabilities shown by technical indicators and benchmarks or as determined under a defined annex. A presumption of high impact can apply when cumulative training compute, measured in floating-point operations, exceeds 1025, with thresholds and benchmarks subject to delegated amendments as technology evolves. Providers must notify the competent authority within two weeks if their model meets systemic-risk criteria, and the authority may designate such models as systemic-risk even if notification is inadequate; designation can also occur ex officio or after a scientific panel alert, with opportunities for providers to submit arguments to avoid designation. The regime includes delegation to update Annex XIII, and requires the authority to publish and maintain an up-to-date public list of systemic-risk models.
1.summary_2:
The material outlines a regulatory framework for identifying and managing systemic risk posed by general-purpose AI models. It establishes that a model can be deemed systemic-risk based on high-impact capabilities identified by appropriate benchmarks or by formal designation through the Commission, including actions initiated by the Commission itself or following a qualified alert from a scientific panel. A key presumption of high impact exists when cumulative training computation surpasses a stated threshold, with the exact thresholds and supporting indicators subject to delegated acts to keep them aligned with technological progress. The framework provides that thresholds and criteria can be updated as technology evolves, ensuring the criteria reflect the state of the art.
Once a model meets the systemic-risk condition, the provider must notify the Commission without delay and no later than two weeks, supplying evidence that the requirement is met. The Commission may designate models as systemic-risk even if they were not notified, based on its assessment or a qualified alert. Providers may submit substantiated arguments explaining why a model that meets the criteria does not pose systemic risks due to its specific characteristics; if the Commission finds these arguments insufficient and the model's traits do not demonstrate a reduced risk, the designation stands. The designation can be triggered ex officio or after alerts, with the Commission empowered to revise the Annex XIII criteria via delegated acts. Providers may request reassessment with objective new reasons, and such reassessments can occur at six-month intervals after designation or a prior reassessment if the designation is maintained.
The Commission must maintain and publish an up-to-date list of systemic-risk models, while respecting intellectual property rights and confidential business information as required by applicable law.

2.unit_title: Obligations for providers of general-purpose AI models
2.summary_1:
A framework is established to enforce transparency, accountability, and regulatory oversight for providers of general-purpose AI models. It requires up-to-date technical documentation of training, testing, and evaluation, and obliges providers to provide this documentation to potential AI-system integrators to support compliance. They must adopt a copyright policy aligned with EU law, publicly summarize training data using a government template, and apply an openness exception for open-source models unless systemic risks exist. They are required to cooperate with the Commission and national authorities and may rely on codes of practice or harmonised standards; the Commission may adopt delegated acts to detail measurement methodologies and amend the supporting annexes, with all obtained information--including trade secrets--kept confidential.
For non-EU providers, the requirements extend to appointing a Union-based authorised representative with a written mandate who can verify compliance with the technical documentation and cooperate with the AI Office and national authorities. The mandate authorizes the representative to maintain and provide the documentation for ten years, supply information on request, and assist in regulatory actions, including when the model is integrated into systems placed on the market. The mandate can be terminated if obligations are breached, and open-source models retain an exemption unless systemic risks are present.
2.summary_2:
The material sets out a framework to ensure transparency, accountability, and regulatory oversight for general-purpose AI models. Providers must maintain up-to-date technical documentation of the model, including training and testing processes and evaluation results, and make this information available to the AI Office and national authorities on request, with content that aligns to specified annexes. They must also prepare and share documentation with AI system providers seeking to integrate the model, including elements from the annexes to aid assessment of capabilities, limitations, and compliance. In addition, providers must implement a policy to ensure copyright compliance, including reservations of rights under relevant directives, and publicly provide a detailed summary of training content using a template from the AI Office. Some obligations are waived for free/open-source GP AI models that publicly disclose parameters, architecture, and usage information, except where those models pose systemic risks. Providers are required to cooperate with the Commission and national competent authorities, may rely on approved codes of practice to demonstrate compliance until harmonised standards are published (with compliance to those standards giving a presumption of conformity; alternatives must be shown if standards or codes are not followed), and the Commission may adopt delegated acts to refine measurement methodologies and to amend the related annexes. Any information or documentation obtained under these provisions, including trade secrets, must be treated as confidential under the relevant confidentiality framework.
For third-country GP AI model providers, an EU-based authorised representative must be appointed by written mandate before market entry. The mandate authorizes the representative to verify that the provider has prepared the required technical documentation and satisfied the relevant obligations, to maintain a copy of that documentation for 10 years after market release (including contact details), and to provide the AI Office with information and documentation as needed to demonstrate compliance. The representative is obligated to cooperate with the AI Office and competent authorities in regulatory actions, including when the model is integrated into other systems. The mandate may authorize the AI Office or authorities to address the representative on compliance matters, either in addition to or instead of the provider. The representative may terminate the mandate if the provider fails to fulfill obligations, and must promptly inform the AI Office of the termination and its reasons. The requirement to appoint an authorised representative does not apply to GP AI models released under free/open-source licenses with publicly available parameters, architecture, and usage, unless those models present systemic risks.

3.unit_title: Obligations of providers of general-purpose AI models with systemic risk
3.summary_1: Providers of general-purpose AI models with systemic risk must conduct standardized evaluations (including adversarial testing), assess systemic risks at the Union level, and promptly report serious incidents to the AI Office and national authorities, while ensuring robust cybersecurity for the model and its infrastructure. They may demonstrate compliance through codes of practice or European harmonised standards, or other adequate means if they do not follow a code or standard. Any information obtained under these provisions, including trade secrets, remains confidential under the applicable confidentiality rules.
3.summary_2: The set of obligations requires providers of general-purpose AI models with systemic risk to go beyond standard duties by conducting state-of-the-art evaluations using standardized protocols and tools, including adversarial testing, to identify and mitigate systemic risks; to assess and address those risks across the Union from development to deployment; to promptly record and report serious incidents and proposed corrective measures to the AI Office and, where appropriate, national authorities; and to ensure robust cybersecurity for both the model and its underlying infrastructure. Providers may rely on codes of practice to demonstrate compliance until a harmonised standard is published, and where such standards apply they confer a presumption of conformity; if a provider does not follow an approved code of practice or harmonised standard, they must demonstrate alternative adequate means of compliance for assessment by the Commission. All information or documentation obtained under this Article, including trade secrets, must be treated as confidential in accordance with the confidentiality obligations set out in Article 78.

4.summary_1: Aimed at advancing EU-wide codes of practice to ensure proper application of the Regulation in a global context, the set envisions codes that cover the Regulation's relevant duties, including information updates, summaries of training data, identification and management of systemic risk, and proportionate risk documentation and procedures. It also establishes participation rules, reporting and KPI requirements, and ongoing monitoring, with a timeline that could lead the European Commission to issue implementing acts if the codes are not finalized by the deadlines.
4.summary_2:
The material outlines a framework for EU-wide codes of practice to support the proper application of the Regulation and to align with international approaches. These codes are meant to guide the implementation of obligations for providers of general-purpose AI models and to govern related risk management across the AI value chain. They must address core duties such as maintaining up-to-date information in light of market and technical changes, providing a sufficiently detailed summary of training data, identifying systemic risks at the Union level and their sources, and setting out proportionate measures, procedures, and documentation for assessing and managing those systemic risks.
Participation in developing the codes is broad and open, inviting GP-model providers, national authorities, civil society, industry, academia, downstream providers, and independent experts to contribute. The codes should clearly state their objectives and include commitments or measures with appropriate key performance indicators, ensuring the needs and interests of all interested parties at the Union level are considered.
Ongoing governance requires regular reporting by participants on implementation, outcomes, and KPI-based performance, with the AI Office and the Board monitoring progress and publishing assessments of the codes' adequacy. The Commission may adopt implementing acts to grant a code general Union validity following the prescribed procedure.
Regarding adherence and evolution, the AI Office may invite GP-model providers to adhere to the codes, while non-systemic-risk models would be limited to information obligations unless they opt into the full code. The Office will pursue review and adaptation of the codes in light of emerging standards and assist with standards assessment. A deadline of 2 May 2025 is set for ready codes, with potential invitations to participate; if a code is not finalized or deemed inadequate by 2 August 2025, the Commission may enact common rules through implementing acts to implement the obligations, including the topics outlined, under the required procedural framework.

V.summary_1: These provisions create a holistic EU regime for general-purpose AI models, combining a systemic-risk framework (identifying models by high-impact capabilities and training compute), mandatory notifications and possible designation as systemic-risk, and a public list maintained by the authorities; they also establish broad transparency and accountability duties for providers, including up-to-date technical documentation, data summaries, copyright compliance, cooperation with regulators, and a Union representative for non-EU entities with confidentiality protections. For models designated as systemic, providers must conduct standardized evaluations and adversarial testing, perform EU-level risk assessments, promptly report serious incidents, and maintain robust cybersecurity, with compliance demonstrated through codes of practice or harmonized standards. In parallel, the package pursues EU-wide codes of practice to harmonize global application, detailing information updates, training data summaries, risk management, monitoring, and KPI reporting, with the possibility of implementing acts if the codes are not finalized on schedule.
V.summary_2:
The material establishes an EU regulatory regime for systemic risk posed by general-purpose AI models, blending risk designation, transparency, and governance to ensure oversight across development and deployment. It envisions a dynamic framework that can adapt to technological progress, with criteria for high-impact capabilities or training compute used to designate models as systemic risk, and with the Commission empowered to update thresholds and indicators through delegated acts. Once a model meets the systemic-risk criteria, providers must promptly notify the Commission, while the Commission can designate models ex officio or based on alerts. Rebuttals from providers are possible but not determinative, and reassessments can occur at six-month intervals or upon maintained designation. The framework also requires maintaining and publishing an up-to-date list of systemic-risk models, subject to confidential information protections.
Transparency and accountability are central. Providers must maintain up-to-date technical documentation covering training, testing, and evaluation results, and make this information available to the AI Office and national authorities on request, including documentation to assist potential integrators in assessing capabilities, limitations, and compliance. They must implement copyright-compliance policies and publicly summarize training content using a standardized template. Free/open-source GP AI models with publicly disclosed parameters may be exempt from certain obligations unless they pose systemic risks. Cooperation with the Commission and national authorities is required, with reliance on approved codes of practice or harmonised standards providing presumptive conformity. Information obtained under these provisions remains confidential, and third-country providers require an EU-based authorised representative who verifies documentation, maintains records for a decade, and facilitates regulatory cooperation, with termination rights if obligations are unmet.
For models deemed to pose systemic risk, obligations extend beyond ordinary duties to include state-of-the-art evaluations using standardized protocols and tools (including adversarial testing), risk assessment and management across the Union from development to deployment, prompt incident reporting and corrective action proposals, and robust cybersecurity for both the model and its infrastructure. Compliance can be demonstrated through codes of practice or harmonised standards, with non-adherence requiring demonstrated alternative means of compliance, and confidentiality obligations applying to all related information.
The material also outlines a framework for EU-wide codes of practice to guide proper implementation and risk management across the GP AI value chain. These codes should address core duties such as maintaining current information, providing detailed training-data summaries, identifying systemic risks at the Union level, and setting proportionate measures and documentation for risk assessment and management. Participation is broad, inviting input from providers, authorities, civil society, industry, academia, downstream providers, and independent experts. Ongoing governance requires regular reporting on implementation and KPI-driven performance, with oversight from the AI Office and the Board, and potential adoption of codes with general Union validity via implementing acts. Adherence ideas distinguish between systemic-risk and non-systemic-risk models, with the latter limited to information obligations unless they opt into the full code. The AI Office will review and adapt codes as standards evolve, and there are concrete deadlines: codes should be ready by a specified date, with invitations to participate; if a code is not finalized or deemed inadequate by an later date, the Commission may enact common rules to implement the outlined obligations through implementing acts.

VI.summary_1: Collectively, this set creates a pan-EU framework for AI regulatory sandboxes and controlled real-world testing that balances innovation with risk management. It requires national AI sandboxes (with optional regional or cross-border setups) governed by a centralized regime and overseen by the AI Office and Board, with cross-border coordination and a Union-wide information platform to streamline guidance and conformity assessment. The Commission will adopt implementing acts detailing eligibility, operation, monitoring, and exit, ensuring open, non-discriminatory access for providers including SMEs and startups, timely decisions, and involvement of notified bodies and European Digital Innovation Hubs, with safeguards to protect fundamental rights and safety. Inside sandboxes, data can be processed for public-interest AI under strict, isolated conditions with logging, data deletion after the sandbox, and publication of technical details; law-enforcement processing requires a legal basis with the same safeguards. Outside sandboxes, high-risk AI systems may be tested under a Commission-approved real-world testing plan with a Union-wide ID, time limits, informed consent where possible, recall and incident reporting, and applicable product laws. SMEs receive priority access, tailored training, and reduced conformity assessment fees; microenterprises may use a simplified subset of quality management requirements under Commission guidelines while remaining subject to core obligations.
VI.summary_2:
These provisions establish an EU-wide framework for AI regulatory sandboxes and related measures to foster innovation in AI while ensuring protection of fundamental rights, data privacy, and market integrity. They outline how sandboxes are to be established, supervised, and accessed; how implementing acts will set detailed rules; how data may be used within sandboxes; how real-world testing outside sandboxes may occur for high-risk systems; consent requirements for such testing; and how SMEs, startups, and microenterprises are supported, including streamlined quality management approaches.
- Establishment, cooperation, and governance of sandboxes
  - Member States must set up at least one national AI regulatory sandbox by a specified date, with options to collaborate with other states or to run additional sandboxes regionally or locally. The Commission may offer technical support.
  - Participation in existing sandboxes is allowed if coverage is equivalent for the participating states. The European Data Protection Supervisor may establish a sandbox for Union institutions.
  - National authorities must allocate resources and cooperate with relevant actors in the AI ecosystem; coordination with other sandboxes and authorities is required to avoid gaps and overlap.
  - Sandboxes provide a controlled environment for development, testing, and validation of innovative AI before market deployment, potentially including real-world testing under supervision. They aim to improve legal certainty, share best practices, and accelerate access to the Union market, with cross-border cooperation and a centralized information interface.
- Implementing acts and operational rules
  - The Commission will adopt implementing acts detailing eligibility criteria, application and participation processes, monitoring, exiting, and termination, and the terms for participants (including sandbox plans and exit reports).
  - Access must be open and fair, with decisions issued within a defined period, and allowances for partnerships with deployers or third parties; national authorities have flexibility to tailor operations.
  - Access must be free for SMEs and startups, subject to narrowly defined exceptional costs; the sandboxes should support conformity assessments and codes of conduct.
  - The acts should promote collaboration with ecosystem actors, harmonize administrative requirements across the Union to avoid fragmentation, and enable mutual recognition of sandboxes across Member States and by the Data Protection Supervisor.
  - They should support development of testing, benchmarking, and assessment tools for AI dimensions linked to regulation and risk mitigation; prospective providers, especially SMEs/startups, should receive guidance on pre-deployment services and access to testing facilities and hubs.
- Data processing within sandboxes
  - Personal data lawfully collected for other purposes may be processed within sandboxes only to develop, train, and test AI systems when it serves a substantial public interest and the project falls within defined areas (e.g., public safety, health, environment, energy, transport, critical infrastructure, public administration), and only to the extent necessary.
  - Safeguards include monitoring mechanisms, isolated processing environments, restricted access, lawful data transfers under Union law, and no external sharing of data created during the sandbox. Processing should not determine outcomes about data subjects or undermine their rights; data must be deleted after the sandbox ends or retention periods expire.
  - A short project summary must be published, and technical documentation must describe the process and rationale. Special rules apply for processing related to criminal offenses, but these are still bound by Union data protection law.
  - If processing involves other authorities, they must be associated to the extent of their powers; liability remains with providers under relevant laws.
- Real-world testing outside sandboxes
  - High-risk AI systems may be tested in real-world conditions outside sandboxes, under a defined testing plan and subject to specific conditions and approvals, coexisting with harmonized product-testing laws.
  - Testing can occur before market placement and may involve providers alone or with deployers; it requires a national market surveillance plan, ethical reviews, and a set of procedural safeguards (including unique identifiers, data transfer controls, domicile/representation, and limitations on testing duration).
  - Testing must protect vulnerable groups, involve properly briefed deployers, and include binding agreements on roles and compliance. Results must be reversible or disregarded when appropriate; sponsors must enable withdrawal of participants at any time.
  - Market surveillance authorities have powers to oversee, request information, and conduct inspections; serious incidents must be reported with mitigation steps; testing can be suspended or terminated with recall procedures. Providers remain liable for damages.
- Informed consent for outside-sandbox testing
  - Freely given informed consent is required for participation in real-world testing outside sandboxes, with participants to receive concise and clear information about the testing, rights to refuse or withdraw, data handling, and how to contact the provider, plus a dated, documented record and a copy.
- Support for SMEs, startups, and procurement
  - Measures must favor SMEs and startups with priority access to sandboxes, alongside awareness, training, and guided participation in standardization processes.
  - Fees for conformity assessment should reflect SME size and capacity, with proportionate costs.
  - The AI Office should provide standardized templates, maintain a user-friendly information platform, run awareness campaigns, and promote harmonized procurement practices for public AI procurements to ensure consistency and predictability.
  - There should be mechanisms to harmonize procurement and ensure SMEs have opportunities to participate in standardization and related ecosystem activities.
- Simplified quality management for microenterprises
  - Microenterprises meeting a defined small-business criterion may implement a simplified portion of the quality management system that accompanies high-risk AI systems, provided they have no partner or linked enterprises as defined in the relevant recommendation.
  - The Commission will identify which elements can be simplified, balancing reduced burden with continued protection; this is not a blanket exemption from other obligations. Microenterprises remain subject to other high-risk AI requirements, including risk management, data governance, documentation, transparency, human oversight, and post-market monitoring.

1.summary_1:
A central governance framework is established to build and harmonize EU AI capabilities, enforcement, and cooperation. An AI Office acts as the hub for EU-level expertise and coordination, while a European Artificial Intelligence Board brings together national authorities with a structured secretariat, rules of procedure, and standing sub-groups for market surveillance and notification, supported by observers and collaboration with the advisory forum to ensure objective, cross-border consistency.
An advisory forum provides broad technical input from industry, startups, civil society, and academia, with permanent participation by key European standardization and security bodies, empowered to issue opinions and recommendations. A scientific panel of independent AI experts is created to support enforcement, advise on general-purpose AI, assist surveillance, and contribute to safeguards, with member states able to consult the panel for expert advice at regulated costs. The Commission will ensure timely access to testing and related support to maximize value across the Union.
1.summary_2:
The material outlines a comprehensive EU governance framework for artificial intelligence, establishing a centralized execution office, a cross-Member State board, a stakeholder forum, an independent scientific panel, and a mechanism for expert support to enforce and implement the Regulation across the Union. It is designed to ensure consistent application, effective enforcement, harmonised standards, and coordinated market surveillance, while fostering transparency, accountability, and collaboration with EU institutions and international partners.
Key components and their roles:
- The central AI Office is charged with building Union-wide AI expertise and capabilities and coordinating entrusted tasks across Member States to support implementation and enforcement of the Regulation.
- The European Artificial Intelligence Board brings one representative from each Member State (with the European Data Protection Supervisor as an observer and the AI Office as secretariat) and operates under rules of procedure adopted by two-thirds' vote. It may form standing and temporary sub-groups, including for market surveillance and notified bodies, and serves to advise and assist the Commission and Member States, coordinate national authorities, promote consistency and data sharing, and harmonise administrative practices. It also oversees standards development, guidance on general-purpose AI, governance, and the functioning of regulatory sandboxes, and it supports AI literacy and international cooperation.
- The Forum is a consultative body comprising a balanced mix of industry, startups, SMEs, civil society, and academia, with permanent participation by the Fundamental Rights Agency, ENISA, CEN, CENELEC, and ETSI. Members are appointed by the Commission for two-year terms (renewable), elect two co-chairs, meet regularly, may form sub-groups, and produce opinions, recommendations, and an annual public report.
- A Scientific Panel of independent experts, established by an implementing act, supports enforcement activities. Its members are independent of AI providers, diverse, and obliged to publish conflict-of-interest declarations. The panel advises on enforcement, classification of general-purpose AI models, tools and benchmarks, alerting the AI Office to systemic risks, and assisting cross-border market surveillance; it may also assist the AI Office in the Union safeguard procedure and related tasks.
- Member States may request expert support from the scientific panel to aid enforcement, with a fee framework set by implementing acts. The Commission ensures timely access to experts and coordinates deployment of both Union testing structures and the expert pool to maximize value and efficiency.
Overall, the configuration aims to maintain objectivity and impartiality, designate clear points of contact and authority, promote consistent interpretation and application of the Regulation, and enable scalable enforcement and oversight mechanisms across the EU.

2.summary_1: Member States must designate independent national competent authorities with adequate resources, including at least one notifying authority and one market surveillance authority, and appoint a single point of contact whose identity is shared with the Commission and publicly listed by a specified deadline.
