169.text: Compliance with the obligations on providers of general-purpose AI models imposed under this Regulation should be enforceable, inter alia, by means of fines. To that end, appropriate levels of fines should also be laid down for infringement of those obligations, including the failure to comply with measures requested by the Commission in accordance with this Regulation, subject to appropriate limitation periods in accordance with the principle of proportionality. All decisions taken by the Commission under this Regulation are subject to review by the Court of Justice of the European Union in accordance with the TFEU, including the unlimited jurisdiction of the Court of Justice with regard to penalties pursuant to Article 261 TFEU.

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

170.text: Union and national law already provide effective remedies to natural and legal persons whose rights and freedoms are adversely affected by the use of AI systems. Without prejudice to those remedies, any natural or legal person that has grounds to consider that there has been an infringement of this Regulation should be entitled to lodge a complaint to the relevant market surveillance authority.

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

171.text: Affected persons should have the right to obtain an explanation where a deployer's decision is based mainly upon the output from certain high-risk AI systems that fall within the scope of this Regulation and where that decision produces legal effects or similarly significantly affects those persons in a way that they consider to have an adverse impact on their health, safety or fundamental rights. That explanation should be clear and meaningful and should provide a basis on which the affected persons are able to exercise their rights. The right to obtain an explanation should not apply to the use of AI systems for which exceptions or restrictions follow from Union or national law and should apply only to the extent this right is not already provided for under Union law.

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

172.text: Persons acting as whistleblowers on the infringements of this Regulation should be protected under the Union law. Directive (EU) 2019/1937 of the European Parliament and of the Council(note: 54) should therefore apply to the reporting of infringements of this Regulation and the protection of persons reporting such infringements.

notes.54: Directive (EU) 2019/1937 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 23 October 2019 on the protection of persons who report breaches of Union law (OJ L 305, 26.11.2019, p. 17).

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

173.text: In order to ensure that the regulatory framework can be adapted where necessary, the power to adopt acts in accordance with Article 290 TFEU should be delegated to the Commission to amend the conditions under which an AI system is not to be considered to be high-risk, the list of high-risk AI systems, the provisions regarding technical documentation, the content of the EU declaration of conformity the provisions regarding the conformity assessment procedures, the provisions establishing the high-risk AI systems to which the conformity assessment procedure based on assessment of the quality management system and assessment of the technical documentation should apply, the threshold, benchmarks and indicators, including by supplementing those benchmarks and indicators, in the rules for the classification of general-purpose AI models with systemic risk, the criteria for the designation of general-purpose AI models with systemic risk, the technical documentation for providers of general-purpose AI models and the transparency information for providers of general-purpose AI models. It is of particular importance that the Commission carry out appropriate consultations during its preparatory work, including at expert level, and that those consultations be conducted in accordance with the principles laid down in the Interinstitutional Agreement of 13 April 2016 on Better Law-Making(note: 55). In particular, to ensure equal participation in the preparation of delegated acts, the European Parliament and the Council receive all documents at the same time as Member States' experts, and their experts systematically have access to meetings of Commission expert groups dealing with the preparation of delegated acts.

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

174.text: Given the rapid technological developments and the technical expertise required to effectively apply this Regulation, the Commission should evaluate and review this Regulation by 2 August 2029 and every four years thereafter and report to the European Parliament and the Council. In addition, taking into account the implications for the scope of this Regulation, the Commission should carry out an assessment of the need to amend the list of high-risk AI systems and the list of prohibited practices once a year. Moreover, by 2 August 2028 and every four years thereafter, the Commission should evaluate and report to the European Parliament and to the Council on the need to amend the list of high-risk areas headings in the annex to this Regulation, the AI systems within the scope of the transparency obligations, the effectiveness of the supervision and governance system and the progress on the development of standardisation deliverables on energy efficient development of general-purpose AI models, including the need for further measures or actions. Finally, by 2 August 2028 and every three years thereafter, the Commission should evaluate the impact and effectiveness of voluntary codes of conduct to foster the application of the requirements provided for high-risk AI systems in the case of AI systems other than high-risk AI systems and possibly other additional requirements for such AI systems.

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

175.text: In order to ensure uniform conditions for the implementation of this Regulation, implementing powers should be conferred on the Commission. Those powers should be exercised in accordance with Regulation (EU) No 182/2011 of the European Parliament and of the Council(note: 56).

notes.56: Regulation (EU) No 182/2011 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 16 February 2011 laying down the rules and general principles concerning mechanisms for control by Member States of the Commission's exercise of implementing powers (OJ L 55, 28.2.2011, p. 13).

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

176.text: Since the objective of this Regulation, namely to improve the functioning of the internal market and to promote the uptake of human centric and trustworthy AI, while ensuring a high level of protection of health, safety, fundamental rights enshrined in the Charter, including democracy, the rule of law and environmental protection against harmful effects of AI systems in the Union and supporting innovation, cannot be sufficiently achieved by the Member States and can rather, by reason of the scale or effects of the action, be better achieved at Union level, the Union may adopt measures in accordance with the principle of subsidiarity as set out in Article 5 TEU. In accordance with the principle of proportionality as set out in that Article, this Regulation does not go beyond what is necessary in order to achieve that objective.

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

177.text: In order to ensure legal certainty, ensure an appropriate adaptation period for operators and avoid disruption to the market, including by ensuring continuity of the use of AI systems, it is appropriate that this Regulation applies to the high-risk AI systems that have been placed on the market or put into service before the general date of application thereof, only if, from that date, those systems are subject to significant changes in their design or intended purpose. It is appropriate to clarify that, in this respect, the concept of significant change should be understood as equivalent in substance to the notion of substantial modification, which is used with regard only to high-risk AI systems pursuant to this Regulation. On an exceptional basis and in light of public accountability, operators of AI systems which are components of the large-scale IT systems established by the legal acts listed in an annex to this Regulation and operators of high-risk AI systems that are intended to be used by public authorities should, respectively, take the necessary steps to comply with the requirements of this Regulation by end of 2030 and by 2 August 2030.

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

178.text: Providers of high-risk AI systems are encouraged to start to comply, on a voluntary basis, with the relevant obligations of this Regulation already during the transitional period.

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

179.text: This Regulation should apply from 2 August 2026. However, taking into account the unacceptable risk associated with the use of AI in certain ways, the prohibitions as well as the general provisions of this Regulation should already apply from 2 February 2025. While the full effect of those prohibitions follows with the establishment of the governance and enforcement of this Regulation, anticipating the application of the prohibitions is important to take account of unacceptable risks and to have an effect on other procedures, such as in civil law. Moreover, the infrastructure related to the governance and the conformity assessment system should be operational before 2 August 2026, therefore the provisions on notified bodies and governance structure should apply from 2 August 2025. Given the rapid pace of technological advancements and adoption of general-purpose AI models, obligations for providers of general-purpose AI models should apply from 2 August 2025. Codes of practice should be ready by 2 May 2025 in view of enabling providers to demonstrate compliance on time. The AI Office should ensure that classification rules and procedures are up to date in light of technological developments. In addition, Member States should lay down and notify to the Commission the rules on penalties, including administrative fines, and ensure that they are properly and effectively implemented by the date of application of this Regulation. Therefore the provisions on penalties should apply from 2 August 2025.

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

180.text: The European Data Protection Supervisor and the European Data Protection Board were consulted in accordance with Article 42(1) and (2) of Regulation (EU) 2018/1725 and delivered their joint opinion on 18 June 2021,

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council
(EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

1.summary_1: It aims to improve the internal market and promote the uptake of human-centric, trustworthy AI while ensuring high protection of health, safety and fundamental rights, including democracy, rule of law, and environmental protection, against AI harms. It establishes harmonised rules for placing AI on the market, putting it into service, and use; prohibitions on certain AI practices; high-risk AI system requirements and operator obligations; transparency rules; governance and enforcement measures; and rules for general-purpose AI models. It also includes measures to support innovation, with a focus on SMEs and start-ups.

need_ref.value: Charter of Fundamental Rights of the European Union

1.summary_2:
The Regulation aims to improve the functioning of the internal market and promote the uptake of human-centric, trustworthy AI while ensuring a high level of protection for health, safety, fundamental rights (including democracy, the rule of law) and the environment, and to support innovation.
It establishes harmonised rules for placing on the market, putting into service, and use of AI systems in the Union; it also bans certain AI practices and imposes specific requirements for high-risk AI systems, with corresponding obligations on their operators.
It provides harmonised transparency rules for certain AI systems and sets harmonised rules for the on-market placement of general-purpose AI models; it also lays out rules on market monitoring, market surveillance, governance, and enforcement.
Finally, it includes measures to support innovation, with particular emphasis on SMEs and start-ups.

2.text:
1. This Regulation applies to:
(a) providers placing on the market or putting into service AI systems or placing on the market general-purpose AI models in the Union, irrespective of whether those providers are established or located within the Union or in a third country;
(b) deployers of AI systems that have their place of establishment or are located within the Union;
(c) providers and deployers of AI systems that have their place of establishment or are located in a third country, where the output produced by the AI system is used in the Union;
(d) importers and distributors of AI systems;
(e) product manufacturers placing on the market or putting into service an AI system together with their product and under their own name or trademark;
(f) authorised representatives of providers, which are not established in the Union;
(g) affected persons that are located in the Union.

2. For AI systems classified as high-risk AI systems in accordance with Article 6(1) related to products covered by the Union harmonisation legislation listed in Section B of Annex I, only Article 6(1), Articles 102 to 109 and Article 112 apply. Article 57 applies only in so far as the requirements for high-risk AI systems under this Regulation have been integrated in that Union harmonisation legislation.

3. This Regulation does not apply to areas outside the scope of Union law, and shall not, in any event, affect the competences of the Member States concerning national security, regardless of the type of entity entrusted by the Member States with carrying out tasks in relation to those competences.
This Regulation does not apply to AI systems where and in so far they are placed on the market, put into service, or used with or without modification exclusively for military, defence or national security purposes, regardless of the type of entity carrying out those activities.
This Regulation does not apply to AI systems which are not placed on the market or put into service in the Union, where the output is used in the Union exclusively for military, defence or national security purposes, regardless of the type of entity carrying out those activities.

4. This Regulation applies neither to public authorities in a third country nor to international organisations falling within the scope of this Regulation pursuant to paragraph 1, where those authorities or organisations use AI systems in the framework of international cooperation or agreements for law enforcement and judicial cooperation with the Union or with one or more Member States, provided that such a third country or international organisation provides adequate safeguards with respect to the protection of fundamental rights and freedoms of individuals.

5. This Regulation shall not affect the application of the provisions on the liability of providers of intermediary services as set out in Chapter II of Regulation (EU) 2022/2065.

6. This Regulation does not apply to AI systems or AI models, including their output, specifically developed and put into service for the sole purpose of scientific research and development.

7. Union law on the protection of personal data, privacy and the confidentiality of communications applies to personal data processed in connection with the rights and obligations laid down in this Regulation. This Regulation shall not affect Regulation (EU) 2016/679 or (EU) 2018/1725, or Directive 2002/58/EC or (EU) 2016/680, without prejudice to Article 10(5) and Article 59 of this Regulation.

8. This Regulation does not apply to any research, testing or development activity regarding AI systems or AI models prior to their being placed on the market or put into service. Such activities shall be conducted in accordance with applicable Union law. Testing in real world conditions shall not be covered by that exclusion.

9. This Regulation is without prejudice to the rules laid down by other Union legal acts related to consumer protection and product safety.

10. This Regulation does not apply to obligations of deployers who are natural persons using AI systems in the course of a purely personal non-professional activity.

11. This Regulation does not preclude the Union or Member States from maintaining or introducing laws, regulations or administrative provisions which are more favourable to workers in terms of protecting their rights in respect of the use of AI systems by employers, or from encouraging or allowing the application of collective agreements which are more favourable to workers.

12. This Regulation does not apply to AI systems released under free and open-source licences, unless they are placed on the market or put into service as high-risk AI systems or as an AI system that falls under Article 5 or 50.

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

2.summary_1: Scope covers providers placing on the market or deploying AI systems and general-purpose AI models in the EU, regardless of location, along with deployers, importers, distributors, authorised representatives, product manufacturers, and affected persons in the Union. It clarifies that high-risk AI systems tied to products under Union harmonisation legislation fall under Article 6(1) and related Articles 102–109 and 112, with Article 57 applying only to integration of those high-risk requirements; and it excludes military/national security, pre-market research activity, and areas outside Union law. It also preserves EU data-protection rules, allows more favorable national worker protections, and excludes open-source licences unless they become high-risk or fall under Article 5 or 50.
2.summary_2:
The Regulation casts a broad, cross-border scope. It applies to providers that place on the market or put into service AI systems or general-purpose AI models in the Union, regardless of where the provider is established; to deployers located in the Union; to providers or deployers outside the Union when the output is used in the Union; to importers and distributors; to product manufacturers that place an AI system together with their product under their own name or trademark; to authorized representatives for non-EU providers; and to individuals in the Union who are affected. This creates a comprehensive, extra-territorial reach centered on Union usage and positioning.
Where AI systems are classified as high-risk under Union product-harmonisation rules, only the core high-risk provisions and related enforcement rules apply, with the regulatory sandbox provision limited to the extent those harmonisation rules incorporate it. In practice, this means that certain high-risk requirements may be integrated through sector-specific legislation, rather than the Regulation's full set of provisions.
Several important exclusions and special regimes shape the scope. The Regulation does not apply to areas outside Union law or to military, defence, or national security purposes, whether or not the activity involves the state or private actors. It does not cover AI systems that are not placed on the market or put into service in the Union, nor outputs used exclusively for military purposes. Public authorities of a third country or international organisations are exempt when used in international cooperation for law enforcement or judicial cooperation, provided adequate safeguards for fundamental rights exist. It does not alter intermediary-liability rules under another Regulation, and it excludes purely scientific research and development activities, though real-world testing is not excluded.
The Regulation also interacts with other EU laws and regimes. It does not override consumer-protection and general product-safety rules, and it does not apply to purely personal, non-professional use by natural persons. It leaves room for Member States or the Union to maintain or adopt more favorable protections for workers, and it does not apply to open-source AI released under licenses unless the OSS is placed on the market or put into service as high-risk or falls under the specified provisions. Personal-data protection remains governed by EU data-protection rules.

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

3.summary_1: Defines the core terms used throughout the Regulation, including provider, deployer, importer, distributor, and operator, and the key concepts of placing on the market, making available, putting into service, and intended use. It also sets out definitions for data protection and biometric data, safety components, conformity assessment, and roles such as notified bodies and the AI Office, along with mechanisms for real-world testing and post-market monitoring. These definitions establish the vocabulary that underpins the Regulation’s high-level risk and governance framework.
3.summary_2:
The definitions establish the core concepts and roles that govern the lifecycle of AI systems. An AI system is a machine-based, partially autonomous system that may adapt after deployment and, based on input, generates outputs (predictions, content, recommendations, or decisions) that can affect real or virtual environments. Risk combines both the probability of harm and its severity. The regulation identifies actors: providers who develop or place AI systems or general-purpose AI models on the market; deployers who use AI systems under authority (excluding personal, non-professional use); authorised representatives in the Union acting for providers; importers and distributors; and operators who comprise any of these roles. Placing on the market, making available, and putting into service refer to first-time market access, commercial supply, and initial use within the Union, all tied to the intended purpose as described in the provider's materials, instructions, and technical documentation. It also covers foreseeable misuse, safety components, instructions for use, recall or withdrawal measures, overall performance, and the provider's post-market monitoring obligations.
The framework for conformity and oversight is defined through conformity assessment and related bodies. A conformity assessment demonstrates that high-risk AI systems meet the specified requirements, to be carried out by conformity assessment bodies, including notified bodies designated under this Regulation. Substantial modification--changes after market introduction that were not foreseen in the initial assessment and that affect compliance or purpose--triggers reassessment. CE marking indicates conformity with the applicable requirements and harmonised Union harmonisation laws. Post-market monitoring systems, market surveillance authorities, and the use of harmonised standards or common specifications guide ongoing conformity, while post-market and surveillance activities ensure continued compliance and safety.
The definitions cover data, biometric processing, and related privacy considerations. Training data, validation data, and testing data define the different stages of AI model development and evaluation. Personal data and non-personal data are distinguished, with biometric data, biometric identification, and biometric verification specifying how human characteristics may be processed. Special categories of personal data, emotion recognition, biometric categorisation, and remote biometric identification (including real-time variants) describe more sensitive uses. "Input data" anchors what the AI system uses to produce outputs, while "informed consent" may be required for real-world testing. The terms "deep fake" and profiling address potential manipulation and automated classification, and the concept of publicly accessible space frames the context in which some biometric or surveillance functions may operate.
Lastly, governance, risk context, and related concepts shape the regulatory environment. The AI Office (the Commission's governance function) and national competent authorities (notifying or market surveillance authorities) oversee implementation, with exceptions for Union institutions. Serious incidents define events causing death, serious health harm, critical infrastructure disruption, rights infringements, or substantial environmental/property harm. The regulation also introduces concepts around high-impact capabilities, systemic risk, general-purpose AI models and systems, downstream providers (those that integrate or deploy models in downstream use), and technical considerations such as floating-point operations. Collectively, these definitions create the vocabulary for roles, safety, compliance, data handling, testing, and risk management within the EU's AI framework.

4.text: Providers and deployers of AI systems shall take measures to ensure, to their best extent, a sufficient level of AI literacy of their staff and other persons dealing with the operation and use of AI systems on their behalf, taking into account their technical knowledge, experience, education and training and the context the AI systems are to be used in, and considering the persons or groups of persons on whom the AI systems are to be used.

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

4.summary_1: Requires providers and deployers of AI systems to take measures ensuring a sufficient level of AI literacy among staff and others involved in operation and use of the systems. The obligation is to tailor this literacy to individuals’ technical knowledge, experience, education, training, and the specific context and user groups for whom the AI is intended.
4.summary_2: Providers and deployers must implement measures to ensure a sufficient level of AI literacy among staff and others who operate or use their AI systems, with measures tailored to individuals' knowledge, experience, education, and training, the context of use, and the characteristics of the users.

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

5.summary_1: Prohibits AI practices that manipulate or deceive individuals, exploit vulnerabilities, or use social scoring, criminal-risk profiling, and biometric inference to deduce sensitive attributes, including the untargeted scraping of facial images to build recognition databases and the inference of emotions in workplace or educational settings (except for medical or safety reasons). It restricts the use of real-time remote biometric identification in publicly accessible spaces for law enforcement, requiring prior authorisation by a judicial or independent authority, a rights impact assessment, narrowly tailored scope, and registration in an EU database, with urgent cases allowed under time-limited exceptions and mandatory data deletion if not authorised. It also mandates national rulemaking and reporting obligations, including notification to market surveillance and data protection authorities and annual Commission reporting, without altering other Union-law prohibitions.
5.summary_2:
The provisions prohibit a broad set of AI practices that threaten autonomy, safety, or nondiscrimination. They ban subliminal or deceptive techniques intended to distort a person's or a group's behavior and cause significant harm, as well as any AI that exploits vulnerabilities due to age, disability, or socio-economic status to distort behavior and harm. They also prohibit evaluating or classifying individuals over time based on social behavior or inferred characteristics in a way that leads to detrimental or unjustified treatment, and exclude AI that performs risk assessments solely from profiling or personality traits unless used to support human, fact-based assessments of criminal activity. In addition, the use of AI to create or expand facial recognition databases from untargeted scraping, to infer emotions in work or education settings (except for medical or safety reasons), and to biometric categorisation that infers sensitive attributes is prohibited, with limited carve-outs for law enforcement labelling or filtering of lawfully acquired biometric data.
The use of real-time remote biometric identification in publicly accessible spaces for law enforcement is generally prohibited, except for three narrowly defined purposes: targeted searches for victims of abduction/trafficking/sexual exploitation or missing persons; prevention of a specific, substantial and imminent threat to life or safety or a current/foreseeable terrorist threat; and locating or identifying a person suspected of committing a crime punishable by at least four years' imprisonment, with the broader prohibition remaining subject to data-protection safeguards. The prohibition explicitly notes that it does not override GDPR provisions on biometric data processing for non-law-enforcement purposes.
For the purposes of the real-time biometric identification provisions, any use in law enforcement must be limited to confirming the identity of the specifically targeted individual and must weigh: (a) the seriousness, probability, and scale of harm if the system is not used, and (b) the rights and freedoms of all involved, including the seriousness and scale of those consequences. Uses must comply with safeguards and national law, particularly temporal, geographic, and personal limits, and are subject to prior assessment and registration requirements.
All deployments must be notified to the national market surveillance authority and the national data protection authority, with the notification containing at least the information specified by the implementing rules and excluding sensitive operational data. Member States may adopt national rules for requests, issuance, exercise, supervision, and reporting of authorisations, and must notify the Commission within 30 days. They may implement more restrictive laws. Annual reporting on such uses is required from national authorities to the Commission, which will provide reporting templates; the Commission will publish aggregated annual reports, excluding sensitive operational data.
The regulation provides that the annual Commission reporting will summarize aggregated data from Member States and that nothing in these provisions affects prohibitions under other Union law.

6.unit_title: Classification rules for high-risk AI systems
6.text:
1. Irrespective of whether an AI system is placed on the market or put into service independently of the products referred to in points (a) and (b), that AI system shall be considered to be high-risk where both of the following conditions are fulfilled:
(a) the AI system is intended to be used as a safety component of a product, or the AI system is itself a product, covered by the Union harmonisation legislation listed in Annex I;
(b) the product whose safety component pursuant to point (a) is the AI system, or the AI system itself as a product, is required to undergo a third-party conformity assessment, with a view to the placing on the market or the putting into service of that product pursuant to the Union harmonisation legislation listed in Annex I.

2. In addition to the high-risk AI systems referred to in paragraph 1, AI systems referred to in Annex III shall be considered to be high-risk.

3. By derogation from paragraph 2, an AI system referred to in Annex III shall not be considered to be high-risk where it does not pose a significant risk of harm to the health, safety or fundamental rights of natural persons, including by not materially influencing the outcome of decision making.
The first subparagraph shall apply where any of the following conditions is fulfilled:
(a) the AI system is intended to perform a narrow procedural task;
(b) the AI system is intended to improve the result of a previously completed human activity;
(c) the AI system is intended to detect decision-making patterns or deviations from prior decision-making patterns and is not meant to replace or influence the previously completed human assessment, without proper human review; or
(d) the AI system is intended to perform a preparatory task to an assessment relevant for the purposes of the use cases listed in Annex III.
Notwithstanding the first subparagraph, an AI system referred to in Annex III shall always be considered to be high-risk where the AI system performs profiling of natural persons.

4. A provider who considers that an AI system referred to in Annex III is not high-risk shall document its assessment before that system is placed on the market or put into service. Such provider shall be subject to the registration obligation set out in Article 49(2). Upon request of national competent authorities, the provider shall provide the documentation of the assessment.

5. The Commission shall, after consulting the European Artificial Intelligence Board (the 'Board'), and no later than 2 February 2026, provide guidelines specifying the practical implementation of this Article in line with Article 96 together with a comprehensive list of practical examples of use cases of AI systems that are high-risk and not high-risk.

6. The Commission is empowered to adopt delegated acts in accordance with Article 97 in order to amend paragraph 3, second subparagraph, of this Article by adding new conditions to those laid down therein, or by modifying them, where there is concrete and reliable evidence of the existence of AI systems that fall under the scope of Annex III, but do not pose a significant risk of harm to the health, safety or fundamental rights of natural persons.

7. The Commission shall adopt delegated acts in accordance with Article 97 in order to amend paragraph 3, second subparagraph, of this Article by deleting any of the conditions laid down therein, where there is concrete and reliable evidence that this is necessary to maintain the level of protection of health, safety and fundamental rights provided for by this Regulation.

8. Any amendment to the conditions laid down in paragraph 3, second subparagraph, adopted in accordance with paragraphs 6 and 7 of this Article shall not decrease the overall level of protection of health, safety and fundamental rights provided for by this Regulation and shall ensure consistency with the delegated acts adopted pursuant to Article 7(1), and take account of market and technological developments.

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

6.summary_1: It classifies AI systems as high-risk if they function as a safety component or product covered by Annex I and require a third-party conformity assessment. It adds Annex III systems as high-risk, with derogations for certain non-significant-risk uses (procedural tasks, improving prior results, detecting decision patterns without replacing human review, or preparatory tasks), but profiling natural persons remains always high-risk. It requires providers to document assessments for Annex III, allows the Commission to issue guidelines by 2 February 2026 and to amend the rules via delegated acts under Articles 96 and 97, while ensuring protection is not reduced.
6.summary_2:
A high-risk classification applies when an AI system is either used as a safety component of a product or is itself a product covered by the Union harmonisation legislation in Annex I, and that product must undergo a third-party conformity assessment before it can be placed on the market or put into service. This ties the risk status directly to the product's safety regime and conformity requirements.
Beyond these criteria, AI systems listed in Annex III are also deemed high-risk. However, there is a derogation: an Annex III system may not be high-risk if it does not pose a significant risk to health, safety, or fundamental rights, including cases where it does not materially influence decision-making outcomes. The derogation applies when the system undertakes a narrow procedural task, enhances the result of a completed human activity, detects decision-making patterns without replacing human assessment (with proper human review), or performs a preparatory task for an assessment relevant to Annex III use cases. Notwithstanding, profiling of natural persons remains always high-risk.
If a provider regards an Annex III AI system as not high-risk, they must document the assessment before placing the system on the market or into service and are subject to the registration obligation; the documentation must be produced to national authorities upon request.
The Commission will issue practical guidelines by 2 February 2026, after consulting the European AI Board, including a comprehensive set of examples distinguishing high-risk and non-high-risk use cases. The Commission may adopt delegated acts to add or modify the conditions that determine the derogation or to delete them, provided concrete, reliable evidence supports such changes and that any amendments do not weaken overall protections, remain consistent with other delegated acts, and reflect market and technological developments.

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

7.summary_1: Delegated acts may add or modify high-risk AI use-cases in Annex III when they operate in areas listed there and pose health and safety risks or adverse effects on fundamental rights at a level equal to or greater than existing high-risk systems. The decision to add uses a set of criteria including intended purpose, extent of use, data processed (including special categories), autonomy and human override, prior harms or concerns, potential magnitude of harm, opt-out feasibility, vulnerability or power imbalances, reversibility, overall benefits, and alignment with existing EU law on redress. The Commission may also remove high-risk AI systems from Annex III if they no longer pose significant risks and such removal does not reduce overall protection of health, safety, and fundamental rights under Union law.
7.summary_2:
The Commission may, through delegated acts, amend Annex III to add or modify use-cases of high-risk AI systems when two conditions are met: the AI is intended for use in areas listed in Annex III, and the AI poses health and safety risks or adverse effects on fundamental rights at a level equal to or greater than the risks posed by high-risk systems already in Annex III.
To assess the risk condition, the Commission must consider a non-exhaustive set of criteria, including the AI's intended purpose; how widely it is used or expected to be used; the nature and quantity of data processed, particularly special categories of personal data; the system's autonomy and whether a human can override it; prior harms or rights concerns demonstrated to authorities; the potential magnitude and distribution of harm; the dependence of affected individuals on the outcome; any power imbalances or vulnerabilities of those harmed; how easily the outcome can be corrected or reversed; the magnitude of potential benefit for individuals, groups, or society; and whether existing Union law provides effective redress (excluding damages) and adequate risk prevention measures.
The Commission may also amend Annex III to remove high-risk designation if the high-risk AI system no longer poses significant risks per the criteria, and its deletion does not reduce overall protection of health, safety, and fundamental rights under Union law. This framework ensures both the expansion and contraction of high-risk coverage are grounded in a structured, rights-protective assessment.

8.text:
1. High-risk AI systems shall comply with the requirements laid down in this Section, taking into account their intended purpose as well as the generally acknowledged state of the art on AI and AI-related technologies. The risk management system referred to in Article 9 shall be taken into account when ensuring compliance with those requirements.

2. Where a product contains an AI system, to which the requirements of this Regulation as well as requirements of the Union harmonisation legislation listed in Section A of Annex I apply, providers shall be responsible for ensuring that their product is fully compliant with all applicable requirements under applicable Union harmonisation legislation. In ensuring the compliance of high-risk AI systems referred to in paragraph 1 with the requirements set out in this Section, and in order to ensure consistency, avoid duplication and minimise additional burdens, providers shall have a choice of integrating, as appropriate, the necessary testing and reporting processes, information and documentation they provide with regard to their product into documentation and procedures that already exist and are required under the Union harmonisation legislation listed in Section A of Annex I.

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

8.summary_1: High-risk AI systems must comply with the section's requirements, taking into account their intended purpose and the generally acknowledged state of the art, with the risk management system from Article 9 considered in ensuring compliance. If a product contains an AI system subject to this Regulation and the applicable Union harmonisation legislation listed in Annex I Section A, providers must ensure full compliance and may integrate testing and reporting processes into existing documentation and procedures to avoid duplication and reduce burdens.
8.summary_2:
High-risk AI systems must comply with the Section's requirements, taking into account their intended purpose and the generally acknowledged state of the art in AI and AI-related technologies. The risk management system referenced in Article 9 must be considered when ensuring compliance.
If a product contains an AI system that is subject to this Regulation and the Union harmonisation legislation listed in Section A of Annex I, providers are responsible for ensuring full compliance with all applicable harmonisation requirements. To promote consistency and minimize duplication and burden, providers may integrate the necessary testing, reporting processes, information, and documentation for the AI product into documentation and procedures that are already required under Section A of Annex I.

9.text:
1. A risk management system shall be established, implemented, documented and maintained in relation to high-risk AI systems.

2. The risk management system shall be understood as a continuous iterative process planned and run throughout the entire lifecycle of a high-risk AI system, requiring regular systematic review and updating. It shall comprise the following steps:
(a) the identification and analysis of the known and the reasonably foreseeable risks that the high-risk AI system can pose to health, safety or fundamental rights when the high-risk AI system is used in accordance with its intended purpose;
(b) the estimation and evaluation of the risks that may emerge when the high-risk AI system is used in accordance with its intended purpose, and under conditions of reasonably foreseeable misuse;
(c) the evaluation of other risks possibly arising, based on the analysis of data gathered from the post-market monitoring system referred to in Article 72;
(d) the adoption of appropriate and targeted risk management measures designed to address the risks identified pursuant to point (a).

3. The risks referred to in this Article shall concern only those which may be reasonably mitigated or eliminated through the development or design of the high-risk AI system, or the provision of adequate technical information.

4. The risk management measures referred to in paragraph 2, point (d), shall give due consideration to the effects and possible interaction resulting from the combined application of the requirements set out in this Section, with a view to minimising risks more effectively while achieving an appropriate balance in implementing the measures to fulfil those requirements.

5. The risk management measures referred to in paragraph 2, point (d), shall be such that the relevant residual risk associated with each hazard, as well as the overall residual risk of the high-risk AI systems is judged to be acceptable.
In identifying the most appropriate risk management measures, the following shall be ensured:
(a) elimination or reduction of risks identified and evaluated pursuant to paragraph 2 in as far as technically feasible through adequate design and development of the high-risk AI system;
(b) where appropriate, implementation of adequate mitigation and control measures addressing risks that cannot be eliminated;
(c) provision of information required pursuant to Article 13 and, where appropriate, training to deployers.
With a view to eliminating or reducing risks related to the use of the high-risk AI system, due consideration shall be given to the technical knowledge, experience, education, the training to be expected by the deployer, and the presumable context in which the system is intended to be used.

6. High-risk AI systems shall be tested for the purpose of identifying the most appropriate and targeted risk management measures. Testing shall ensure that high-risk AI systems perform consistently for their intended purpose and that they are in compliance with the requirements set out in this Section.

7. Testing procedures may include testing in real-world conditions in accordance with Article 60.

8. The testing of high-risk AI systems shall be performed, as appropriate, at any time throughout the development process, and, in any event, prior to their being placed on the market or put into service. Testing shall be carried out against prior defined metrics and probabilistic thresholds that are appropriate to the intended purpose of the high-risk AI system.

9. When implementing the risk management system as provided for in paragraphs 1 to 7, providers shall give consideration to whether in view of its intended purpose the high-risk AI system is likely to have an adverse impact on persons under the age of 18 and, as appropriate, other vulnerable groups.

10. For providers of high-risk AI systems that are subject to requirements regarding internal risk management processes under other relevant provisions of Union law, the aspects provided in paragraphs 1 to 9 may be part of, or combined with, the risk management procedures established pursuant to that law.

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

9.summary_1: High-risk AI systems must have a continuous, lifecycle-spanning risk management system that identifies, analyzes, and mitigates known and foreseeable health, safety, or fundamental-rights risks, with input from post-market monitoring and ongoing testing. The framework requires iterative assessment and mitigation to ensure residual risk is acceptable, mandates testing before market (including real-world testing per Article 60), and requires consideration of impacts on minors and other vulnerable groups, while allowing integration with other Union-law risk-management provisions.
9.summary_2:
A risk management system must be created, implemented, documented, and maintained for high-risk AI systems, and it must function as a continuous, iterative process throughout the system's lifecycle. It requires regular reviews and updates and comprises identifying known and foreseeable health, safety, and fundamental rights risks (for the intended use), estimating risks under intended use and foreseeable misuse, evaluating additional risks using post-market monitoring data, and adopting targeted measures to address identified risks.
The risks addressed are limited to those that can reasonably be mitigated or eliminated through design, development, or provision of adequate technical information. Risk management measures should account for how the various regulatory requirements interact and should aim to minimize risk effectively while balancing the need to satisfy those requirements.
The measures must strive to eliminate or reduce risks where technically feasible, implement adequate mitigation for risks that cannot be eliminated, and provide required information and, where appropriate, training to deployers. In identifying the most appropriate measures, due consideration must be given to the technical knowledge, experience, education, expected training of the deployer, and the intended context of use.
High-risk AI systems must be tested to identify appropriate risk management measures, ensuring consistent performance for the intended purpose and compliance with the risk management requirements. Testing may include real-world testing where permitted, and should occur at any stage of development and, in any event, before market entry or deployment, conducted against predefined metrics and probabilistic thresholds. The process must also consider potential adverse impacts on persons under 18 and other vulnerable groups, and, where applicable, may be integrated with internal risk management procedures established under other Union-law requirements.

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

10.summary_1: High-risk AI systems using training, validation and testing data must be developed on data sets that meet defined quality criteria and are governed by data-management practices covering design choices, data origin, data-preparation operations, assumptions, data availability, and bias detection, mitigation, and data-gap addressing. These data sets must be relevant, representative, and as error-free as possible, with appropriate statistical properties, and take into account the geographical, contextual, and functional setting of use. Processing of special categories of personal data for bias detection and correction is allowed only under strict safeguards and the conditions set out in applicable privacy laws; if a system does not involve training data, the governance requirements apply only to testing data sets.
10.summary_2:
High-risk AI systems that involve training data must use training, validation and testing data sets that meet the quality criteria set out in the following paragraphs whenever such data are used.
Data governance and management practices must be appropriate for the intended purpose and cover: design choices; data collection processes and data origin (including the original purpose if personal data); data-preparation operations such as annotation, labeling, cleaning, updating, enrichment and aggregation; the formulation of assumptions about what the data measure and represent; assessment of the availability, quantity and suitability of the data; examination for biases that could affect health and safety, infringe fundamental rights, or cause discrimination; measures to detect, prevent and mitigate such biases; and identification of data gaps and how to address them.
Training, validation and testing data sets must be relevant, sufficiently representative, and as free from errors and as complete as possible for the intended purpose, with appropriate statistical properties--also with regard to the persons or groups the system is intended to serve. These characteristics may be achieved at the level of an individual data set or at the level of a combination of data sets.
Data sets must account for the geographical, contextual, behavioral or functional setting in which the high-risk AI system will be used, to the extent required by the intended purpose.
To the extent strictly necessary for bias detection and correction, providers may exceptionally process special categories of personal data, subject to safeguards beyond those in Regulation (EU) 2016/679, Directive (EU) 2016/680 and Regulation (EU) 2018/1725, provided the following conditions are met: bias detection/correction cannot be effectively fulfilled using other data (including synthetic or anonymised data); the data are protected by technical limitations on reuse and by state-of-the-art security and privacy-preserving measures (e.g., pseudonymisation); strict access controls and documentation to ensure only authorized personnel access with confidentiality obligations; no transmission to or access by other parties; deletion once the bias is corrected or the retention period ends; and records of processing activities explain why processing of special categories was strictly necessary and why other data could not achieve the objective.
For high-risk AI systems that do not involve training techniques, paragraphs 2 to 5 apply only to the testing data sets.

11.text:
1. The technical documentation of a high-risk AI system shall be drawn up before that system is placed on the market or put into service and shall be kept up-to date.
The technical documentation shall be drawn up in such a way as to demonstrate that the high-risk AI system complies with the requirements set out in this Section and to provide national competent authorities and notified bodies with the necessary information in a clear and comprehensive form to assess the compliance of the AI system with those requirements. It shall contain, at a minimum, the elements set out in Annex IV. SMEs, including start-ups, may provide the elements of the technical documentation specified in Annex IV in a simplified manner. To that end, the Commission shall establish a simplified technical documentation form targeted at the needs of small and microenterprises. Where an SME, including a start-up, opts to provide the information required in Annex IV in a simplified manner, it shall use the form referred to in this paragraph. Notified bodies shall accept the form for the purposes of the conformity assessment.

2. Where a high-risk AI system related to a product covered by the Union harmonisation legislation listed in Section A of Annex I is placed on the market or put into service, a single set of technical documentation shall be drawn up containing all the information set out in paragraph 1, as well as the information required under those legal acts.

3. The Commission is empowered to adopt delegated acts in accordance with Article 97 in order to amend Annex IV, where necessary, to ensure that, in light of technical progress, the technical documentation provides all the information necessary to assess the compliance of the system with the requirements set out in this Section.

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

11.summary_1: Technical documentation for high-risk AI systems must be prepared before market or deployment and kept up to date, and it must clearly demonstrate compliance and provide authorities with necessary information, including at least the elements in Annex IV (with a simplified form for SMEs/start-ups). If the system relates to a product within Union harmonisation legislation in Annex I Section A, a single consolidated technical documentation set must cover both the core documentation and the relevant acts. The Commission may adopt delegated acts under Article 97 to amend Annex IV to reflect technical progress.
11.summary_2:
Technical documentation for high-risk AI systems must be prepared before the system is marketed or placed into service and kept up to date. The documentation must be capable of showing compliance with the relevant requirements and provide national competent authorities and notified bodies with clear, comprehensive information to assess conformity.
It shall include at least the elements set out in Annex IV. SMEs and startups may supply Annex IV elements in a simplified form, and the Commission will establish a simplified documentation form tailored to small and microenterprises. If an SME or startup chooses the simplified path, it must use the Commission-provided form, which notified bodies must accept for conformity assessment.
For high-risk AI systems related to products covered by Union harmonisation legislation listed in Annex I, a single set of documentation must be drawn up that includes both the information required in the general paragraph and the information required by those applicable legal acts.
The Commission is empowered to adopt delegated acts to amend Annex IV as needed to reflect technical progress, ensuring the documentation remains sufficient to assess compliance with the applicable requirements.

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

12.summary_1: High-risk AI systems must provide automatic event logging throughout their lifecycle to enable traceability. Logs should capture situations that may create risk, support post-market monitoring, and monitor operation, with minimum detailed records for systems listed in Annex III, such as use timing, input data checks, and verification personnel. These requirements ensure auditable records and accountability to support ongoing risk management and oversight.
12.summary_2:
High-risk AI systems must be capable of automatically recording logs for their entire operational life. This imposes an implementation obligation on providers to ensure comprehensive, system-wide event recording.
The logging capabilities must capture events that support traceability for the system's intended purpose, specifically to (i) identify situations that may create risk or prompt substantial modification, (ii) facilitate post-market monitoring, and (iii) monitor the system's ongoing operation as described in the applicable provisions.
For those high-risk systems that fall within the specified Annex III subset (a), the logging must at a minimum include: the start and end times of each use; the reference database used to check input data; the input data that produced a match; and the identification of the natural persons involved in verifying the results.

13.unit_title: Transparency and provision of information to deployers

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

13.summary_1: High-risk AI systems must be designed with sufficient transparency to enable deployers to interpret outputs and use them appropriately, reflecting the obligations of providers and deployers. They must be accompanied by instructions for use in a digital format that are concise, complete, correct, and accessible, including the provider’s identity and contact details, system characteristics and limitations, expected performance metrics and validation, foreseeable risks, explanation capabilities, data-set information, and guidance to interpret outputs. The instructions must also cover pre-determined changes at conformity assessment, human oversight measures, necessary resources and maintenance, and the mechanisms to collect, store, and interpret logs in line with the logging requirements.
13.summary_2:
High-risk AI systems must be designed so their operation is sufficiently transparent to allow deployers to interpret outputs and use them appropriately, with the level of transparency aligned to satisfy the provider's and the deployer's obligations in the relevant section governing their duties.
The systems must be accompanied by instructions for use in an appropriate format that are concise, complete, correct, and clear, and that deployers can access and understand.
The instructions must contain at least: (a) the provider's identity and contact details (and authorized representative, if applicable); (b) the system's characteristics, capabilities, and performance limitations, including its intended purpose; the level of accuracy, robustness, and cybersecurity against which it has been tested and validated, and any foreseeable factors that could affect those levels; any known or foreseeable misuse scenarios that could create health, safety, or fundamental-rights risks; (iv) the technical capabilities to explain outputs where applicable; (v) performance for specific individuals or groups when relevant; (vi) input-data specifications or related information on training, validation, and testing data sets used; and (vii) information enabling deployers to interpret outputs and apply them correctly.
The instructions must also disclose any pre-determined changes to the system and its performance identified at the initial conformity assessment, if any.
Additionally, the instructions must cover the human-oversight measures described in Article 14, including the technical means to aid deployers in interpreting outputs; the computational and hardware resources required, the system's expected lifetime, and required maintenance and care measures (including frequency and handling of software updates); and, where relevant, information about mechanisms within the system that enable deployers to collect, store, and interpret logs in compliance with Article 12.

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

14.summary_1: High-risk AI systems must be designed for effective human oversight during use, supported by appropriate interfaces. Oversight aims to prevent health, safety, or fundamental-rights risks and may be built into the system or implemented by the deployer, depending on feasibility and use context. For Annex III systems, oversight must require separate verification by at least two competent natural persons, while deployers and users must understand capacities, guard against automation bias, interpret outputs correctly, and have the ability to override or stop the system.
14.summary_2:
High-risk AI systems must be designed and developed so that natural persons can effectively oversee them during use, including through appropriate human-machine interface tools.
Oversight should aim to prevent or minimize risks to health, safety, or fundamental rights that may arise from the system's intended use or reasonably foreseeable misuse, especially when such risks persist despite other regulatory requirements.
The oversight measures must be proportionate to the risks, the level of autonomy, and the context of use, and may be implemented either by the provider before market or deployment, or by the deployer before market or service, or a combination of both.
To implement oversight, the deployer must be provided with the system in a way that enables designated humans to: understand the system's capacities and limitations and monitor operations; detect anomalies and address them; avoid automation bias; correctly interpret outputs; decide not to use or override the system when appropriate; and intervene or stop the system to bring it to a safe state.
For high-risk systems in the biometric/remote identification category listed in Annex III, the measures must also ensure that no action or decision based on the system's identification occurs unless it has been independently verified and confirmed by at least two natural persons with the necessary competence, training, and authority. This two-person verification requirement does not apply in law enforcement, migration, border control, or asylum contexts where applicable law deems it disproportionate.

15.text:
1. High-risk AI systems shall be designed and developed in such a way that they achieve an appropriate level of accuracy, robustness, and cybersecurity, and that they perform consistently in those respects throughout their lifecycle.

2. To address the technical aspects of how to measure the appropriate levels of accuracy and robustness set out in paragraph 1 and any other relevant performance metrics, the Commission shall, in cooperation with relevant stakeholders and organisations such as metrology and benchmarking authorities, encourage, as appropriate, the development of benchmarks and measurement methodologies.

3. The levels of accuracy and the relevant accuracy metrics of high-risk AI systems shall be declared in the accompanying instructions of use.

4. High-risk AI systems shall be as resilient as possible regarding errors, faults or inconsistencies that may occur within the system or the environment in which the system operates, in particular due to their interaction with natural persons or other systems. Technical and organisational measures shall be taken in this regard.
The robustness of high-risk AI systems may be achieved through technical redundancy solutions, which may include backup or fail-safe plans.
High-risk AI systems that continue to learn after being placed on the market or put into service shall be developed in such a way as to eliminate or reduce as far as possible the risk of possibly biased outputs influencing input for future operations (feedback loops), and as to ensure that any such feedback loops are duly addressed with appropriate mitigation measures.

5. High-risk AI systems shall be resilient against attempts by unauthorised third parties to alter their use, outputs or performance by exploiting system vulnerabilities.
The technical solutions aiming to ensure the cybersecurity of high-risk AI systems shall be appropriate to the relevant circumstances and the risks.
The technical solutions to address AI specific vulnerabilities shall include, where appropriate, measures to prevent, detect, respond to, resolve and control for attacks trying to manipulate the training data set (data poisoning), or pre-trained components used in training (model poisoning), inputs designed to cause the AI model to make a mistake (adversarial examples or model evasion), confidentiality attacks or model flaws.

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

15.summary_1: High-risk AI systems must achieve appropriate levels of accuracy, robustness, and cybersecurity across their lifecycle, with explicit metrics declared in their accompanying use instructions. The Commission, in cooperation with stakeholders and bodies such as metrology and benchmarking authorities, should develop benchmarks and methodologies to measure these performance levels. The standard also requires resilience against errors, faulty interactions with users or other systems, and protection against cybersecurity threats, including protections against data poisoning, model poisoning, adversarial inputs, and other attacks on training data or components.
15.summary_2:
High-risk AI systems must be designed and developed to achieve appropriate levels of accuracy, robustness, and cybersecurity, and to maintain these qualities consistently throughout their lifecycle. They should perform reliably across these dimensions despite environmental conditions or interactions with people and other systems.
The Commission, together with relevant stakeholders and organisations such as metrology and benchmarking authorities, should promote the development of benchmarks and measurement methodologies to quantify these performance aspects as appropriate to the context.
The responsible use instructions must declare the specified levels of accuracy and the relevant metrics, ensuring users are informed of the system's performance standards. High-risk systems should be built to be as resistant as possible to errors, faults, or inconsistencies, with technical and organisational measures in place; redundancy such as backup or fail-safe mechanisms may be employed.
For systems that continue to learn after deployment, designers must minimize the risk of biased outputs influencing future inputs (feedback loops) and implement mitigation measures to address any such effects. They must also be resilient against unauthorized attempts to alter use, outputs, or performance, applying cybersecurity measures appropriate to the circumstances and risks, including specific safeguards against data poisoning, model poisoning, adversarial inputs, confidentiality attacks, and other model flaws.

16.unit_title: Obligations of providers of high-risk AI systems

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

16.summary_1: Providers of high-risk AI systems must ensure compliance with applicable requirements, implement a quality management system, and maintain the required documentation and logs. They must undergo the relevant conformity assessment, issue an EU declaration of conformity, and affix the CE marking where possible while providing contact details. They must meet registration obligations, take corrective actions when needed, demonstrate conformity upon request by national authorities, and ensure accessibility in line with applicable EU directives.
16.summary_2:
Providers of high-risk AI systems must ensure overall compliance with the applicable requirements, maintain a quality management system, preserve the required technical and governance documentation, and support oversight actions. They are to subject the system to the appropriate conformity assessment before market or service deployment, draft an EU declaration of conformity, and affix the CE marking to indicate compliance. They must also participate in registration regimes and take prompt corrective actions when non-conformities are found, including informing distributors and authorities as required, and be able to demonstrate conformity to national authorities upon a reasoned request.
In addition, providers must clearly identify themselves and provide contact details. This identification must be shown on the high-risk AI system itself or, if not feasible, on its packaging or accompanying documentation. They are also obligated to ensure accessibility requirements are met in line with the relevant EU accessibility directives.
Under their control, providers must retain logs automatically generated by the systems, and keep the relevant documentation and records for the period and in the manner required by the regulation. They must ensure ongoing compliance through the conformity assessment and maintain the necessary documentation to support continued conformity, including the ability to provide information and cooperate with authorities in case of inquiries or investigations.
Finally, providers must implement and adhere to accessibility obligations, ensuring the high-risk AI system complies with the EU accessibility directives, thereby supporting usable and accessible deployment across the relevant contexts.

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

17.summary_1: Providers of high-risk AI systems must establish a documented quality management system that ensures compliance with this Regulation, expressed through written policies and procedures and covering design control, development, data management, risk management, post-market monitoring, incident reporting, record-keeping, governance, and communications with authorities. The system must address conformity assessment procedures, management of modifications, the risk management system referenced in Article 9, post-market monitoring under Article 72, and serious incident reporting under Article 73, applying applicable standards or the means to ensure compliance when harmonised standards do not fully cover requirements, with a proportionate implementation to the provider's size. For financial institutions, the obligation to implement a QMS (excluding some points) is deemed fulfilled by compliance with internal governance arrangements or processes under Union financial services law, and harmonised standards in Article 40 are to be taken into account.
17.summary_2:
Providers of high-risk AI systems must establish a documented quality management system (QMS) that ensures regulatory compliance. The QMS must be written and organized as policies, procedures, and instructions, covering: a compliance strategy (including conformity assessment procedures and how changes to the high-risk AI system are managed); design controls and verification; development, quality control, and quality assurance; examination, testing, and validation before, during, and after development (with stated frequencies); technical specifications and, if harmonised standards are not fully applied, the means to ensure compliance with relevant requirements; data management across the data lifecycle; the risk management system; post-market monitoring; reporting of serious incidents; communications with authorities, data providers, notified bodies, operators, customers, and other parties; record-keeping; resource management including security-of-supply; and an accountability framework detailing management and staff responsibilities.
Implementation of the QMS must be proportionate to the provider's size, while maintaining the appropriate degree of rigor and protection required to ensure compliance with the Regulation.
If a provider is subject to sector-specific Union law that already imposes quality management or an equivalent function, those obligations may incorporate the listed QMS aspects as part of the existing quality management system under that law.
For financial institutions, the obligation to establish a QMS (except for the aspects on risk management, post-market monitoring, and incident reporting) can be considered fulfilled by complying with internal governance rules under Union financial services law, provided harmonised standards referenced in Article 40 are taken into account.

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

18.summary_1: Providers of high-risk AI systems must keep, for 10 years after market placement or service, the technical documentation (Article 11), the quality management system documentation (Article 17), and documentation related to changes approved by notified bodies and decisions or documents issued by notified bodies, as well as the EU declaration of conformity (Article 47). Member States may set conditions for keeping this documentation if the provider or its authorized representative goes bankrupt or ceases activity before the retention period ends. Financial institutions must also maintain the technical documentation as part of the documentation kept under Union financial services law.
18.summary_2:
The provider must retain and keep available for 10 years after a high-risk AI system is placed on the market or placed into service the core and related documentation listed in this provision: the technical documentation (Article 11), the quality management system documentation (Article 17), records of changes approved by notified bodies (when applicable), decisions and other documents issued by notified bodies (when applicable), and the EU declaration of conformity (Article 47). This documentation must be at the disposal of national competent authorities.
Member States may set conditions governing how this documentation remains available for the full 10-year period in cases where the provider or its authorized representative established in the Member State goes bankrupt or ceases activity before the period ends.
Providers that are financial institutions and subject to internal governance requirements under Union financial services law must include the technical documentation as part of the documentation kept under that applicable law.

19.text:
1. Providers of high-risk AI systems shall keep the logs referred to in Article 12(1), automatically generated by their high-risk AI systems, to the extent such logs are under their control. Without prejudice to applicable Union or national law, the logs shall be kept for a period appropriate to the intended purpose of the high-risk AI system, of at least six months, unless provided otherwise in the applicable Union or national law, in particular in Union law on the protection of personal data.

2. Providers that are financial institutions subject to requirements regarding their internal governance, arrangements or processes under Union financial services law shall maintain the logs automatically generated by their high-risk AI systems as part of the documentation kept under the relevant financial services law.

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

19.summary_1: Providers of high-risk AI systems must retain logs automatically generated by their systems for at least six months, to the extent they are under their control, with the period aligned to the system’s intended purpose and in compliance with applicable data protection laws. If the provider is a financial institution subject to Union financial services law, the logs must be included as part of the documentation kept under that framework. This supports accountability and regulatory oversight by ensuring ready access to operation logs, subject to applicable legal overrides.
19.summary_2:
High-risk AI system providers must retain the automatically generated logs they control for a minimum of six months, with a retention period that may be longer if required by applicable Union or national law, particularly data protection rules.
- Financial institutions governed by Union financial services law must include these automatically generated logs as part of the documentation kept under the relevant financial services regime.

20.unit_title: Corrective actions and duty of information
20.text:
1. Providers of high-risk AI systems which consider or have reason to consider that a high-risk AI system that they have placed on the market or put into service is not in conformity with this Regulation shall immediately take the necessary corrective actions to bring that system into conformity, to withdraw it, to disable it, or to recall it, as appropriate. They shall inform the distributors of the high-risk AI system concerned and, where applicable, the deployers, the authorised representative and importers accordingly.

2. Where the high-risk AI system presents a risk within the meaning of Article 79(1) and the provider becomes aware of that risk, it shall immediately investigate the causes, in collaboration with the reporting deployer, where applicable, and inform the market surveillance authorities competent for the high-risk AI system concerned and, where applicable, the notified body that issued a certificate for that high-risk AI system in accordance with Article 44, in particular, of the nature of the non-compliance and of any relevant corrective action taken.

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

20.summary_1: Providers of high-risk AI systems must take immediate corrective actions to bring a non-conforming system into conformity, withdraw it, disable it, or recall it, and inform the distributors, the deployers, the authorised representative, and importers as applicable. If the system presents a risk as defined in Article 79(1), the provider must immediately investigate the causes in collaboration with the reporting deployer and inform the competent market surveillance authorities and, where applicable, the notified body that issued the certificate, of the nature of the non-conformity and any corrective actions taken.
20.summary_2:
Providers of high-risk AI systems must act immediately to correct non-conformities, withdraw, disable, or recall the system as appropriate, and must notify the distributors, and, where applicable, the deployers, authorized representative, and importers.
