39.summary_2: Third-country conformity assessment bodies established under the law of a non-EU country with which the Union has an agreement may be authorised to perform the notified-body functions under this Regulation, provided they meet the applicable requirements or demonstrate an equivalent level of compliance.

40.text:
1. High-risk AI systems or general-purpose AI models which are in conformity with harmonised standards or parts thereof the references of which have been published in the Official Journal of the European Union in accordance with Regulation (EU) No 1025/2012 shall be presumed to be in conformity with the requirements set out in Section 2 of this Chapter or, as applicable, with the obligations set out in of Chapter V, Sections 2 and 3, of this Regulation, to the extent that those standards cover those requirements or obligations.

2. In accordance with Article 10 of Regulation (EU) No 1025/2012, the Commission shall issue, without undue delay, standardisation requests covering all requirements set out in Section 2 of this Chapter and, as applicable, standardisation requests covering obligations set out in Chapter V, Sections 2 and 3, of this Regulation. The standardisation request shall also ask for deliverables on reporting and documentation processes to improve AI systems' resource performance, such as reducing the high-risk AI system's consumption of energy and of other resources during its lifecycle, and on the energy-efficient development of general-purpose AI models. When preparing a standardisation request, the Commission shall consult the Board and relevant stakeholders, including the advisory forum.
When issuing a standardisation request to European standardisation organisations, the Commission shall specify that standards have to be clear, consistent, including with the standards developed in the various sectors for products covered by the existing Union harmonisation legislation listed in Annex I, and aiming to ensure that high-risk AI systems or general-purpose AI models placed on the market or put into service in the Union meet the relevant requirements or obligations laid down in this Regulation.
The Commission shall request the European standardisation organisations to provide evidence of their best efforts to fulfil the objectives referred to in the first and the second subparagraph of this paragraph in accordance with Article 24 of Regulation (EU) No 1025/2012.

3. The participants in the standardisation process shall seek to promote investment and innovation in AI, including through increasing legal certainty, as well as the competitiveness and growth of the Union market, to contribute to strengthening global cooperation on standardisation and taking into account existing international standards in the field of AI that are consistent with Union values, fundamental rights and interests, and to enhance multi-stakeholder governance ensuring a balanced representation of interests and the effective participation of all relevant stakeholders in accordance with Articles 5, 6, and 7 of Regulation (EU) No 1025/2012.

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

40.summary_1: High-risk AI systems and general-purpose models that conform to harmonised standards published in the Official Journal are presumed to meet applicable requirements or obligations under this Regulation. The Commission must issue standardisation requests and seek deliverables on reporting, documentation, and energy/resource efficiency, consulting the Board and stakeholders. The standardisation process should foster investment and innovation while aligning with existing international standards and ensuring balanced multi-stakeholder governance and EU values.
40.summary_2:
High-risk AI systems and general-purpose AI models that conform to harmonised standards published in the Official Journal under Regulation 1025/2012 are presumed to meet the applicable requirements in Section 2 or the relevant obligations in Chapter V, Sections 2-3, to the extent those standards address them.
The Commission must issue standardisation requests under Regulation 1025/2012 to cover all Section 2 requirements and applicable Chapter V obligations, including deliverables on reporting and documentation to improve resource performance (e.g., lifecycle energy and resource use) and on energy-efficient development of general-purpose AI models. The Commission must consult the Board and stakeholders, and when engaging European standardisation organisations, require clear, consistent standards aligned with existing Union harmonisation legislation in Annex I. It must also obtain evidence of best efforts to meet these objectives in accordance with Regulation 1025/2012 Article 24.
The standardisation process should promote AI investment and innovation, enhance legal certainty, and strengthen the EU market's competitiveness and growth, while contributing to global standardisation cooperation. It must take into account existing international standards compatible with EU values and fundamental rights, and advance multi-stakeholder governance with balanced representation and effective participation of all relevant stakeholders in line with Regulation 1025/2012.

41.text:
1. The Commission may adopt, implementing acts establishing common specifications for the requirements set out in Section 2 of this Chapter or, as applicable, for the obligations set out in Sections 2 and 3 of Chapter V where the following conditions have been fulfilled:
(a) the Commission has requested, pursuant to Article 10(1) of Regulation (EU) No 1025/2012, one or more European standardisation organisations to draft a harmonised standard for the requirements set out in Section 2 of this Chapter, or, as applicable, for the obligations set out in Sections 2 and 3 of Chapter V, and:
    (i) the request has not been accepted by any of the European standardisation organisations; or
    (ii) the harmonised standards addressing that request are not delivered within the deadline set in accordance with Article 10(1) of Regulation (EU) No 1025/2012; or
    (iii) the relevant harmonised standards insufficiently address fundamental rights concerns; or
    (iv) the harmonised standards do not comply with the request; and
(b) no reference to harmonised standards covering the requirements referred to in Section 2 of this Chapter or, as applicable, the obligations referred to in Sections 2 and 3 of Chapter V has been published in the Official Journal of the European Union in accordance with Regulation (EU) No 1025/2012, and no such reference is expected to be published within a reasonable period.
When drafting the common specifications, the Commission shall consult the advisory forum referred to in Article 67.
The implementing acts referred to in the first subparagraph of this paragraph shall be adopted in accordance with the examination procedure referred to in Article 98(2).

2. Before preparing a draft implementing act, the Commission shall inform the committee referred to in Article 22 of Regulation (EU) No 1025/2012 that it considers the conditions laid down in paragraph 1 of this Article to be fulfilled.

3. High-risk AI systems or general-purpose AI models which are in conformity with the common specifications referred to in paragraph 1, or parts of those specifications, shall be presumed to be in conformity with the requirements set out in Section 2 of this Chapter or, as applicable, to comply with the obligations referred to in Sections 2 and 3 of Chapter V, to the extent those common specifications cover those requirements or those obligations.

4. Where a harmonised standard is adopted by a European standardisation organisation and proposed to the Commission for the publication of its reference in the Official Journal of the European Union, the Commission shall assess the harmonised standard in accordance with Regulation (EU) No 1025/2012. When reference to a harmonised standard is published in the Official Journal of the European Union, the Commission shall repeal the implementing acts referred to in paragraph 1, or parts thereof which cover the same requirements set out in Section 2 of this Chapter or, as applicable, the same obligations set out in Sections 2 and 3 of Chapter V.

5. Where providers of high-risk AI systems or general-purpose AI models do not comply with the common specifications referred to in paragraph 1, they shall duly justify that they have adopted technical solutions that meet the requirements referred to in Section 2 of this Chapter or, as applicable, comply with the obligations set out in Sections 2 and 3 of Chapter V to a level at least equivalent thereto.

6. Where a Member State considers that a common specification does not entirely meet the requirements set out in Section 2 or, as applicable, comply with obligations set out in Sections 2 and 3 of Chapter V, it shall inform the Commission thereof with a detailed explanation. The Commission shall assess that information and, if appropriate, amend the implementing act establishing the common specification concerned.

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

41.summary_1: The Commission may adopt implementing acts to establish common specifications for the requirements in Section 2 or for the obligations in Sections 2 and 3 of Chapter V, but only if harmonised standards have not been delivered, do not address fundamental rights, or are not compliant, and if no EU reference to such standards exists or is expected; it requires consultation with the advisory forum and adherence to the Article 98(2) examination procedure. Before drafting an implementing act, the Commission must inform the committee under Regulation (EU) No 1025/2012 that the conditions are fulfilled. High-risk AI systems or general-purpose AI models conforming to these common specifications are presumed to conform to the corresponding requirements or obligations; non-compliance must be justified with equivalent technical measures; and if a harmonised standard is adopted and published, the Commission shall assess it and repeal predecessor acts; and Member States can flag any partial non-fulfillment with a detailed explanation prompting possible amendments.

need_ref.value: Official Journal of the European Union

41.summary_2:
The implementing acts establishing common specifications for the required obligations may be adopted only if the Commission has requested European standardisation organisations to draft a harmonised standard for the relevant requirements or obligations, and the request has not been accepted, the harmonised standards are not delivered within the deadline, the standards do not adequately address fundamental rights concerns, or they do not comply with the request; and there is no published reference to harmonised standards in the Official Journal, nor is one expected within a reasonable period. The Commission must consult the advisory forum when drafting these specifications, and the implementing acts are to be adopted using the examination procedure set out in the applicable rules. 
Before preparing a draft implementing act, the Commission must inform the committee under Regulation (EU) No 1025/2012 that the conditions for adopting common specifications are fulfilled. 
High-risk AI systems or general-purpose AI models that conform to the common specifications (or parts of them) are presumed to conform to the corresponding requirements in Section 2 or the obligations in Chapter V to the extent covered by those specifications. If a harmonised standard is adopted and published reference is provided in the Official Journal, the Commission must assess the standard under Regulation (EU) No 1025/2012 and repeal the relevant implementing acts (or the parts) that cover the same requirements or obligations. 
If providers do not comply with the common specifications, they must justify that they have adopted technical solutions achieving at least an equivalent level of compliance with the relevant requirements or obligations. If a Member State considers that a common specification does not fully meet the requirements or obligations, it must inform the Commission with a detailed explanation, and the Commission shall assess and, if appropriate, amend the implementing act establishing the common specification.

42.unit_title: Presumption of conformity with certain requirements
42.text:
1. High-risk AI systems that have been trained and tested on data reflecting the specific geographical, behavioural, contextual or functional setting within which they are intended to be used shall be presumed to comply with the relevant requirements laid down in Article 10(4).

2. High-risk AI systems that have been certified or for which a statement of conformity has been issued under a cybersecurity scheme pursuant to Regulation (EU) 2019/881 and the references of which have been published in the Official Journal of the European Union shall be presumed to comply with the cybersecurity requirements set out in Article 15 of this Regulation in so far as the cybersecurity certificate or statement of conformity or parts thereof cover those requirements.

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

42.summary_1: High-risk AI systems trained and tested in the exact setting where they will be used are presumed to comply with the relevant requirements in Article 10(4). High-risk AI systems certified under a cybersecurity scheme under Regulation (EU) 2019/881 and published in the Official Journal are presumed to meet the cybersecurity requirements in Article 15, insofar as the certificate covers those requirements.

need_ref.value: Official Journal of the European Union

42.summary_2:
High-risk AI systems that have been trained and tested on data reflecting the particular geographical, behavioural, contextual, or functional setting in which they will be used are presumed to comply with the relevant data governance and quality requirements set out in Article 10(4), including aspects such as data origin, preparation, bias detection and mitigation, and overall data suitability for the intended use.
Additionally, high-risk AI systems that have been certified or for which a conformity statement has been issued under a cybersecurity scheme pursuant to Regulation (EU) 2019/881, with references published in the Official Journal, are presumed to comply with the cybersecurity requirements in Article 15 to the extent that the certificate or conformity statement covers those requirements.

43.text:
1. For high-risk AI systems listed in point 1 of Annex III, where, in demonstrating the compliance of a high-risk AI system with the requirements set out in Section 2, the provider has applied harmonised standards referred to in Article 40, or, where applicable, common specifications referred to in Article 41, the provider shall opt for one of the following conformity assessment procedures based on:
(a) the internal control referred to in Annex VI; or
(b) the assessment of the quality management system and the assessment of the technical documentation, with the involvement of a notified body, referred to in Annex VII.
In demonstrating the compliance of a high-risk AI system with the requirements set out in Section 2, the provider shall follow the conformity assessment procedure set out in Annex VII where:
(a) harmonised standards referred to in Article 40 do not exist, and common specifications referred to in Article 41 are not available;
(b) the provider has not applied, or has applied only part of, the harmonised standard;
(c) the common specifications referred to in point (a) exist, but the provider has not applied them;
(d) one or more of the harmonised standards referred to in point (a) has been published with a restriction, and only on the part of the standard that was restricted.
For the purposes of the conformity assessment procedure referred to in Annex VII, the provider may choose any of the notified bodies. However, where the high-risk AI system is intended to be put into service by law enforcement, immigration or asylum authorities or by Union institutions, bodies, offices or agencies, the market surveillance authority referred to in Article 74(8) or (9), as applicable, shall act as a notified body.

2. For high-risk AI systems referred to in points 2 to 8 of Annex III, providers shall follow the conformity assessment procedure based on internal control as referred to in Annex VI, which does not provide for the involvement of a notified body.

3. For high-risk AI systems covered by the Union harmonisation legislation listed in Section A of Annex I, the provider shall follow the relevant conformity assessment procedure as required under those legal acts. The requirements set out in Section 2 of this Chapter shall apply to those high-risk AI systems and shall be part of that assessment. Points 4.3., 4.4., 4.5. and the fifth paragraph of point 4.6 of Annex VII shall also apply.
For the purposes of that assessment, notified bodies which have been notified under those legal acts shall be entitled to control the conformity of the high-risk AI systems with the requirements set out in Section 2, provided that the compliance of those notified bodies with requirements laid down in Article 31(4), (5), (10) and (11) has been assessed in the context of the notification procedure under those legal acts.
Where a legal act listed in Section A of Annex I enables the product manufacturer to opt out from a third-party conformity assessment, provided that that manufacturer has applied all harmonised standards covering all the relevant requirements, that manufacturer may use that option only if it has also applied harmonised standards or, where applicable, common specifications referred to in Article 41, covering all requirements set out in Section 2 of this Chapter.

4. High-risk AI systems that have already been subject to a conformity assessment procedure shall undergo a new conformity assessment procedure in the event of a substantial modification, regardless of whether the modified system is intended to be further distributed or continues to be used by the current deployer.
For high-risk AI systems that continue to learn after being placed on the market or put into service, changes to the high-risk AI system and its performance that have been pre-determined by the provider at the moment of the initial conformity assessment and are part of the information contained in the technical documentation referred to in point 2(f) of Annex IV, shall not constitute a substantial modification.

5. The Commission is empowered to adopt delegated acts in accordance with Article 97 in order to amend Annexes VI and VII by updating them in light of technical progress.

6. The Commission is empowered to adopt delegated acts in accordance with Article 97 in order to amend paragraphs 1 and 2 of this Article in order to subject high-risk AI systems referred to in points 2 to 8 of Annex III to the conformity assessment procedure referred to in Annex VII or parts thereof. The Commission shall adopt such delegated acts taking into account the effectiveness of the conformity assessment procedure based on internal control referred to in Annex VI in preventing or minimising the risks to health and safety and protection of fundamental rights posed by such systems, as well as the availability of adequate capacities and resources among notified bodies.

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

43.summary_1: This article governs conformity assessment for high-risk AI systems, allowing providers to choose between internal control (Annex VI) or a notified-body assessment of quality management and technical documentation (Annex VII) depending on whether harmonised standards (Art. 40) or common specifications (Art. 41) apply, and it sets the internal-control path for HR AI listed in Annex III points 2–8. It also covers HR AI under Union harmonisation in Annex I Section A, the authorities to amend Annexes VI and VII by delegated acts (Art. 97), requirements for new conformity assessments after substantial modifications (with exceptions for pre-determined changes in continuing-learning systems), and references to specific annexes and articles (including Annex I, Annex III, Annex IV, Article 74 and Article 31).
43.summary_2:
For high-risk AI systems listed in Annex III point 1, conformity assessment offers two paths if harmonised standards (or, where applicable, common specifications) have been applied: (i) internal control under Annex VI, or (ii) a third-party route involving a notified body to assess the quality management system and the technical documentation under Annex VII. The Annex VII route must be used if harmonised standards do not exist or have not been applied (fully or partially), common specifications are unavailable or not applied, or harmonised standards exist but are restricted to only part of the requirements. If the system is intended for use by law enforcement, immigration/asylum authorities, or EU institutions, the market surveillance authority may act as the notified body.
For high-risk AI systems in Annex III points 2 to 8, conformity is strictly via internal control under Annex VI, which does not involve a notified body. This pathway emphasizes ongoing internal processes and documentation without third-party certification. The regime reflects a lighter-touch oversight for these subcategories but still requires adherence to the Section 2 requirements.
For high-risk AI systems covered by Union harmonisation legislation in Annex I Section A, the provider must follow the conformity assessment prescribed by those Acts, with Section 2 requirements incorporated into that assessment. The relevant provisions of Annex VII (including its 4.3-4.6 and the fifth paragraph of 4.6) apply, and notified bodies under those acts may supervise conformity, provided their own eligibility criteria (Article 31(4), (5), (10), (11)) were assessed during the act's notification procedure. An opt-out from third-party assessment is permitted only if the legal act allows it and harmonised standards (covering all Section 2 requirements) have been applied, including equivalent common specifications.
High-risk AI systems that have already undergone conformity assessment must undergo a new conformity assessment if there is a substantial modification, regardless of distribution or current deployment. Systems that continue to learn after market and whose changes were predetermined by the provider and documented in the technical documentation ( Annex IV, point 2(f)) are not considered a substantial modification.
The Commission may adopt delegated acts to update Annexes VI and VII in light of technical progress, and to extend Annex VII conformity assessment to high-risk AI systems in Annex III points 2-8 (or parts thereof). Such acts will consider the effectiveness of the internal-control approach (Annex VI), the risk-management and fundamental-rights protections, and the capacity and resources of notified bodies.

44.text:
1. Certificates issued by notified bodies in accordance with Annex VII shall be drawn-up in a language which can be easily understood by the relevant authorities in the Member State in which the notified body is established.

2. Certificates shall be valid for the period they indicate, which shall not exceed five years for AI systems covered by Annex I, and four years for AI systems covered by Annex III. At the request of the provider, the validity of a certificate may be extended for further periods, each not exceeding five years for AI systems covered by Annex I, and four years for AI systems covered by Annex III, based on a re-assessment in accordance with the applicable conformity assessment procedures. Any supplement to a certificate shall remain valid, provided that the certificate which it supplements is valid.

3. Where a notified body finds that an AI system no longer meets the requirements set out in Section 2, it shall, taking account of the principle of proportionality, suspend or withdraw the certificate issued or impose restrictions on it, unless compliance with those requirements is ensured by appropriate corrective action taken by the provider of the system within an appropriate deadline set by the notified body. The notified body shall give reasons for its decision.
An appeal procedure against decisions of the notified bodies, including on conformity certificates issued, shall be available.

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

44.summary_1: Certificates issued by notified bodies under Annex VII must be drawn up in language easily understood by the relevant authorities. Their validity shall not exceed five years for AI systems covered by Annex I or four years for AI systems covered by Annex III, with possible extensions after re-assessment; any supplement to a certificate remains valid if the underlying certificate is valid. If the AI system no longer meets the requirements, the notified body may suspend, withdraw, or restrict the certificate, with reasons, and providers have an appeal procedure for such decisions.
44.summary_2:
Certificates issued by notified bodies must be drawn up in a language easily understood by the relevant authorities in the Member State where the notified body is established.
Certificates are valid for the period stated, up to five years for AI systems covered by Annex I and up to four years for AI systems covered by Annex III. At the provider's request, validity may be extended for additional periods of up to five years (Annex I) or four years (Annex III) following a re-assessment under the applicable conformity assessment procedures. Any certificate supplement remains valid so long as the underlying certificate is valid.
If a notified body determines that an AI system no longer meets the requirements in Section 2, it must suspend or withdraw the certificate or impose restrictions, applying the principle of proportionality, unless the provider takes appropriate corrective action within a deadline set by the notified body. The notified body must provide reasons for its decision.
An appeal procedure is available against decisions of the notified bodies, including decisions on conformity certificates.

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

45.summary_1: Notified bodies must promptly inform the notifying authority about Union technical documentation assessment certificates, supplements, and quality management system approvals issued under Annex VII, as well as refusals, restrictions, suspensions, or withdrawals; they must report any circumstances affecting notification and any information requests from market surveillance authorities, and, on request, conformity assessment activities including cross-border and subcontracting. They must also inform other notified bodies about refusals, suspensions, or withdrawals of quality management system approvals and about certificates and supplements they have refused, withdrawn, suspended, or issued upon request. Additionally, they must share with other notified bodies performing similar conformity assessment activities information on negative conformity assessments and, on request, positive results, while safeguarding confidentiality as required by Article 78.
45.summary_2:
Notified bodies must promptly inform the notifying authority about: all Union technical documentation assessment certificates, any supplements, and any quality management system approvals issued under Annex VII; any refusals, restrictions, suspensions, or withdrawals of such certificates or approvals; any changes affecting the scope or conditions of their notification; any information requests from market surveillance authorities related to conformity assessment activities; and, on request, conformity assessment activities within their notification scope, including cross-border and subcontracted work.
They must also inform other notified bodies about: refusals, suspensions, or withdrawals of quality management system approvals (and, on request, approvals issued); refusals, withdrawals, suspensions, or restrictions of Union technical documentation assessment certificates or supplements (and, on request, the certificates and/or supplements issued).
Further, each notified body shall provide other notified bodies carrying out similar conformity assessment activities for the same AI system types with information on issues concerning negative conformity assessment results and, on request, positive results.
Notified bodies are obligated to safeguard the confidentiality of the information they obtain, in line with Article 78.

46.text:
1. By way of derogation from Article 43 and upon a duly justified request, any market surveillance authority may authorise the placing on the market or the putting into service of specific high-risk AI systems within the territory of the Member State concerned, for exceptional reasons of public security or the protection of life and health of persons, environmental protection or the protection of key industrial and infrastructural assets. That authorisation shall be for a limited period while the necessary conformity assessment procedures are being carried out, taking into account the exceptional reasons justifying the derogation. The completion of those procedures shall be undertaken without undue delay.

2. In a duly justified situation of urgency for exceptional reasons of public security or in the case of specific, substantial and imminent threat to the life or physical safety of natural persons, law-enforcement authorities or civil protection authorities may put a specific high-risk AI system into service without the authorisation referred to in paragraph 1, provided that such authorisation is requested during or after the use without undue delay. If the authorisation referred to in paragraph 1 is refused, the use of the high-risk AI system shall be stopped with immediate effect and all the results and outputs of such use shall be immediately discarded.

3. The authorisation referred to in paragraph 1 shall be issued only if the market surveillance authority concludes that the high-risk AI system complies with the requirements of Section 2. The market surveillance authority shall inform the Commission and the other Member States of any authorisation issued pursuant to paragraphs 1 and 2. This obligation shall not cover sensitive operational data in relation to the activities of law-enforcement authorities.

4. Where, within 15 calendar days of receipt of the information referred to in paragraph 3, no objection has been raised by either a Member State or the Commission in respect of an authorisation issued by a market surveillance authority of a Member State in accordance with paragraph 1, that authorisation shall be deemed justified.

5. Where, within 15 calendar days of receipt of the notification referred to in paragraph 3, objections are raised by a Member State against an authorisation issued by a market surveillance authority of another Member State, or where the Commission considers the authorisation to be contrary to Union law, or the conclusion of the Member States regarding the compliance of the system as referred to in paragraph 3 to be unfounded, the Commission shall, without delay, enter into consultations with the relevant Member State. The operators concerned shall be consulted and have the possibility to present their views. Having regard thereto, the Commission shall decide whether the authorisation is justified. The Commission shall address its decision to the Member State concerned and to the relevant operators.

6. Where the Commission considers the authorisation unjustified, it shall be withdrawn by the market surveillance authority of the Member State concerned.

7. For high-risk AI systems related to products covered by Union harmonisation legislation listed in Section A of Annex I, only the derogations from the conformity assessment established in that Union harmonisation legislation shall apply.

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

46.summary_1: Allows a market surveillance authority to authorise temporary placement or use of specific high-risk AI systems for exceptional public security, life/health protection, environmental protection, or critical infrastructure protection, for a limited period while the required conformity assessment is completed. In urgent cases of imminent risk, law-enforcement or civil-protection authorities may deploy a high-risk AI system without prior authorisation, but such deployment must be authorised during or after use and stopped if later refused. Any authorisation must be notified to the Commission and other Member States; if no objections are raised within 15 calendar days it shall be deemed justified, otherwise the Commission will decide and, if deemed unjustified, the authorisation must be withdrawn, with only derogations from the relevant Union harmonisation legislation applying to products listed in Annex I.
46.summary_2:
Market surveillance authorities may authorize the placing on the market or putting into service of specific high-risk AI systems within a Member State for exceptional reasons of public security, protection of life and health, environmental protection, or protection of key industrial and infrastructural assets. The authorisation shall be for a limited period while the necessary conformity assessment procedures are carried out, and these procedures must be completed without undue delay.
In duly justified urgent situations for exceptional public-security reasons or imminent threats to life or physical safety, law-enforcement or civil-protection authorities may deploy a specific high-risk AI system without the authorisation of paragraph 1, provided that such authorisation is requested during or after the use without undue delay. If the paragraph 1 authorisation is refused, the use of the system must be stopped immediately and all results and outputs discarded.
The authorisation under paragraph 1 shall be issued only if the market surveillance authority concludes that the high-risk AI system complies with the applicable requirements. The authority shall inform the Commission and the other Member States of any authorisation, with the exception of sensitive operational data related to law-enforcement activities.
If no objection is raised within 15 calendar days of receipt of the information, the authorisation shall be deemed justified. If objections are raised or the Commission considers the authorisation contrary to Union law or the compliance conclusion unfounded, the Commission shall consult with the relevant Member State and affected operators, allowing them to present views, and shall decide whether the authorisation is justified, with the decision addressed to the Member State concerned and the operators.
If the Commission deems the authorisation unjustified, it shall be withdrawn by the relevant Member State's market surveillance authority. For high-risk AI systems related to products covered by Union harmonisation legislation listed in Section A of Annex I, only derogations established in that harmonisation framework may apply.

47.text:
1. The provider shall draw up a written machine readable, physical or electronically signed EU declaration of conformity for each high-risk AI system, and keep it at the disposal of the national competent authorities for 10 years after the high-risk AI system has been placed on the market or put into service. The EU declaration of conformity shall identify the high-risk AI system for which it has been drawn up. A copy of the EU declaration of conformity shall be submitted to the relevant national competent authorities upon request.

2. The EU declaration of conformity shall state that the high-risk AI system concerned meets the requirements set out in Section 2. The EU declaration of conformity shall contain the information set out in Annex V, and shall be translated into a language that can be easily understood by the national competent authorities of the Member States in which the high-risk AI system is placed on the market or made available.

3. Where high-risk AI systems are subject to other Union harmonisation legislation which also requires an EU declaration of conformity, a single EU declaration of conformity shall be drawn up in respect of all Union law applicable to the high-risk AI system. The declaration shall contain all the information required to identify the Union harmonisation legislation to which the declaration relates.

4. By drawing up the EU declaration of conformity, the provider shall assume responsibility for compliance with the requirements set out in Section 2. The provider shall keep the EU declaration of conformity up-to-date as appropriate.

5. The Commission is empowered to adopt delegated acts in accordance with Article 97 in order to amend Annex V by updating the content of the EU declaration of conformity set out in that Annex, in order to introduce elements that become necessary in light of technical progress.

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

47.summary_1: The article requires providers of high-risk AI systems to draw up a written EU declaration of conformity, keep it for 10 years, and make it available to national authorities upon request, identifying the specific system. It mandates that the declaration state conformity with the applicable Section 2 requirements, include the information set out in Annex V, and be translated into a language understandable by authorities in the Member States where the system is marketed. If the system is also subject to other Union harmonisation legislation requiring a declaration, a single EU declaration suffices and must identify the applicable legislation; the provider assumes responsibility for compliance and must keep the declaration up to date, while the Commission may amend Annex V via delegated acts under Article 97 to reflect technical progress.
47.summary_2:
The provider must prepare a written EU declaration of conformity for each high-risk AI system. The declaration must be signed (machine readable, physical, or electronically) and kept available to national competent authorities for 10 years after the system is placed on the market or brought into service. It must identify the specific high-risk AI system and a copy must be provided to the relevant national authorities upon request.
The declaration must state that the high-risk AI system complies with the requirements in Section 2, and it must include the information specified in Annex V. It must be translated into a language that can be readily understood by the competent authorities of the Member States where the system is marketed or made available.
If the high-risk AI system is also subject to other Union harmonisation legislation that requires an EU declaration of conformity, a single declaration may cover all applicable Union law. This consolidated declaration must identify the harmonisation legislation to which it relates and include the information needed to identify that legislation.
By drafting the EU declaration of conformity, the provider assumes responsibility for compliance with Section 2 and must keep the declaration up to date as appropriate to reflect any changes.
The Commission may adopt delegated acts to amend Annex V, updating the content of the EU declaration of conformity to accommodate technical progress.

48.text:
1. The CE marking shall be subject to the general principles set out in Article 30 of Regulation (EC) No 765/2008.

2. For high-risk AI systems provided digitally, a digital CE marking shall be used, only if it can easily be accessed via the interface from which that system is accessed or via an easily accessible machine-readable code or other electronic means.

3. The CE marking shall be affixed visibly, legibly and indelibly for high-risk AI systems. Where that is not possible or not warranted on account of the nature of the high-risk AI system, it shall be affixed to the packaging or to the accompanying documentation, as appropriate.

4. Where applicable, the CE marking shall be followed by the identification number of the notified body responsible for the conformity assessment procedures set out in Article 43. The identification number of the notified body shall be affixed by the body itself or, under its instructions, by the provider or by the provider's authorised representative. The identification number shall also be indicated in any promotional material which mentions that the high-risk AI system fulfils the requirements for CE marking.

5. Where high-risk AI systems are subject to other Union law which also provides for the affixing of the CE marking, the CE marking shall indicate that the high-risk AI system also fulfil the requirements of that other law.

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

48.summary_1: CE marking requirements for high-risk AI systems are laid out, including a digital CE mark for digitally provided systems and rules on accessibility of the mark via interfaces or a machine-readable code. The marking must be visible, legible and indelible where feasible, and otherwise placed on packaging or accompanying documentation, with the notified body’s identification number included and echoed in promotional materials. If the system is also governed by other Union law requiring CE marking, the marking must indicate compliance with that law as well.
48.summary_2:
The CE marking for high-risk AI systems must comply with the general CE marking principles governing conformity assessment and marking.
For high-risk AI systems provided digitally, a digital CE marking is permitted and must be easily accessible via the interface from which the system is accessed or through an easily accessible machine-readable code or other electronic means.
The CE marking must be affixed visibly, legibly and indelibly for high-risk AI systems; if this is not possible or warranted by the nature of the system, it may be affixed to the packaging or to the accompanying documentation.
Where applicable, the CE marking must be followed by the identification number of the notified body responsible for the conformity assessment procedures; this number must be affixed by the body itself or by the provider or the provider's authorized representative under the body's instructions, and it must appear in any promotional material that references CE marking.
If the high-risk AI system is also subject to other Union law that requires a CE marking, the marking must indicate that the system also fulfils the requirements of that other law.

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

49.summary_1: Article 49 requires registration of high-risk AI systems and certain other AI systems in the EU database under Article 71, before market or use. It distinguishes between systems listed in Annex III (with some exceptions) and systems deemed non-high-risk under Article 6, with deployers also required to register and log use. For high-risk systems in law-enforcement, migration, asylum, and border control, registration occurs in a secure non-public section of the database with restricted data; those in Annex III point 2 are registered at national level.
49.summary_2:
For most high-risk AI systems listed in Annex III, providers or their authorized representatives must register themselves and their system in the EU database before placing the system on the market or putting it into service, with the exception of systems referred to in Annex III point 2.
- If the provider has concluded that the AI system is not high-risk under Article 6(3), that provider or their authorized representative must register themselves and the system in the EU database before market introduction or service.
- Before putting into service or using a high-risk AI system listed in Annex III, deployers that are public authorities, Union institutions, bodies, offices or agencies, or persons acting on their behalf, must register themselves, select the system, and register its use in the EU database.
- For high-risk AI systems in the areas of law enforcement, migration, asylum and border control management (Annex III points 1, 6 and 7), registration must occur in a secure non-public section of the EU database. It shall include only the specified information from Annex VIII and IX, and access to these restricted sections is limited to the Commission and national authorities under Article 74(8).
- High-risk AI systems referred to in Annex III point 2 must be registered at the national level.

50.unit_title: Transparency obligations for providers and deployers of certain AI systems
50.text:
1. Providers shall ensure that AI systems intended to interact directly with natural persons are designed and developed in such a way that the natural persons concerned are informed that they are interacting with an AI system, unless this is obvious from the point of view of a natural person who is reasonably well-informed, observant and circumspect, taking into account the circumstances and the context of use. This obligation shall not apply to AI systems authorised by law to detect, prevent, investigate or prosecute criminal offences, subject to appropriate safeguards for the rights and freedoms of third parties, unless those systems are available for the public to report a criminal offence.

2. Providers of AI systems, including general-purpose AI systems, generating synthetic audio, image, video or text content, shall ensure that the outputs of the AI system are marked in a machine-readable format and detectable as artificially generated or manipulated. Providers shall ensure their technical solutions are effective, interoperable, robust and reliable as far as this is technically feasible, taking into account the specificities and limitations of various types of content, the costs of implementation and the generally acknowledged state of the art, as may be reflected in relevant technical standards. This obligation shall not apply to the extent the AI systems perform an assistive function for standard editing or do not substantially alter the input data provided by the deployer or the semantics thereof, or where authorised by law to detect, prevent, investigate or prosecute criminal offences.

3. Deployers of an emotion recognition system or a biometric categorisation system shall inform the natural persons exposed thereto of the operation of the system, and shall process the personal data in accordance with Regulations (EU) 2016/679 and (EU) 2018/1725 and Directive (EU) 2016/680, as applicable. This obligation shall not apply to AI systems used for biometric categorisation and emotion recognition, which are permitted by law to detect, prevent or investigate criminal offences, subject to appropriate safeguards for the rights and freedoms of third parties, and in accordance with Union law.

4. Deployers of an AI system that generates or manipulates image, audio or video content constituting a deep fake, shall disclose that the content has been artificially generated or manipulated. This obligation shall not apply where the use is authorised by law to detect, prevent, investigate or prosecute criminal offence. Where the content forms part of an evidently artistic, creative, satirical, fictional or analogous work or programme, the transparency obligations set out in this paragraph are limited to disclosure of the existence of such generated or manipulated content in an appropriate manner that does not hamper the display or enjoyment of the work.
Deployers of an AI system that generates or manipulates text which is published with the purpose of informing the public on matters of public interest shall disclose that the text has been artificially generated or manipulated. This obligation shall not apply where the use is authorised by law to detect, prevent, investigate or prosecute criminal offences or where the AI-generated content has undergone a process of human review or editorial control and where a natural or legal person holds editorial responsibility for the publication of the content.

5. The information referred to in paragraphs 1 to 4 shall be provided to the natural persons concerned in a clear and distinguishable manner at the latest at the time of the first interaction or exposure. The information shall conform to the applicable accessibility requirements.

6. Paragraphs 1 to 4 shall not affect the requirements and obligations set out in Chapter III, and shall be without prejudice to other transparency obligations laid down in Union or national law for deployers of AI systems.

7. The AI Office shall encourage and facilitate the drawing up of codes of practice at Union level to facilitate the effective implementation of the obligations regarding the detection and labelling of artificially generated or manipulated content. The Commission may adopt implementing acts to approve those codes of practice in accordance with the procedure laid down in Article 56 (6). If it deems the code is not adequate, the Commission may adopt an implementing act specifying common rules for the implementation of those obligations in accordance with the examination procedure laid down in Article 98(2).

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

50.summary_1: Providers must inform users when they are interacting with an AI and mark outputs of synthetic media as AI-generated or manipulated, with lawful exceptions for crime-related uses. Deployers of emotion recognition or biometric categorisation must inform individuals and process data in line with GDPR and related union law, with exceptions for lawful criminal-offence purposes; deepfake content and AI-generated text intended for public-interest information must also be disclosed, subject to similar exceptions and contextual limits for artistic works. The disclosure must occur at first interaction in an accessible manner, the rules do not override Chapter III or other transparency duties, and the AI Office is tasked with promoting codes of practice while the Commission may adopt implementing acts to standardize these obligations.
50.summary_2:
Providers must ensure that individuals interacting directly with AI know they are engaging with an AI, unless it is obvious to a reasonably informed person from the context. This obligation does not apply to AI systems legally authorised to detect, prevent, investigate or prosecute criminal offences, provided appropriate safeguards for third parties' rights and freedoms are in place, and only where those systems are not publicly reportable.
Outputs of AI systems that generate synthetic audio, image, video or text must be marked in a machine-readable format and detectable as artificially generated or manipulated. Providers must implement technical solutions that are effective, interoperable, robust and reliable as far as feasible, considering content type, implementation costs and state-of-the-art standards. Exceptions apply where the system merely assists standard editing or does not substantially alter input data or where law authorises detection or prosecution of offences.
Deployers of emotion recognition or biometric categorisation systems must inform users about the operation of the system and process personal data in line with applicable EU data-protection laws. This obligation does not apply to systems used for biometric categorisation or emotion recognition that are legally permitted to detect, prevent or investigate criminal offences, provided safeguards for rights and freedoms and compliance with Union law are observed.
For content that generates or manipulates image, audio or video as a deep fake, deployers must disclose that the content is artificially generated or manipulated. This does not apply where law authorises the detection or investigation of offences. If the content is part of an evidently artistic, creative, satirical, fictional or analogous work, disclosure need only indicate the existence of such content in a manner that does not hinder display or enjoyment. For AI-generated text published to inform the public on matters of public interest, disclosure is required unless the use is legally authorised to detect/prevent offences, or where the content has undergone human review or editorial control and editorial responsibility rests with a natural or legal person.
The information required under these rules must be provided clearly and in a distinguishable manner at the latest at the first interaction or exposure, and must meet applicable accessibility requirements. These obligations do not alter Chapter III requirements or other Union or national transparency duties for deployers. The AI Office shall promote Union-level codes of practice to facilitate effective detection and labelling of artificially generated or manipulated content, with the Commission empowered to adopt implementing acts to approve such codes or, if inadequate, to establish common rules for their implementation through the examination procedure.

51.unit_title: Classification of general-purpose AI models as general-purpose AI models with systemic risk
51.text:
1. A general-purpose AI model shall be classified as a general-purpose AI model with systemic risk if it meets any of the following conditions:
(a) it has high impact capabilities evaluated on the basis of appropriate technical tools and methodologies, including indicators and benchmarks;
(b) based on a decision of the Commission, ex officio or following a qualified alert from the scientific panel, it has capabilities or an impact equivalent to those set out in point (a) having regard to the criteria set out in Annex XIII.

2. A general-purpose AI model shall be presumed to have high impact capabilities pursuant to paragraph 1, point (a), when the cumulative amount of computation used for its training measured in floating point operations is greater than 1025.

3. The Commission shall adopt delegated acts in accordance with Article 97 to amend the thresholds listed in paragraphs 1 and 2 of this Article, as well as to supplement benchmarks and indicators in light of evolving technological developments, such as algorithmic improvements or increased hardware efficiency, when necessary, for these thresholds to reflect the state of the art.

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

51.summary_1: Classifies a general-purpose AI model as having systemic risk if it possesses high impact capabilities per technical indicators and benchmarks, or if the Commission, ex officio or after a scientific panel alert, determines its impact is equivalent under Annex XIII. A presumption of high impact applies when the cumulative training compute measured in floating point operations exceeds 1025. The Commission must adopt delegated acts under Article 97 to amend these thresholds and to supplement benchmarks and indicators as technology evolves.
51.summary_2:
A general-purpose AI model is classified as having systemic risk if it (i) possesses high impact capabilities as assessed by appropriate technical tools and benchmarks, or (ii) is found by the Commission--either on its own initiative or following a qualified alert from the scientific panel--to have capabilities or impact equivalent to the high-impact criteria, applying the Annex XIII criteria.
A general-purpose AI model is presumed to have high impact capabilities under the first criterion when the cumulative training computation, measured in floating point operations, exceeds 1025.
The Commission may adopt delegated acts to amend the thresholds in paragraphs 1 and 2 and to supplement benchmarks and indicators in light of evolving technological developments, such as algorithmic improvements or increased hardware efficiency, so that the thresholds reflect the state of the art.

52.text:
1. Where a general-purpose AI model meets the condition referred to in Article 51(1), point (a), the relevant provider shall notify the Commission without delay and in any event within two weeks after that requirement is met or it becomes known that it will be met. That notification shall include the information necessary to demonstrate that the relevant requirement has been met. If the Commission becomes aware of a general-purpose AI model presenting systemic risks of which it has not been notified, it may decide to designate it as a model with systemic risk.

2. The provider of a general-purpose AI model that meets the condition referred to in Article 51(1), point (a), may present, with its notification, sufficiently substantiated arguments to demonstrate that, exceptionally, although it meets that requirement, the general-purpose AI model does not present, due to its specific characteristics, systemic risks and therefore should not be classified as a general-purpose AI model with systemic risk.

3. Where the Commission concludes that the arguments submitted pursuant to paragraph 2 are not sufficiently substantiated and the relevant provider was not able to demonstrate that the general-purpose AI model does not present, due to its specific characteristics, systemic risks, it shall reject those arguments, and the general-purpose AI model shall be considered to be a general-purpose AI model with systemic risk.

4. The Commission may designate a general-purpose AI model as presenting systemic risks, ex officio or following a qualified alert from the scientific panel pursuant to Article 90(1), point (a), on the basis of criteria set out in Annex XIII.
The Commission is empowered to adopt delegated acts in accordance with Article 97 in order to amend Annex XIII by specifying and updating the criteria set out in that Annex.

5. Upon a reasoned request of a provider whose model has been designated as a general-purpose AI model with systemic risk pursuant to paragraph 4, the Commission shall take the request into account and may decide to reassess whether the general-purpose AI model can still be considered to present systemic risks on the basis of the criteria set out in Annex XIII. Such a request shall contain objective, detailed and new reasons that have arisen since the designation decision. Providers may request reassessment at the earliest six months after the designation decision. Where the Commission, following its reassessment, decides to maintain the designation as a general-purpose AI model with systemic risk, providers may request reassessment at the earliest six months after that decision.

6. The Commission shall ensure that a list of general-purpose AI models with systemic risk is published and shall keep that list up to date, without prejudice to the need to observe and protect intellectual property rights and confidential business information or trade secrets in accordance with Union and national law.

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

52.summary_1: It requires providers to notify the Commission within two weeks when a general-purpose AI model meets the systemic-risk condition under Article 51(1)(a), and allows designation of such models if not properly notified. It lets providers submit substantiated arguments to avoid designation, but the Commission may reject them and designate the model as systemic-risk. It authorizes ex officio designation or after a scientific panel alert per Annex XIII, and enables delegated amendments to Annex XIII under Article 97, while requiring the Commission to publish and keep up to date a list of systemic-risk models.
52.summary_2:
Where a general-purpose AI model meets the condition triggering systemic-risk designation, the provider must notify the Commission without delay and in any case within two weeks, supplying information showing that the requirement is met. If the Commission learns of a systemic-risk model that was not notified, it may designate it as a model with systemic risk.
The provider may accompany the notification with sufficiently substantiated arguments to show, exceptionally, that despite meeting the requirement, the model does not pose systemic risks due to its specific characteristics and should not be classified as systemic-risk.
If the Commission finds the provider's arguments insufficient and the provider has not demonstrated that the model does not present systemic risks for its specific traits, it shall reject the arguments and the model shall be designated as a systemic-risk model.
A model may be designated as presenting systemic risks ex officio or after a qualified alert from the scientific panel, based on criteria set out in Annex XIII, with the Commission empowered to amend Annex XIII by delegated acts. Upon a reasoned request from a designated provider, the Commission must consider reassessment and may determine that the model no longer presents systemic risks according to Annex XIII; requests must be supported by objective, new reasons arising since designation, and may be made at six-month intervals after designation or after a previous reassessment if the designation is maintained.
The Commission must publish and keep up to date a list of systemic-risk models, while respecting intellectual property rights and confidential business information as required by Union and national law.

53.unit_title: Obligations for providers of general-purpose AI models
53.text:
1. Providers of general-purpose AI models shall:
(a) draw up and keep up-to-date the technical documentation of the model, including its training and testing process and the results of its evaluation, which shall contain, at a minimum, the information set out in Annex XI for the purpose of providing it, upon request, to the AI Office and the national competent authorities;
(b) draw up, keep up-to-date and make available information and documentation to providers of AI systems who intend to integrate the general-purpose AI model into their AI systems. Without prejudice to the need to observe and protect intellectual property rights and confidential business information or trade secrets in accordance with Union and national law, the information and documentation shall:
    (i) enable providers of AI systems to have a good understanding of the capabilities and limitations of the general-purpose AI model and to comply with their obligations pursuant to this Regulation; and
    (ii) contain, at a minimum, the elements set out in Annex XII;
(c) put in place a policy to comply with Union law on copyright and related rights, and in particular to identify and comply with, including through state-of-the-art technologies, a reservation of rights expressed pursuant to Article 4(3) of Directive (EU) 2019/790;
(d) draw up and make publicly available a sufficiently detailed summary about the content used for training of the general-purpose AI model, according to a template provided by the AI Office.

2. The obligations set out in paragraph 1, points (a) and (b), shall not apply to providers of AI models that are released under a free and open-source licence that allows for the access, usage, modification, and distribution of the model, and whose parameters, including the weights, the information on the model architecture, and the information on model usage, are made publicly available. This exception shall not apply to general-purpose AI models with systemic risks.

3. Providers of general-purpose AI models shall cooperate as necessary with the Commission and the national competent authorities in the exercise of their competences and powers pursuant to this Regulation.

4. Providers of general-purpose AI models may rely on codes of practice within the meaning of Article 56 to demonstrate compliance with the obligations set out in paragraph 1 of this Article, until a harmonised standard is published. Compliance with European harmonised standards grants providers the presumption of conformity to the extent that those standards cover those obligations. Providers of general-purpose AI models who do not adhere to an approved code of practice or do not comply with a European harmonised standard shall demonstrate alternative adequate means of compliance for assessment by the Commission.

5. For the purpose of facilitating compliance with Annex XI, in particular points 2 (d) and (e) thereof, the Commission is empowered to adopt delegated acts in accordance with Article 97 to detail measurement and calculation methodologies with a view to allowing for comparable and verifiable documentation.

6. The Commission is empowered to adopt delegated acts in accordance with Article 97(2) to amend Annexes XI and XII in light of evolving technological developments.

7. Any information or documentation obtained pursuant to this Article, including trade secrets, shall be treated in accordance with the confidentiality obligations set out in Article 78.

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

53.summary_1: Providers of general-purpose AI models must maintain up-to-date technical documentation (training, testing, and evaluation) per Annex XI and provide documentation to potential AI-system integrators per Annex XII to support compliance. They must adopt a copyright policy in line with Directive (EU) 2019/790 and publicly summarize the training data using a template from the AI Office, with an openness exception for open-source models unless systemic risks apply, and they must cooperate with the Commission and national authorities and may rely on codes of practice in Article 56 or harmonised standards, defaulting to alternative compliance if not. The Commission may adopt delegated acts (Article 97) to detail measurement methodologies and to amend Annexes XI and XII, and all obtained information—including trade secrets—remains confidential under Article 78.
53.summary_2:
Providers must maintain up-to-date technical documentation for the general-purpose AI model, including its training and testing processes and evaluation results, and provide this to the AI Office and national authorities on request (with the content aligned to Annex XI). They must also prepare, update, and share documentation with AI system providers who intend to integrate the model, containing at least the elements in Annex XII to help assess capabilities, limitations, and compliance. Additionally, they must implement a policy to ensure compliance with Union copyright and related rights, including reservations of rights under Directive 2019/790, and publicly provide a sufficiently detailed summary of training content using a template from the AI Office.
The obligations in the prior bullet points do not apply to general-purpose AI models released under a free and open-source licence that makes access, usage, modification, and distribution available and publicly discloses model parameters, architecture, and usage information, except for models with systemic risks.
Providers must cooperate with the Commission and national competent authorities as needed to exercise their powers under the Regulation.
They may rely on codes of practice (Article 56) to demonstrate compliance until a harmonised standard is published; compliance with European harmonised standards gives a presumption of conformity for the covered obligations, while providers who do not follow an approved code or standard must demonstrate alternative adequate means of compliance to the Commission.
The Commission may adopt delegated acts to detail measurement and calculation methodologies to support comparable and verifiable documentation (Annex XI), and to amend Annexes XI and XII in light of evolving technology. Any information or documentation obtained under this Article, including trade secrets, must be treated under the confidentiality obligations of Article 78.

54.unit_title: Authorised representatives of providers of general-purpose AI models

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

54.summary_1: Requires non-EU providers of general-purpose AI models to appoint a Union-based authorised representative via a written mandate, who can verify the provider’s compliance with the technical documentation and cooperate with the AI Office and national authorities. The mandate authorizes the representative to keep and provide the technical documentation for 10 years, supply information on request, and assist in regulatory actions, including when the model is integrated into systems placed on the market. It allows termination of the mandate if the provider breaches obligations and preserves an exemption for open-source models unless systemic risks are present.
54.summary_2:
Third-country providers of general-purpose AI models must appoint an EU-based authorised representative by written mandate before placing the model on the Union market. The mandate authorizes the representative to carry out the duties assigned by the provider and to act on its behalf for regulatory purposes.
The authorised representative is empowered to verify that the provider has drawn up the required technical documentation (Annex XI) and satisfied obligations under Articles 53 and 55 (and, where applicable, 55), to keep a copy of that documentation available to the AI Office and national authorities for 10 years after market release (including the provider's contact details), and to provide the AI Office with all information and documentation necessary to demonstrate compliance upon reasoned request. The representative must cooperate with the AI Office and competent authorities in any action related to the GP AI model, including when the model is integrated into other AI systems in the market or in service.
The mandate may also authorize the AI Office or competent authorities to address the authorised representative on all compliance-related issues, either in addition to or instead of the provider.
The authorised representative may terminate the mandate if it believes the provider is not fulfilling its obligations, and must immediately inform the AI Office of the termination and its reasons. The obligation to appoint an authorised representative does not apply to GP AI models released under a free/open-source license with publicly available parameters, architecture, and usage, unless those models present systemic risks.

55.unit_title: Obligations of providers of general-purpose AI models with systemic risk

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

55.summary_1: Providers of general-purpose AI models with systemic risk must perform standardized model evaluation (including adversarial testing), assess systemic risks at the Union level, and promptly report serious incidents to the AI Office and national authorities, while ensuring adequate cybersecurity for the model and its infrastructure. They may rely on codes of practice under Article 56 or on European harmonised standards to demonstrate compliance, with alternative adequate means of compliance if they do not follow a code or standard. All information obtained under this Article, including trade secrets, remains subject to the confidentiality obligations in Article 78.
55.summary_2:
Providers of general-purpose AI models with systemic risk must go beyond general obligations by conducting state-of-the-art evaluations using standardized protocols and tools, including adversarial testing, to identify and mitigate systemic risks; assess and mitigate systemic risks at the Union level from development to use; promptly record and report serious incidents and potential corrective measures to the AI Office and, where appropriate, national authorities; and ensure adequate cybersecurity for both the model and its underlying infrastructure.
They may rely on codes of practice to demonstrate compliance until a harmonised standard is published, and where such harmonised standards cover the obligations, they provide a presumption of conformity. If a provider does not follow an approved code of practice or harmonised standard, it must demonstrate alternative adequate means of compliance for assessment by the Commission.
All information or documentation obtained under this Article, including trade secrets, must be treated in accordance with the confidentiality obligations set out in Article 78.

56.text:
1. The AI Office shall encourage and facilitate the drawing up of codes of practice at Union level in order to contribute to the proper application of this Regulation, taking into account international approaches.

2. The AI Office and the Board shall aim to ensure that the codes of practice cover at least the obligations provided for in Articles 53 and 55, including the following issues:
(a) the means to ensure that the information referred to in Article 53(1), points (a) and (b), is kept up to date in light of market and technological developments;
(b) the adequate level of detail for the summary about the content used for training;
(c) the identification of the type and nature of the systemic risks at Union level, including their sources, where appropriate;
(d) the measures, procedures and modalities for the assessment and management of the systemic risks at Union level, including the documentation thereof, which shall be proportionate to the risks, take into consideration their severity and probability and take into account the specific challenges of tackling those risks in light of the possible ways in which such risks may emerge and materialise along the AI value chain.

3. The AI Office may invite all providers of general-purpose AI models, as well as relevant national competent authorities, to participate in the drawing-up of codes of practice. Civil society organisations, industry, academia and other relevant stakeholders, such as downstream providers and independent experts, may support the process.

4. The AI Office and the Board shall aim to ensure that the codes of practice clearly set out their specific objectives and contain commitments or measures, including key performance indicators as appropriate, to ensure the achievement of those objectives, and that they take due account of the needs and interests of all interested parties, including affected persons, at Union level.

5. The AI Office shall aim to ensure that participants to the codes of practice report regularly to the AI Office on the implementation of the commitments and the measures taken and their outcomes, including as measured against the key performance indicators as appropriate. Key performance indicators and reporting commitments shall reflect differences in size and capacity between various participants.

6. The AI Office and the Board shall regularly monitor and evaluate the achievement of the objectives of the codes of practice by the participants and their contribution to the proper application of this Regulation. The AI Office and the Board shall assess whether the codes of practice cover the obligations provided for in Articles 53 and 55, and shall regularly monitor and evaluate the achievement of their objectives. They shall publish their assessment of the adequacy of the codes of practice.
The Commission may, by way of an implementing act, approve a code of practice and give it a general validity within the Union. That implementing act shall be adopted in accordance with the examination procedure referred to in Article 98(2).

7. The AI Office may invite all providers of general-purpose AI models to adhere to the codes of practice. For providers of general-purpose AI models not presenting systemic risks this adherence may be limited to the obligations provided for in Article 53, unless they declare explicitly their interest to join the full code.

8. The AI Office shall, as appropriate, also encourage and facilitate the review and adaptation of the codes of practice, in particular in light of emerging standards. The AI Office shall assist in the assessment of available standards.

9. Codes of practice shall be ready at the latest by 2 May 2025. The AI Office shall take the necessary steps, including inviting providers pursuant to paragraph 7.
If, by 2 August 2025, a code of practice cannot be finalised, or if the AI Office deems it is not adequate following its assessment under paragraph 6 of this Article, the Commission may provide, by means of implementing acts, common rules for the implementation of the obligations provided for in Articles 53 and 55, including the issues set out in paragraph 2 of this Article. Those implementing acts shall be adopted in accordance with the examination procedure referred to in Article 98(2).

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

56.summary_1: Calls on the AI Office to facilitate Union-level codes of practice to support the Regulation’s proper application, taking international approaches into account. It requires codes of practice to cover the obligations in Articles 53 and 55, detailing information updates, training-data summaries, systemic risk identification and management, and proportionate risk documentation and procedures. It also sets participation rules, reporting and KPI requirements, ongoing monitoring, and a timeline that may lead to Commission implementing acts if codes are not finalised by the specified dates.
56.summary_2:
The AI Office is tasked with fostering EU-level codes of practice to support the proper application of the Regulation, while aligning with international approaches. These codes are intended to guide the implementation of the obligations for providers of general-purpose AI models and related risk governance.
The codes must cover core obligations, including keeping information updated in light of market and technological developments; providing a sufficiently detailed summary of training data; identifying systemic risks at the Union level and their sources; and setting out proportionate measures, procedures, and documentation for assessment and management of those systemic risks across the AI value chain.
Participation in developing the codes is open: the AI Office may invite providers of general-purpose AI models, national authorities, civil society, industry, academia, downstream providers, and independent experts to contribute or support. Codes should clearly state objectives and include commitments or measures with appropriate key performance indicators, ensuring consideration of the needs and interests of all interested parties at the Union level.
Ongoing governance requires regular reporting by participants on implementation, outcomes, and KPI-based performance, with recognition of size and capacity differences. The AI Office and the Board will monitor and evaluate progress, assess whether the codes satisfy the obligations for providers of general-purpose AI models, and publish assessments of the codes' adequacy. The Commission may adopt implementing acts to grant a code general Union validity, following the prescribed procedure.
Regarding adherence and evolution, the AI Office may invite GP model providers to adhere to the codes, with non-systemic-risk models limited to the information obligations unless they opt into the full code. The Office will pursue review and adaptation of the codes in light of emerging standards and assist with standards assessment.
