A deadline of 2 May 2025 is set for ready codes, with potential invitations to participate; if a code is not finalized or deemed inadequate by 2 August 2025, the Commission may enact common rules through implementing acts to implement the obligations, including the topics outlined in paragraph 2, under the required procedural framework.

57.text:
1. Member States shall ensure that their competent authorities establish at least one AI regulatory sandbox at national level, which shall be operational by 2 August 2026. That sandbox may also be established jointly with the competent authorities of other Member States. The Commission may provide technical support, advice and tools for the establishment and operation of AI regulatory sandboxes.
The obligation under the first subparagraph may also be fulfilled by participating in an existing sandbox in so far as that participation provides an equivalent level of national coverage for the participating Member States.

2. Additional AI regulatory sandboxes at regional or local level, or established jointly with the competent authorities of other Member States may also be established.

3. The European Data Protection Supervisor may also establish an AI regulatory sandbox for Union institutions, bodies, offices and agencies, and may exercise the roles and the tasks of national competent authorities in accordance with this Chapter.

4. Member States shall ensure that the competent authorities referred to in paragraphs 1 and 2 allocate sufficient resources to comply with this Article effectively and in a timely manner. Where appropriate, national competent authorities shall cooperate with other relevant authorities, and may allow for the involvement of other actors within the AI ecosystem. This Article shall not affect other regulatory sandboxes established under Union or national law. Member States shall ensure an appropriate level of cooperation between the authorities supervising those other sandboxes and the national competent authorities.

5. AI regulatory sandboxes established under paragraph 1 shall provide for a controlled environment that fosters innovation and facilitates the development, training, testing and validation of innovative AI systems for a limited time before their being placed on the market or put into service pursuant to a specific sandbox plan agreed between the providers or prospective providers and the competent authority. Such sandboxes may include testing in real world conditions supervised therein.

6. Competent authorities shall provide, as appropriate, guidance, supervision and support within the AI regulatory sandbox with a view to identifying risks, in particular to fundamental rights, health and safety, testing, mitigation measures, and their effectiveness in relation to the obligations and requirements of this Regulation and, where relevant, other Union and national law supervised within the sandbox.

7. Competent authorities shall provide providers and prospective providers participating in the AI regulatory sandbox with guidance on regulatory expectations and how to fulfil the requirements and obligations set out in this Regulation.
Upon request of the provider or prospective provider of the AI system, the competent authority shall provide a written proof of the activities successfully carried out in the sandbox. The competent authority shall also provide an exit report detailing the activities carried out in the sandbox and the related results and learning outcomes. Providers may use such documentation to demonstrate their compliance with this Regulation through the conformity assessment process or relevant market surveillance activities. In this regard, the exit reports and the written proof provided by the national competent authority shall be taken positively into account by market surveillance authorities and notified bodies, with a view to accelerating conformity assessment procedures to a reasonable extent.

8. Subject to the confidentiality provisions in Article 78, and with the agreement of the provider or prospective provider, the Commission and the Board shall be authorised to access the exit reports and shall take them into account, as appropriate, when exercising their tasks under this Regulation. If both the provider or prospective provider and the national competent authority explicitly agree, the exit report may be made publicly available through the single information platform referred to in this Article.

9. The establishment of AI regulatory sandboxes shall aim to contribute to the following objectives:
(a) improving legal certainty to achieve regulatory compliance with this Regulation or, where relevant, other applicable Union and national law;
(b) supporting the sharing of best practices through cooperation with the authorities involved in the AI regulatory sandbox;
(c) fostering innovation and competitiveness and facilitating the development of an AI ecosystem;
(d) contributing to evidence-based regulatory learning;
(e) facilitating and accelerating access to the Union market for AI systems, in particular when provided by SMEs, including start-ups.

10. National competent authorities shall ensure that, to the extent the innovative AI systems involve the processing of personal data or otherwise fall under the supervisory remit of other national authorities or competent authorities providing or supporting access to data, the national data protection authorities and those other national or competent authorities are associated with the operation of the AI regulatory sandbox and involved in the supervision of those aspects to the extent of their respective tasks and powers.

11. The AI regulatory sandboxes shall not affect the supervisory or corrective powers of the competent authorities supervising the sandboxes, including at regional or local level. Any significant risks to health and safety and fundamental rights identified during the development and testing of such AI systems shall result in an adequate mitigation. National competent authorities shall have the power to temporarily or permanently suspend the testing process, or the participation in the sandbox if no effective mitigation is possible, and shall inform the AI Office of such decision. National competent authorities shall exercise their supervisory powers within the limits of the relevant law, using their discretionary powers when implementing legal provisions in respect of a specific AI regulatory sandbox project, with the objective of supporting innovation in AI in the Union.

12. Providers and prospective providers participating in the AI regulatory sandbox shall remain liable under applicable Union and national liability law for any damage inflicted on third parties as a result of the experimentation taking place in the sandbox. However, provided that the prospective providers observe the specific plan and the terms and conditions for their participation and follow in good faith the guidance given by the national competent authority, no administrative fines shall be imposed by the authorities for infringements of this Regulation. Where other competent authorities responsible for other Union and national law were actively involved in the supervision of the AI system in the sandbox and provided guidance for compliance, no administrative fines shall be imposed regarding that law.

13. The AI regulatory sandboxes shall be designed and implemented in such a way that, where relevant, they facilitate cross-border cooperation between national competent authorities.

14. National competent authorities shall coordinate their activities and cooperate within the framework of the Board.

15. National competent authorities shall inform the AI Office and the Board of the establishment of a sandbox, and may ask them for support and guidance. The AI Office shall make publicly available a list of planned and existing sandboxes and keep it up to date in order to encourage more interaction in the AI regulatory sandboxes and cross-border cooperation.

16. National competent authorities shall submit annual reports to the AI Office and to the Board, from one year after the establishment of the AI regulatory sandbox and every year thereafter until its termination, and a final report. Those reports shall provide information on the progress and results of the implementation of those sandboxes, including best practices, incidents, lessons learnt and recommendations on their setup and, where relevant, on the application and possible revision of this Regulation, including its delegated and implementing acts, and on the application of other Union law supervised by the competent authorities within the sandbox. The national competent authorities shall make those annual reports or abstracts thereof available to the public, online. The Commission shall, where appropriate, take the annual reports into account when exercising its tasks under this Regulation.

17. The Commission shall develop a single and dedicated interface containing all relevant information related to AI regulatory sandboxes to allow stakeholders to interact with AI regulatory sandboxes and to raise enquiries with competent authorities, and to seek non-binding guidance on the conformity of innovative products, services, business models embedding AI technologies, in accordance with Article 62(1), point (c). The Commission shall proactively coordinate with national competent authorities, where relevant.

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

57.summary_1: Member States must establish at least one AI regulatory sandbox at the national level by 2 August 2026, with the option of regional or cross-border setups. The framework sets governance, supervision, and reporting requirements, including providing guidance to providers, exit reports, liability rules, and involvement of data protection authorities to address fundamental rights and data protection. It also requires annual reports to the AI Office and the Board, coordination for cross-border activities, and a centralized information platform to facilitate interaction, guidance, and faster conformity assessment.
57.summary_2:
Member States must ensure at least one AI regulatory sandbox at national level, operational by 2 August 2026, and may establish them jointly with other Member States. They may also create additional sandboxes at regional or local levels, or jointly with others, and the Commission may provide technical support, advice and tools. Participation in an existing sandbox is permissible if it provides equivalent national coverage for the participating States.
The European Data Protection Supervisor may establish a sandbox for Union institutions and may exercise the roles and tasks of national competent authorities where appropriate. Member States must allocate sufficient resources and cooperate with other relevant authorities, with the possible involvement of other actors within the AI ecosystem. This does not affect other sandboxes under Union or national law, and there must be appropriate cooperation between the authorities supervising those other sandboxes and the national competent authorities.
Sandboxes provide a controlled environment to foster innovation and enable development, testing and validation of innovative AI systems for a limited time prior to market deployment, potentially including real-world testing under supervision. Competent authorities offer guidance, supervision and support to identify risks--such as fundamental rights, health and safety, testing and mitigation measures--and to assess their effectiveness in relation to this Regulation and relevant Union and national law.
Providers and prospective providers receive guidance on regulatory expectations and how to comply. On request, they may obtain written proof of sandbox activities and an exit report detailing activities, results and learning outcomes, which can support conformity assessments and may be taken into account by market surveillance authorities and notified bodies to accelerate procedures. Subject to confidentiality and provider agreement, the Commission and the Board may access exit reports and may publish them on the single information platform with the provider's consent.
The sandboxes aim to improve legal certainty, share best practices, foster innovation and competitiveness, enable evidence-based regulatory learning, and facilitate faster access to the Union market for AI systems, particularly from SMEs and startups. If processing of personal data or other supervision involves other authorities, those authorities must be associated with the sandbox to the extent of their powers. Supervision remains with the relevant authorities; significant risks require mitigation, and testing or participation may be suspended if effective mitigation is not possible. Providers remain liable under liability law for damages; however, compliance with the plan and guidance can prevent administrative fines, and involvement of other authorities means no fines under those laws. The process supports cross-border cooperation, with national authorities coordinating within the Board, informing the AI Office and the Board of sandbox establishment and progress, and with the Commission maintaining a single interface to coordinate information and guidance for stakeholders.

58.unit_title: Detailed arrangements for, and functioning of, AI regulatory sandboxes

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

58.summary_1: The article requires the Commission to adopt implementing acts that specify the detailed arrangements for the establishment, development, operation, and supervision of AI regulatory sandboxes, including eligibility, application processes, monitoring, and exit details. It mandates open, transparent, and non-discriminatory access for providers (including SMEs and start-ups), ensures timely decisions within three months, allows partnerships, and aims for simple, Union-wide procedures to prevent fragmentation. It also emphasizes free access for SMEs, supports learning outcomes to aid conformity assessment and voluntary codes of conduct, promotes involvement of other actors such as notified bodies and European Digital Innovation Hubs, and sets safeguards for real-world testing to protect fundamental rights and safety across the Union.
58.summary_2:
The Commission must adopt implementing acts that set out the detailed arrangements for establishing, developing, operating, and supervising AI regulatory sandboxes, including common criteria for eligibility and selection, the processes for applying, participating, monitoring, exiting, and terminating a sandbox, and the terms governing participants (including the sandbox plan and exit report). These acts are to be produced using the procedure specified in Article 98(2).
The implementing acts must ensure open and fair access to sandboxes for any applying AI provider that meets the criteria, with transparent decisions issued within three months; broad and equal access, including permitting partnerships with deployers or third parties; and flexibility for national authorities to tailor sandbox operations. They must also ensure access is free of charge for SMEs and startups, subject to exceptional, fairly recovered costs, and that the sandbox supports compliance with conformity assessment obligations and the voluntary use of codes of conduct. The acts should promote collaboration with other ecosystem actors (notified bodies, standardisation bodies, SMEs, testing facilities, EU Digital Innovation Hubs, centers of excellence, researchers) and harmonise administrative requirements across the Union to avoid fragmentation, with mutual recognition of sandboxes across Member States and by the European Data Protection Supervisor, and include a limited duration appropriate to project scope, potentially extendable by the competent authority. They should facilitate the development of testing, benchmarking, and assessment tools for AI dimensions relevant to regulation (e.g., accuracy, robustness, cybersecurity) and for mitigating risks to fundamental rights and society.
Prospective providers, especially SMEs and startups, should be directed to pre-deployment services related to implementing the regulation, as well as additional value-added services such as guidance on standardisation and certification, access to testing facilities, and links to European Digital Innovation Hubs and centers of excellence.
If authorities authorize real-world testing within an AI regulatory sandbox, they must agree the terms, safeguards, and conditions with participants to protect fundamental rights, health, and safety, and coordinate with other national authorities to ensure consistent practices across the Union where appropriate.

59.unit_title: Further processing of personal data for developing certain AI systems in the public interest in the AI regulatory sandbox

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

59.summary_1: Within the AI regulatory sandbox, personal data lawfully collected for other purposes may be processed solely to develop, train, and test certain AI systems when the activity serves a substantial public interest (e.g., public safety, environment, energy sustainability, transport resilience, or efficient public administration) and is necessary to meet requirements that cannot be fulfilled by anonymised or non-personal data. Processing must occur in a functionally separate, protected environment, be limited to authorized personnel, keep processing logs, delete data after the sandbox ends, and not be shared outside the sandbox; a detailed technical description and a short project summary must be published. For law-enforcement purposes, processing must be based on specific Union or national law and remain subject to the same safeguards and monitoring to identify and mitigate risks to data subjects' rights.
59.summary_2:
In the AI regulatory sandbox, personal data lawfully collected for other purposes may be processed solely to develop, train, and test certain AI systems when the processing serves a substantial public interest and the projects fall within areas such as public safety/health, environmental protection and climate action, energy sustainability, transport and critical infrastructure safety, or improved public administration. The data must also be necessary to meet specific legal requirements that cannot be satisfied by anonymized or non-personal data alone.
The processing may proceed only if there are effective monitoring mechanisms to detect risks to data subjects' rights and freedoms and response measures to mitigate or stop processing if needed. Data must be processed in a functionally separate, isolated environment under the provider's control, with access restricted to authorized personnel. Data may be shared further only under Union data protection law, and any data created during the sandbox cannot be shared outside of it. Processing must not result in measures or decisions about data subjects or otherwise undermine their data-protection rights, and appropriate technical and organizational safeguards must be applied, with deletion after the sandbox ends or the data retention period expires. Processing logs must be kept for the duration of participation, and a complete description of the training/testing/validation process and its rationale must be included in the technical documentation.
A short project summary, including objectives and expected results, must be published on the competent authorities' website, excluding sensitive operational data related to law enforcement, border control, immigration or asylum activities. The technical documentation requirement also ensures that testing results are documented and traceable.
In the context of criminal offences or penalties, data processing in AI sandboxes must be based on specific Union or national law and still meet the same cumulative conditions as above. This provision does not limit other Union or national laws that exclude processing for other purposes or that provide other valid legal bases for processing necessary to develop, test, or train innovative AI systems, provided such processing remains compliant with Union data protection law.

60.unit_title: Testing of high-risk AI systems in real world conditions outside AI regulatory sandboxes

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

60.summary_1: Article 60 allows high-risk AI systems to be tested in real-world conditions outside sandboxes, subject to a real-world testing plan approved by a market surveillance authority and registered with a Union-wide ID. Tests must be time-limited, may involve deployers, require informed consent where possible, protect data and vulnerable groups, enable recall and oversight, and trigger incident reporting and liability provisions. The Commission may specify detailed plan elements by implementing acts, and testing remains subject to applicable Union and national laws, including product-harmonization rules.
60.summary_2:
Testing of high-risk AI systems in real-world conditions outside AI regulatory sandboxes is permitted for listed high-risk systems, subject to a real-world testing plan and this Article, and without prejudice to the prohibitions in Article 5. The Commission will specify the plan's detailed elements by implementing acts adopted through the procedure in Article 98(2). This regime coexists with Union or national product-testing laws under harmonisation legislation listed in Annex I.
Testing may occur at any time before market placement or service start, either by the provider alone or with one or more deployers or prospective deployers. Tests must be conducted in accordance with the real-world testing plan and approved by the national market surveillance authority, and ethical reviews under Union or national law remain applicable. Before testing, providers must satisfy a set of conditions: submit the plan to the competent market surveillance authority, obtain its approval (or, after 30 days of no response, tacit approval unless tacit approval is not recognised by national law), register the testing with a Union-wide unique ID and the Annex IX information (with certain limited non-public registrations for specified law enforcement/migration/border-control categories), establish Union domicile or EU representation, ensure lawful data transfers with appropriate safeguards, and cap the testing duration at six months (extendable by up to six months with prior notice and justification). Testing must protect vulnerable groups, involve deployers with proper briefing and use of instructions, and be conducted under agreements outlining roles and compliance obligations; testing results must be reversible or disregarded when appropriate, and informed consent under Article 61 is generally required (with law-enforcement exceptions where consent would undermine testing and where data may be deleted after the test).
Tests must be overseen by qualified personnel representing the provider and any deployers, and the system's predictions or decisions must be reversible or disregarded where needed. Subjects can withdraw at any time by revoking consent without penalty, and their personal data must be deleted promptly; withdrawals do not affect ongoing activities already underway. Market surveillance authorities have powers to require information, conduct unannounced inspections, and verify compliance with testing requirements. Any serious incidents identified during testing must be reported to the national market surveillance authority with immediate mitigation or suspension measures, and the testing may be suspended or terminated as needed, with a recall procedure established upon termination.
Upon suspension or termination, providers must notify the national market surveillance authority of the outcome. They remain liable under applicable Union and national liability regimes for any damage caused during testing.

61.unit_title: Informed consent to participate in testing in real world conditions outside AI regulatory sandboxes

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

61.summary_1: Requires freely given informed consent from participants before real-world testing outside AI regulatory sandboxes, with clear information about the test purpose, conditions, and expected duration, as well as the right to refuse or withdraw at any time. It also covers provisions to request reversal or disregard of the AI system’s predictions, and the use and disclosure of a Union-wide unique testing ID, with the consent dated, documented, and a copy provided to the participant or their legal representative.
61.summary_2:
Freely-given informed consent is required before participating in real-world testing outside AI regulatory sandboxes. Participants must be informed with concise, clear, relevant, and understandable information about: the nature and objectives of the testing and any potential inconvenience; the testing conditions and expected duration; their rights and guarantees, including the right to refuse participation and to withdraw at any time without detriment or justification; how to request reversal or disregard of the AI system's predictions or decisions; and the testing's Union-wide unique ID and the provider's or legal representative's contact details.
The consent must be dated and documented, and a copy must be provided to the participant or their legal representative.

62.unit_title: Measures for providers and deployers, in particular SMEs, including start-ups

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

62.summary_1: Member States must give SMEs and start-ups priority access to AI regulatory sandboxes and provide tailored awareness, training, and communication channels to support their participation and interaction with standardisation processes. SME-specific considerations must be applied to conformity assessment fees, with reductions proportional to size and market indicators. The AI Office must provide standardised templates, maintain a Union-wide information platform, run awareness campaigns, and promote convergence of best practices in public procurement for AI systems.
62.summary_2:
Member States must implement measures benefiting SMEs and start-ups, including those with a registered office or branch in the Union, by giving them priority access to AI regulatory sandboxes (without excluding other SMEs/start-ups that meet eligibility and selection criteria); they must also provide targeted awareness, training, and guidance, establish or use dedicated communication channels for SMEs, deployers, and local authorities, and facilitate SMEs' participation in standardisation processes.
- The fees charged for conformity assessment under Article 43 must reflect SME interests, with reductions proportionate to an SME's size, market size, and other relevant indicators, ensuring costs are more proportionate to their scale.
- The AI Office must provide standardised templates for areas covered by the Regulation; maintain a single, user-friendly information platform for operators across the Union; run awareness-raising campaigns about the Regulation's obligations; and promote convergence of best practices in public procurement procedures related to AI systems.
- In addition, the AI Office is tasked with evaluating and promoting harmonised procurement practices to streamline how AI systems are acquired by public authorities, supporting consistency and predictability in public-sector AI procurements.

63.text:
1. Microenterprises within the meaning of Recommendation 2003/361/EC may comply with certain elements of the quality management system required by Article 17 of this Regulation in a simplified manner, provided that they do not have partner enterprises or linked enterprises within the meaning of that Recommendation. For that purpose, the Commission shall develop guidelines on the elements of the quality management system which may be complied with in a simplified manner considering the needs of microenterprises, without affecting the level of protection or the need for compliance with the requirements in respect of high-risk AI systems.

2. Paragraph 1 of this Article shall not be interpreted as exempting those operators from fulfilling any other requirements or obligations laid down in this Regulation, including those established in Articles 9, 10, 11, 12, 13, 14, 15, 72 and 73.

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

63.summary_1: Microenterprises, as defined in Recommendation 2003/361/EC, may satisfy a simplified subset of Article 17's quality management requirements, provided they have no partner or linked enterprises, with the Commission to issue guidelines on which elements can be simplified without reducing protection for high-risk AI systems. This derogation does not exempt these operators from other obligations under the Regulation. They remain subject to Articles 9–15 and 72–73.
63.summary_2:
Microenterprises meeting the definition in Recommendation 2003/361/EC may implement a simplified portion of the quality management system that accompanies high-risk AI systems, provided they have no partner or linked enterprises as defined in that Recommendation. The Commission will issue guidelines identifying which elements may be simplified, tailored to microenterprises' needs, while preserving protection levels and maintaining compliance with the high-risk AI requirements.
This simplified approach is not a blanket exemption from other obligations under the Regulation. Microenterprises remain subject to all other applicable requirements, including risk management, data management, documentation, transparency, human oversight, and post-market monitoring and incident reporting set out in Articles 9-15, 72, and 73.

64.text:
1. The Commission shall develop Union expertise and capabilities in the field of AI through the AI Office.

2. Member States shall facilitate the tasks entrusted to the AI Office, as reflected in this Regulation.

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

64.summary_1: This article designates the AI Office as the mechanism through which the Commission develops Union-level expertise and capabilities in AI. It further obligates Member States to facilitate tasks entrusted to the AI Office, as reflected in this Regulation. Together, the provisions establish governance and collaboration to advance EU AI capabilities.
64.summary_2: The Commission is required to develop Union-wide expertise and capabilities in artificial intelligence through the AI Office. Member States are obligated to facilitate the AI Office's entrusted tasks as set out in the Regulation.

65.unit_title: Establishment and structure of the European Artificial Intelligence Board
65.text:
1. A European Artificial Intelligence Board (the 'Board') is hereby established.

2. The Board shall be composed of one representative per Member State. The European Data Protection Supervisor shall participate as observer. The AI Office shall also attend the Board's meetings, without taking part in the votes. Other national and Union authorities, bodies or experts may be invited to the meetings by the Board on a case by case basis, where the issues discussed are of relevance for them.

3. Each representative shall be designated by their Member State for a period of three years, renewable once.

4. Member States shall ensure that their representatives on the Board:
(a) have the relevant competences and powers in their Member State so as to contribute actively to the achievement of the Board's tasks referred to in Article 66;
(b) are designated as a single contact point vis-à-vis the Board and, where appropriate, taking into account Member States' needs, as a single contact point for stakeholders;
(c) are empowered to facilitate consistency and coordination between national competent authorities in their Member State as regards the implementation of this Regulation, including through the collection of relevant data and information for the purpose of fulfilling their tasks on the Board.

5. The designated representatives of the Member States shall adopt the Board's rules of procedure by a two-thirds majority. The rules of procedure shall, in particular, lay down procedures for the selection process, the duration of the mandate of, and specifications of the tasks of, the Chair, detailed arrangements for voting, and the organisation of the Board's activities and those of its sub-groups.

6. The Board shall establish two standing sub-groups to provide a platform for cooperation and exchange among market surveillance authorities and notifying authorities about issues related to market surveillance and notified bodies respectively.
The standing sub-group for market surveillance should act as the administrative cooperation group (ADCO) for this Regulation within the meaning of Article 30 of Regulation (EU) 2019/1020.
The Board may establish other standing or temporary sub-groups as appropriate for the purpose of examining specific issues. Where appropriate, representatives of the advisory forum referred to in Article 67 may be invited to such sub-groups or to specific meetings of those subgroups as observers.

7. The Board shall be organised and operated so as to safeguard the objectivity and impartiality of its activities.

8. The Board shall be chaired by one of the representatives of the Member States. The AI Office shall provide the secretariat for the Board, convene the meetings upon request of the Chair, and prepare the agenda in accordance with the tasks of the Board pursuant to this Regulation and its rules of procedure.

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

65.summary_1: Establishes the European Artificial Intelligence Board, composed of one representative per Member State with the European Data Protection Supervisor as an observer and the AI Office serving as the secretariat and meeting organizer. Representatives are appointed for three-year terms (renewable once) and must have the necessary competences to contribute to the Board's tasks referred to in Article 66, act as a single national contact point, and facilitate coordination among national authorities, with the Board adopting its rules of procedure by a two-thirds majority. It creates two standing sub-groups for market surveillance and for notifying authorities (the ADCO group under Article 30 of Regulation (EU) 2019/1020), may form additional sub-groups, may invite the advisory forum from Article 67 as observers, and is organized to safeguard objectivity, chaired by a Member State representative with the AI Office providing the secretariat.
65.summary_2:
The European Artificial Intelligence Board is established to facilitate the consistent and effective application of the Regulation, with its work geared toward supporting the Commission and Member States in implementing Article 66 tasks. The Board has one representative from each Member State; the European Data Protection Supervisor participates as an observer, and the AI Office attends meetings without voting rights. Other national or Union authorities, bodies or experts may be invited as needed on a case-by-case basis when issues are relevant to them.
Member States must ensure that their representatives have the appropriate competences and powers to contribute to the Board's tasks, are designated as the single contact point for the Board and stakeholders, and are empowered to promote consistency and coordination among their national competent authorities, including collecting relevant data and information for the Board's work.
The designated representatives shall adopt the Board's rules of procedure by a two-thirds majority, which must specify the selection process, the Chair's mandate and responsibilities, voting arrangements, and the organization of the Board and its sub-groups. The Board shall establish two standing sub-groups to coordinate market surveillance authorities and notifying authorities related to market surveillance and notified bodies; the market surveillance sub-group functions as the ADCO for this Regulation under Regulation (EU) 2019/1020. The Board may create additional standing or temporary sub-groups, and the advisory forum may, where appropriate, participate as observers.
The Board must be organized and operated to safeguard objectivity and impartiality. It is chaired by a representative of the Member States, while the AI Office provides the secretariat, convenes meetings at the Chair's request, and prepares the agenda in line with the Board's tasks and procedural rules.

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

66.summary_1: The Board is tasked with advising and assisting the Commission and Member States to achieve a consistent and effective application of the Regulation. Its functions include coordinating national competent authorities, sharing technical and regulatory expertise, advising on enforcement and implementation (including general-purpose AI models), harmonising administrative practices, issuing recommendations and opinions, promoting AI literacy, and developing common criteria, benchmarks, and cross‑border cooperation with EU institutions and international partners. It also supports the development of AI regulatory sandboxes, training, and guidance, and it addresses potential amendments to Annex III or Article 5 in relation to harmonised standards and technical specifications.
66.summary_2:
The Board's core role is to advise and assist the Commission and Member States to ensure a consistent and effective application of the Regulation. It may coordinate among national competent authorities, support joint market surveillance activities, collect and share technical and regulatory expertise and best practices, advise on implementation--especially regarding general-purpose AI models--and harmonise administrative practices, including derogations from conformity assessment, AI regulatory sandboxes, and testing in real-world conditions.
It may issue recommendations and written opinions on matters related to implementation, including codes of conduct and the Commission's guidelines; evaluation and review of the Regulation, including serious incident reports and the functioning of the EU database, and the preparation of delegated or implementing acts; alignment with Union harmonisation legislation; and considerations on technical specifications, use of harmonised standards or common specifications, and trends affecting European competitiveness, AI uptake, and accountability. It may also advise on potential amendments to high-risk AI use-cases and related provisions as evidence and developments warrant.
The Board supports the development of standards and governance by providing input on technical specifications, existing standards, and the use of harmonised standards or common specifications; it contributes to the development of benchmarks and a shared understanding of key concepts among market operators and authorities; and it advises on the evolution of the regulation in light of technological advances, including the AI value chains and accountability implications.
In addition, the Board promotes AI literacy, public awareness of benefits, risks, safeguards and rights, and assists in developing guidance documents. It facilitates cooperation with other EU institutions, bodies, and international partners, supports the AI Office in establishing and linking AI regulatory sandboxes, and helps assess training needs for staff. It may provide opinions on qualified alerts regarding general-purpose AI models and receive Member State opinions on such alerts and national experiences with monitoring and enforcement, particularly for systems integrating general-purpose AI models.

67.text:
1. An advisory forum shall be established to provide technical expertise and advise the Board and the Commission, and to contribute to their tasks under this Regulation.

2. The membership of the advisory forum shall represent a balanced selection of stakeholders, including industry, start-ups, SMEs, civil society and academia. The membership of the advisory forum shall be balanced with regard to commercial and non-commercial interests and, within the category of commercial interests, with regard to SMEs and other undertakings.

3. The Commission shall appoint the members of the advisory forum, in accordance with the criteria set out in paragraph 2, from amongst stakeholders with recognised expertise in the field of AI.

4. The term of office of the members of the advisory forum shall be two years, which may be extended by up to no more than four years.

5. The Fundamental Rights Agency, ENISA, the European Committee for Standardization (CEN), the European Committee for Electrotechnical Standardization (CENELEC), and the European Telecommunications Standards Institute (ETSI) shall be permanent members of the advisory forum.

6. The advisory forum shall draw up its rules of procedure. It shall elect two co-chairs from among its members, in accordance with criteria set out in paragraph 2. The term of office of the co-chairs shall be two years, renewable once.

7. The advisory forum shall hold meetings at least twice a year. The advisory forum may invite experts and other stakeholders to its meetings.

8. The advisory forum may prepare opinions, recommendations and written contributions at the request of the Board or the Commission.

9. The advisory forum may establish standing or temporary sub-groups as appropriate for the purpose of examining specific questions related to the objectives of this Regulation.

10. The advisory forum shall prepare an annual report on its activities. That report shall be made publicly available.

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

67.summary_1: An advisory forum is established to provide technical expertise to the Board and the Commission and to contribute to tasks under the Regulation. Its membership must be a balanced representation of industry, startups and SMEs, civil society, and academia, with permanent participation by the Fundamental Rights Agency, ENISA, CEN, CENELEC, and ETSI. It is empowered to draw up rules of procedure, elect two co-chairs, meet at least twice a year, form standing or temporary sub-groups, and issue opinions, recommendations, and an annual report of its activities.
67.summary_2:
The forum is established to provide technical expertise and to advise the Board and the Commission, contributing to tasks under the Regulation. It serves as a consultative body to support the development and implementation of AI-related work within the policy framework.
Membership must reflect a balanced mix of stakeholders, including industry, start-ups, SMEs, civil society, and academia, and balance commercial versus non-commercial interests; within commercial interests, emphasis on SMEs and other undertakings. In addition, the Fundamental Rights Agency, ENISA, CEN, CENELEC, and ETSI shall be permanent members. Members are appointed by the Commission from stakeholders with recognised expertise in AI, and terms of office are two years, extendable by up to four years.
The forum shall draw up its rules of procedure and elect two co-chairs from among its members, with each co-chair serving a two-year term, renewable once. It must meet at least twice a year and may invite experts and other stakeholders to its meetings. It may establish standing or temporary sub-groups as needed to examine specific questions related to the Regulation's objectives.
The forum may prepare opinions, recommendations, and written contributions at the request of the Board or the Commission. It is required to prepare an annual report on its activities, which shall be publicly available.

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

68.summary_1: The Commission must establish a scientific panel of independent AI experts by implementing act to support enforcement of the Regulation, with criteria ensuring expertise, independence, and diligence, and with gender and geographical representation. The panel shall advise and support the AI Office on general-purpose AI models and systems, assist market surveillance and cross-border surveillance, and contribute to the Union safeguard procedure. Members must act impartially, keep information confidential, refrain from taking instructions, and publicly disclose their interests; the implementing act will set conditions for alerts and for requesting AI Office assistance.
68.summary_2:
A scientific panel of independent experts shall be established by an implementing act to support enforcement activities under this Regulation, with the act adopted under the procedure specified in the implementing acts framework. The panel's purpose is to assist in enforcing the Regulation regarding AI.
The panel shall comprise experts selected for up-to-date scientific or technical AI expertise, and who are independent of AI system or general-purpose AI model providers and capable of diligent, accurate, and objective work. The Commission, in consultation with the Board, will determine the panel's size and must ensure fair gender and geographical representation.
The panel's duties include advising and supporting the AI Office on: the implementation and enforcement for general-purpose AI models and systems; alerting the AI Office to possible systemic EU-level risks (per Article 90); aiding the development of tools and benchmarks to evaluate capabilities; providing guidance on the classification of general-purpose AI models with systemic risk and on classifications of various models; and contributing to the development of tools and templates. It may also support market surveillance authorities on request, assist cross-border market surveillance under Article 74(11), and aid the AI Office in the Union safeguard procedure (Article 81).
Experts must act with impartiality and objectivity, maintain confidentiality, and not take instructions in exercising their tasks. Each expert must publish a declaration of interests, and the AI Office must implement systems to actively manage and prevent conflicts of interest.
The implementing act shall lay down the conditions, procedures, and detailed arrangements for the panel and its members to issue alerts and to request the AI Office's assistance in performing the panel's tasks.

69.unit_title: Access to the pool of experts by the Member States
69.text:
1. Member States may call upon experts of the scientific panel to support their enforcement activities under this Regulation.

2. The Member States may be required to pay fees for the advice and support provided by the experts. The structure and the level of fees as well as the scale and structure of recoverable costs shall be set out in the implementing act referred to in Article 68(1), taking into account the objectives of the adequate implementation of this Regulation, cost-effectiveness and the necessity of ensuring effective access to experts for all Member States.

3. The Commission shall facilitate timely access to the experts by the Member States, as needed, and ensure that the combination of support activities carried out by Union AI testing support pursuant to Article 84 and experts pursuant to this Article is efficiently organised and provides the best possible added value.

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

69.summary_1: Member States may consult the scientific panel to support enforcement under the Regulation, with fees for expert advice to be set in the implementing act referred to in Article 68(1). The arrangement aims to ensure cost-effectiveness and broad access to experts for all Member States. The Commission must ensure timely access and coordinate Union AI testing support under Article 84 with expert assistance to maximize added value.
69.summary_2:
Member States may request support from experts of the scientific panel to assist their enforcement activities under the Regulation.
Fees for the advice and support provided by the experts may be required from Member States. The implementing act will set the fee structure, level, and recoverable costs, taking into account effective implementation, cost-efficiency, and ensuring accessible expert support for all Member States.
The Commission must ensure timely access to the experts as needed and coordinate the deployment of support from both Union AI testing structures and the expert pool to maximize added value and efficiency.

70.unit_title: Designation of national competent authorities and single points of contact
70.text:
1. Each Member State shall establish or designate as national competent authorities at least one notifying authority and at least one market surveillance authority for the purposes of this Regulation. Those national competent authorities shall exercise their powers independently, impartially and without bias so as to safeguard the objectivity of their activities and tasks, and to ensure the application and implementation of this Regulation. The members of those authorities shall refrain from any action incompatible with their duties. Provided that those principles are observed, such activities and tasks may be performed by one or more designated authorities, in accordance with the organisational needs of the Member State.

2. Member States shall communicate to the Commission the identity of the notifying authorities and the market surveillance authorities and the tasks of those authorities, as well as any subsequent changes thereto. Member States shall make publicly available information on how competent authorities and single points of contact can be contacted, through electronic communication means by 2 August 2025. Member States shall designate a market surveillance authority to act as the single point of contact for this Regulation, and shall notify the Commission of the identity of the single point of contact. The Commission shall make a list of the single points of contact publicly available.

3. Member States shall ensure that their national competent authorities are provided with adequate technical, financial and human resources, and with infrastructure to fulfil their tasks effectively under this Regulation. In particular, the national competent authorities shall have a sufficient number of personnel permanently available whose competences and expertise shall include an in-depth understanding of AI technologies, data and data computing, personal data protection, cybersecurity, fundamental rights, health and safety risks and knowledge of existing standards and legal requirements. Member States shall assess and, if necessary, update competence and resource requirements referred to in this paragraph on an annual basis.

4. National competent authorities shall take appropriate measures to ensure an adequate level of cybersecurity.

5. When performing their tasks, the national competent authorities shall act in accordance with the confidentiality obligations set out in Article 78.

6. By 2 August 2025, and once every two years thereafter, Member States shall report to the Commission on the status of the financial and human resources of the national competent authorities, with an assessment of their adequacy. The Commission shall transmit that information to the Board for discussion and possible recommendations.

7. The Commission shall facilitate the exchange of experience between national competent authorities.

8. National competent authorities may provide guidance and advice on the implementation of this Regulation, in particular to SMEs including start-ups, taking into account the guidance and advice of the Board and the Commission, as appropriate. Whenever national competent authorities intend to provide guidance and advice with regard to an AI system in areas covered by other Union law, the national competent authorities under that Union law shall be consulted, as appropriate.

9. Where Union institutions, bodies, offices or agencies fall within the scope of this Regulation, the European Data Protection Supervisor shall act as the competent authority for their supervision.

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

70.summary_1: Member States must designate national competent authorities (at least one notifying authority and one market surveillance authority) with independence and adequate resources, and appoint a single point of contact whose identity must be communicated to the Commission and publicly listed by 2 August 2025. They must maintain cybersecurity and ensure technical, financial and human resources with expertise in AI, data, data protection, cybersecurity, fundamental rights, and health and safety, with annual assessments of competence and resources, and must report resource adequacy to the Commission every two years, which will be considered by the Board. They may issue guidance to SMEs, must consult other Union-law authorities when appropriate, and for Union institutions the European Data Protection Supervisor acts as the competent supervisor.
70.summary_2:
Member States must designate at least one notifying authority and one market surveillance authority with independence, impartiality, and objectivity in their exercise of powers to ensure proper application of the Regulation; these authorities may be grouped or split as needed for national organization.
Member States must inform the Commission of the identities, tasks, and any changes to these authorities, and publish publicly accessible contact information by 2 August 2025. They must designate a market surveillance authority as the single point of contact for the Regulation, with the Commission publishing a public list of these points of contact. National competent authorities must have adequate technical, financial, and human resources, including personnel with expertise in AI, data processing, personal data protection, cybersecurity, fundamental rights, health and safety, and relevant standards, with annual assessments of resource and competence needs. They must ensure an adequate level of cybersecurity and operate under the confidentiality obligations set out in the applicable provision.
The authorities are required to report to the Commission every two years on their financial and human resources and their adequacy, with the Commission sharing this information with the Board for discussion and recommendations. The Commission shall facilitate exchange of experience between national authorities. National authorities may provide guidance to SMEs and startups, considering input from the Board and the Commission, and must consult other Union-law competent authorities when their guidance touches areas governed by those laws. For Union institutions, the European Data Protection Supervisor acts as the competent authority for supervision.

71.unit_title: EU database for high-risk AI systems listed in Annex III
71.text:
1. The Commission shall, in collaboration with the Member States, set up and maintain an EU database containing information referred to in paragraphs 2 and 3 of this Article concerning high-risk AI systems referred to in Article 6(2) which are registered in accordance with Articles 49 and 60 and AI systems that are not considered as high-risk pursuant to Article 6(3) and which are registered in accordance with Article 6(4) and Article 49. When setting the functional specifications of such database, the Commission shall consult the relevant experts, and when updating the functional specifications of such database, the Commission shall consult the Board.

2. The data listed in Sections A and B of Annex VIII shall be entered into the EU database by the provider or, where applicable, by the authorised representative.

3. The data listed in Section C of Annex VIII shall be entered into the EU database by the deployer who is, or who acts on behalf of, a public authority, agency or body, in accordance with Article 49(3) and (4).

4. With the exception of the section referred to in Article 49(4) and Article 60(4), point (c), the information contained in the EU database registered in accordance with Article 49 shall be accessible and publicly available in a user-friendly manner. The information should be easily navigable and machine-readable. The information registered in accordance with Article 60 shall be accessible only to market surveillance authorities and the Commission, unless the prospective provider or provider has given consent for also making the information accessible the public.

5. The EU database shall contain personal data only in so far as necessary for collecting and processing information in accordance with this Regulation. That information shall include the names and contact details of natural persons who are responsible for registering the system and have the legal authority to represent the provider or the deployer, as applicable.

6. The Commission shall be the controller of the EU database. It shall make available to providers, prospective providers and deployers adequate technical and administrative support. The EU database shall comply with the applicable accessibility requirements.

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

71.summary_1: The Commission, with Member States, shall set up and maintain an EU database containing information about high-risk AI systems listed in Annex III (and certain non-high-risk systems registered under Articles 49, 60, and related provisions), with the database’s functional specifications developed after consulting relevant experts and updated with input from the EU Board. Data in Annex VIII Sections A and B must be entered by the provider or authorized representative, and data in Section C must be entered by the deployer acting for a public authority under Article 49(3)–(4). Public accessibility of the database is the default for information registered under Article 49 in a user-friendly and machine-readable format, while information registered under Article 60 is accessible only to market surveillance authorities and the Commission unless consent is given; personal data is limited to what is necessary, including names and contact details of those responsible, and the Commission acts as the data controller with obligations to provide support and ensure accessibility.
71.summary_2:
The Commission, with Member States, must establish and maintain an EU database containing information about high-risk AI systems registered under Articles 49 and 60, as well as certain non-high-risk systems registered under Article 6(4) and Article 49. Functional specifications are to be developed with expert consultation, and updates to those specifications require consultation with the Board.
- Data from Sections A and B of Annex VIII must be entered into the EU database by the provider or, where applicable, its authorized representative. Data from Section C of Annex VIII must be entered by the deployer who is, or acts on behalf of, a public authority, agency, or body, in accordance with Articles 49(3) and (4).
- With limited exceptions, information registered under Article 49 is publicly accessible in a user-friendly and machine-readable format. Information registered under Article 60 is accessible only to market surveillance authorities and the Commission, unless the prospective provider or provider consents to public access (where permitted).
- Personal data may be included only to the extent necessary for collecting and processing under this Regulation, and may include the names and contact details of natural persons responsible for registering the system and who have the authority to represent the provider or deployer. The Commission acts as the data controller and must provide appropriate technical and administrative support, with the EU database meeting accessibility requirements.

72.unit_title: Post-market monitoring by providers and post-market monitoring plan for high-risk AI systems
72.text:
1. Providers shall establish and document a post-market monitoring system in a manner that is proportionate to the nature of the AI technologies and the risks of the high-risk AI system.

2. The post-market monitoring system shall actively and systematically collect, document and analyse relevant data which may be provided by deployers or which may be collected through other sources on the performance of high-risk AI systems throughout their lifetime, and which allow the provider to evaluate the continuous compliance of AI systems with the requirements set out in Chapter III, Section 2. Where relevant, post-market monitoring shall include an analysis of the interaction with other AI systems. This obligation shall not cover sensitive operational data of deployers which are law-enforcement authorities.

3. The post-market monitoring system shall be based on a post-market monitoring plan. The post-market monitoring plan shall be part of the technical documentation referred to in Annex IV. The Commission shall adopt an implementing act laying down detailed provisions establishing a template for the post-market monitoring plan and the list of elements to be included in the plan by 2 February 2026. That implementing act shall be adopted in accordance with the examination procedure referred to in Article 98(2).

4. For high-risk AI systems covered by the Union harmonisation legislation listed in Section A of Annex I, where a post-market monitoring system and plan are already established under that legislation, in order to ensure consistency, avoid duplications and minimise additional burdens, providers shall have a choice of integrating, as appropriate, the necessary elements described in paragraphs 1, 2 and 3 using the template referred in paragraph 3 into systems and plans already existing under that legislation, provided that it achieves an equivalent level of protection.
The first subparagraph of this paragraph shall also apply to high-risk AI systems referred to in point 5 of Annex III placed on the market or put into service by financial institutions that are subject to requirements under Union financial services law regarding their internal governance, arrangements or processes.

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

72.summary_1: Providers must establish and document a post-market monitoring system for high-risk AI systems, proportionate to the nature of the AI technologies and their risks. The system shall actively collect, document and analyze relevant data on performance throughout the system's lifetime to assess ongoing compliance with Chapter III, Section 2, including interactions with other AI systems where relevant, and it shall not cover sensitive operational data of deployers. The post-market monitoring plan, forming part of the technical documentation in Annex IV, will be complemented by an implementing act due by 2 February 2026 detailing the template and elements; for high-risk systems covered by Annex I Section A or placed by financial institutions under Union financial services law, providers may integrate these elements into existing post-market arrangements to avoid duplication, provided an equivalent level of protection is achieved.
72.summary_2:
Providers must establish and document a post-market monitoring system for high-risk AI that is proportionate to the AI's nature and risk.
The system must actively and systematically collect, document, and analyze relevant data on performance throughout the AI's lifetime, using data from deployers or other sources to assess ongoing compliance with applicable requirements; it may examine interactions with other AI systems, but it must not include sensitive operational data of deployers that are law-enforcement authorities.
The monitoring system must be based on a post-market monitoring plan, which forms part of the technical documentation in Annex IV. The Commission will publish an implementing act with a template and required elements for the plan by 2 February 2026, following the procedure in Article 98(2).
If high-risk AI systems fall under Union harmonisation legislation in Annex I Section A with an existing post-market monitoring regime, providers may integrate the necessary elements from paragraphs 1-3 into existing systems/plans using the new template, to avoid duplication and burdens, provided the level of protection is equivalent. This also applies to high-risk AI systems described in Annex III point 5 placed on the market or into service by financial institutions subject to Union financial services internal governance requirements.

73.text:
1. Providers of high-risk AI systems placed on the Union market shall report any serious incident to the market surveillance authorities of the Member States where that incident occurred.

2. The report referred to in paragraph 1 shall be made immediately after the provider has established a causal link between the AI system and the serious incident or the reasonable likelihood of such a link, and, in any event, not later than 15 days after the provider or, where applicable, the deployer, becomes aware of the serious incident.
The period for the reporting referred to in the first subparagraph shall take account of the severity of the serious incident.

3. Notwithstanding paragraph 2 of this Article, in the event of a widespread infringement or a serious incident as defined in Article 3, point (49)(b), the report referred to in paragraph 1 of this Article shall be provided immediately, and not later than two days after the provider or, where applicable, the deployer becomes aware of that incident.

4. Notwithstanding paragraph 2, in the event of the death of a person, the report shall be provided immediately after the provider or the deployer has established, or as soon as it suspects, a causal relationship between the high-risk AI system and the serious incident, but not later than 10 days after the date on which the provider or, where applicable, the deployer becomes aware of the serious incident.

5. Where necessary to ensure timely reporting, the provider or, where applicable, the deployer, may submit an initial report that is incomplete, followed by a complete report.

6. Following the reporting of a serious incident pursuant to paragraph 1, the provider shall, without delay, perform the necessary investigations in relation to the serious incident and the AI system concerned. This shall include a risk assessment of the incident, and corrective action.
The provider shall cooperate with the competent authorities, and where relevant with the notified body concerned, during the investigations referred to in the first subparagraph, and shall not perform any investigation which involves altering the AI system concerned in a way which may affect any subsequent evaluation of the causes of the incident, prior to informing the competent authorities of such action.

7. Upon receiving a notification related to a serious incident referred to in Article 3, point (49)(c), the relevant market surveillance authority shall inform the national public authorities or bodies referred to in Article 77(1). The Commission shall develop dedicated guidance to facilitate compliance with the obligations set out in paragraph 1 of this Article. That guidance shall be issued by 2 August 2025, and shall be assessed regularly.

8. The market surveillance authority shall take appropriate measures, as provided for in Article 19 of Regulation (EU) 2019/1020, within seven days from the date it received the notification referred to in paragraph 1 of this Article, and shall follow the notification procedures as provided in that Regulation.

9. For high-risk AI systems referred to in Annex III that are placed on the market or put into service by providers that are subject to Union legislative instruments laying down reporting obligations equivalent to those set out in this Regulation, the notification of serious incidents shall be limited to those referred to in Article 3, point (49)(c).

10. For high-risk AI systems which are safety components of devices, or are themselves devices, covered by Regulations (EU) 2017/745 and (EU) 2017/746, the notification of serious incidents shall be limited to those referred to in Article 3, point (49)(c) of this Regulation, and shall be made to the national competent authority chosen for that purpose by the Member States where the incident occurred.

11. National competent authorities shall immediately notify the Commission of any serious incident, whether or not they have taken action on it, in accordance with Article 20 of Regulation (EU) 2019/1020.

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

73.summary_1: High-risk AI providers must report serious incidents to the market surveillance authorities where the incident occurred, with reporting timelines depending on severity (immediate for deaths or widespread infringements, and up to 15 days for other cases). An initial incomplete report may be submitted, followed by a complete report, and providers must then investigate, conduct a risk assessment, and cooperate with authorities under applicable notification procedures. The Commission will issue dedicated guidance by 2 August 2025 to facilitate compliance.
73.summary_2:
Providers of high-risk AI systems placed on the Union market must report any serious incident to the market surveillance authorities of the Member State where the incident occurred. Reports should be made as soon as a causal link between the AI system and the incident is established or reasonably likely, and in any event no later than 15 days after becoming aware; however, for widespread infringements or the defined serious incidents, reports must be immediate or within two days of awareness, and for deaths the report must be immediate after establishing or suspecting a causal link, but no more than 10 days.
An initial report may be submitted to ensure timely notification if incomplete, with a subsequent complete report to follow. After reporting, the provider must promptly conduct investigations, perform a risk assessment, and implement corrective actions. The provider must cooperate with competent authorities and, where relevant, with the notified body, and must not alter the AI system in a way that could affect subsequent evaluation of the incident without informing the authorities.
When a notification relates to a serious incident, the market surveillance authority must inform the national public authorities or bodies promptly, and the Commission will develop dedicated guidance to facilitate compliance, with guidance to be issued by 2 August 2025 and regularly updated. The market surveillance authority must take appropriate measures within seven days of notification and follow the procedures under the relevant regulatory framework for such notifications.
For high-risk AI systems covered by Annex III with equivalent reporting obligations under other EU instruments, notification is limited to the subset of incidents requiring such reporting. For high-risk AI systems that are safety components of devices or devices themselves covered by the medical device regulations, notification is limited to the same subset and must be made to the national competent authority designated by the Member State where the incident occurred. National competent authorities must immediately notify the Commission of any serious incident, in accordance with the established governance framework.

74.unit_title: Market surveillance and control of AI systems in the Union market
74.text:
1. Regulation (EU) 2019/1020 shall apply to AI systems covered by this Regulation. For the purposes of the effective enforcement of this Regulation:
(a) any reference to an economic operator under Regulation (EU) 2019/1020 shall be understood as including all operators identified in Article 2(1) of this Regulation;
(b) any reference to a product under Regulation (EU) 2019/1020 shall be understood as including all AI systems falling within the scope of this Regulation.

2. As part of their reporting obligations under Article 34(4) of Regulation (EU) 2019/1020, the market surveillance authorities shall report annually to the Commission and relevant national competition authorities any information identified in the course of market surveillance activities that may be of potential interest for the application of Union law on competition rules. They shall also annually report to the Commission about the use of prohibited practices that occurred during that year and about the measures taken.

3. For high-risk AI systems related to products covered by the Union harmonisation legislation listed in Section A of Annex I, the market surveillance authority for the purposes of this Regulation shall be the authority responsible for market surveillance activities designated under those legal acts.
By derogation from the first subparagraph, and in appropriate circumstances, Member States may designate another relevant authority to act as a market surveillance authority, provided they ensure coordination with the relevant sectoral market surveillance authorities responsible for the enforcement of the Union harmonisation legislation listed in Annex I.

4. The procedures referred to in Articles 79 to 83 of this Regulation shall not apply to AI systems related to products covered by the Union harmonisation legislation listed in section A of Annex I, where such legal acts already provide for procedures ensuring an equivalent level of protection and having the same objective. In such cases, the relevant sectoral procedures shall apply instead.

5. Without prejudice to the powers of market surveillance authorities under Article 14 of Regulation (EU) 2019/1020, for the purpose of ensuring the effective enforcement of this Regulation, market surveillance authorities may exercise the powers referred to in Article 14(4), points (d) and (j), of that Regulation remotely, as appropriate.

6. For high-risk AI systems placed on the market, put into service, or used by financial institutions regulated by Union financial services law, the market surveillance authority for the purposes of this Regulation shall be the relevant national authority responsible for the financial supervision of those institutions under that legislation in so far as the placing on the market, putting into service, or the use of the AI system is in direct connection with the provision of those financial services.

7. By way of derogation from paragraph 6, in appropriate circumstances, and provided that coordination is ensured, another relevant authority may be identified by the Member State as market surveillance authority for the purposes of this Regulation.
National market surveillance authorities supervising regulated credit institutions regulated under Directive 2013/36/EU, which are participating in the Single Supervisory Mechanism established by Regulation (EU) No 1024/2013, should report, without delay, to the European Central Bank any information identified in the course of their market surveillance activities that may be of potential interest for the prudential supervisory tasks of the European Central Bank specified in that Regulation.

8. For high-risk AI systems listed in point 1 of Annex III to this Regulation, in so far as the systems are used for law enforcement purposes, border management and justice and democracy, and for high-risk AI systems listed in points 6, 7 and 8 of Annex III to this Regulation, Member States shall designate as market surveillance authorities for the purposes of this Regulation either the competent data protection supervisory authorities under Regulation (EU) 2016/679 or Directive (EU) 2016/680, or any other authority designated pursuant to the same conditions laid down in Articles 41 to 44 of Directive (EU) 2016/680. Market surveillance activities shall in no way affect the independence of judicial authorities, or otherwise interfere with their activities when acting in their judicial capacity.

9. Where Union institutions, bodies, offices or agencies fall within the scope of this Regulation, the European Data Protection Supervisor shall act as their market surveillance authority, except in relation to the Court of Justice of the European Union acting in its judicial capacity.

10. Member States shall facilitate coordination between market surveillance authorities designated under this Regulation and other relevant national authorities or bodies which supervise the application of Union harmonisation legislation listed in Annex I, or in other Union law, that might be relevant for the high-risk AI systems referred to in Annex III.

11. Market surveillance authorities and the Commission shall be able to propose joint activities, including joint investigations, to be conducted by either market surveillance authorities or market surveillance authorities jointly with the Commission, that have the aim of promoting compliance, identifying non-compliance, raising awareness or providing guidance in relation to this Regulation with respect to specific categories of high-risk AI systems that are found to present a serious risk across two or more Member States in accordance with Article 9 of Regulation (EU) 2019/1020. The AI Office shall provide coordination support for joint investigations.

12. Without prejudice to the powers provided for under Regulation (EU) 2019/1020, and where relevant and limited to what is necessary to fulfil their tasks, the market surveillance authorities shall be granted full access by providers to the documentation as well as the training, validation and testing data sets used for the development of high-risk AI systems, including, where appropriate and subject to security safeguards, through application programming interfaces (API) or other relevant technical means and tools enabling remote access.

13. Market surveillance authorities shall be granted access to the source code of the high-risk AI system upon a reasoned request and only when both of the following conditions are fulfilled:
(a) access to source code is necessary to assess the conformity of a high-risk AI system with the requirements set out in Chapter III, Section 2; and
(b) testing or auditing procedures and verifications based on the data and documentation provided by the provider have been exhausted or proved insufficient.

14. Any information or documentation obtained by market surveillance authorities shall be treated in accordance with the confidentiality obligations set out in Article 78.

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

74.summary_1: Sets harmonised market surveillance rules for AI systems in the Union, applying Regulation (EU) 2019/1020 and assigning surveillance and reporting roles across sectoral, financial services, and harmonisation contexts. It requires market surveillance to report annually on information relevant to competition and prohibited practices, designates appropriate authorities for high-risk AI in various sectors, and allows access to documentation, data sets, and source code for conformity assessment under strict conditions. It enables joint investigations, coordination by an AI Office, and confidentiality of information obtained.
74.summary_2:
This provision ensures that enforcement and oversight of AI systems in the Union mirror established market-surveillance frameworks.
