It extends the application of the existing product-and-operator regime to AI systems, clarifying that references to economic operators and products under the older regulation cover all actors and AI systems within this Regulation's scope. Market surveillance authorities have ongoing reporting duties to the Commission and national competition authorities, including information that may affect Union competition law and any use of prohibited practices and the measures taken.
For high-risk AI systems related to products governed by Union harmonisation acts, the market surveillance function is entrusted to the authority responsible under those acts. Member States may designate another appropriate authority, provided there is adequate coordination with the relevant sectoral authorities enforcing the harmonisation acts. This aims to ensure coherent supervision across applicable product-safety and market rules.
If a high-risk AI system related to harmonised products is covered by sectoral instruments that already provide equivalent protection, the general procedures for market surveillance under this Regulation do not apply; the sectoral procedures take precedence. In addition, authorities may exercise certain enforcement powers remotely to support effective enforcement where appropriate, and where relevant to financial services, the national authority responsible for financial supervision takes the lead for placing on the market, putting into service, or use in direct connection with those services, with scope for designation of another authority if coordinated.
The enforcement regime also contemplates special arrangements for law-enforcement, border-management, and justice-focused AI systems, allowing designation of the competent data protection authorities or other authorities under Directive 2016/680. Independence of judicial authorities must remain intact. Union institutions fall under the European Data Protection Supervisor as market surveillance authority, with an exception for the Court of Justice acting in its judicial capacity. The text encourages coordination among market surveillance authorities, other national bodies, and, where cross-border risk is identified, joint investigations and coordinated actions. Providers must grant authorities broad access to documentation, development data, and, upon a reasoned request and after other audit avenues are exhausted, access to source code, all subject to confidentiality rules.

75.unit_title: Mutual assistance, market surveillance and control of general-purpose AI systems
75.text:
1. Where an AI system is based on a general-purpose AI model, and the model and the system are developed by the same provider, the AI Office shall have powers to monitor and supervise compliance of that AI system with obligations under this Regulation. To carry out its monitoring and supervision tasks, the AI Office shall have all the powers of a market surveillance authority provided for in this Section and Regulation (EU) 2019/1020.

2. Where the relevant market surveillance authorities have sufficient reason to consider general-purpose AI systems that can be used directly by deployers for at least one purpose that is classified as high-risk pursuant to this Regulation to be non-compliant with the requirements laid down in this Regulation, they shall cooperate with the AI Office to carry out compliance evaluations, and shall inform the Board and other market surveillance authorities accordingly.

3. Where a market surveillance authority is unable to conclude its investigation of the high-risk AI system because of its inability to access certain information related to the general-purpose AI model despite having made all appropriate efforts to obtain that information, it may submit a reasoned request to the AI Office, by which access to that information shall be enforced. In that case, the AI Office shall supply to the applicant authority without delay, and in any event within 30 days, any information that the AI Office considers to be relevant in order to establish whether a high-risk AI system is non-compliant. Market surveillance authorities shall safeguard the confidentiality of the information that they obtain in accordance with Article 78 of this Regulation. The procedure provided for in Chapter VI of Regulation (EU) 2019/1020 shall apply mutatis mutandis.

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

75.summary_1: The AI Office is empowered to monitor and supervise compliance of general-purpose AI systems developed by the same provider. Market surveillance authorities must cooperate with the AI Office to evaluate high-risk uses and report to the Board when non-compliance is suspected. If access to information about the GP model is blocked, the AI Office may compel disclosure, providing the information within 30 days, with confidentiality protected and the procedure governed mutatis mutandis by Chapter VI of Regulation (EU) 2019/1020.
75.summary_2:
The AI Office may monitor and supervise compliance of a general-purpose AI system when the GP model and the system are developed by the same provider, and it possesses the full market surveillance powers available under this Regulation and Regulation (EU) 2019/1020.
If market surveillance authorities have sufficient reasons to deem GP AI systems deployable for at least one high-risk use non-compliant, they must cooperate with the AI Office to conduct compliance evaluations and must inform the Board and other market surveillance authorities accordingly.
If an investigation cannot be concluded because access to certain GP model information is unavailable despite efforts to obtain it, a market surveillance authority may submit a reasoned request to the AI Office to enforce access. The AI Office must provide any information it deems relevant within 30 days to determine non-compliance, while authorities must protect confidentiality as required by Article 78.
The procedure set out in Chapter VI of Regulation (EU) 2019/1020 applies mutatis mutandis.

76.unit_title: Supervision of testing in real world conditions by market surveillance authorities
76.text:
1. Market surveillance authorities shall have competences and powers to ensure that testing in real world conditions is in accordance with this Regulation.

2. Where testing in real world conditions is conducted for AI systems that are supervised within an AI regulatory sandbox under Article 58, the market surveillance authorities shall verify the compliance with Article 60 as part of their supervisory role for the AI regulatory sandbox. Those authorities may, as appropriate, allow the testing in real world conditions to be conducted by the provider or prospective provider, in derogation from the conditions set out in Article 60(4), points (f) and (g).

3. Where a market surveillance authority has been informed by the prospective provider, the provider or any third party of a serious incident or has other grounds for considering that the conditions set out in Articles 60 and 61 are not met, it may take either of the following decisions on its territory, as appropriate:
(a) to suspend or terminate the testing in real world conditions;
(b) to require the provider or prospective provider and the deployer or prospective deployer to modify any aspect of the testing in real world conditions.

4. Where a market surveillance authority has taken a decision referred to in paragraph 3 of this Article, or has issued an objection within the meaning of Article 60(4), point (b), the decision or the objection shall indicate the grounds therefor and how the provider or prospective provider can challenge the decision or objection.

5. Where applicable, where a market surveillance authority has taken a decision referred to in paragraph 3, it shall communicate the grounds therefor to the market surveillance authorities of other Member States in which the AI system has been tested in accordance with the testing plan.

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

76.summary_1: Market surveillance authorities have powers to ensure that testing in real-world conditions complies with the Regulation. When testing occurs within an AI regulatory sandbox, they verify compliance with the applicable rules and may allow testing by providers under modified conditions. They may suspend or modify testing, require changes by the provider or deployer, and must communicate the grounds for decisions to other Member States' authorities where testing has occurred.
76.summary_2:
Market surveillance authorities have the competence and powers to ensure that testing in real-world conditions complies with this Regulation.
When testing real-world conditions involves AI systems supervised within an AI regulatory sandbox, the authorities must verify compliance with the real-world testing rules and may allow the testing to be conducted by the provider or prospective provider, potentially derogating from certain conditions in Article 60(4) (f) and (g) as appropriate.
If a market surveillance authority is informed of a serious incident or has grounds to believe that the conditions set out in Articles 60 and 61 are not met, it may suspend or terminate the testing in real-world conditions, or require the provider and deployer to modify the testing arrangements.
Any decision to suspend, terminate, or modify testing, or any objection under Article 60(4)(b), must state the grounds and how the provider or prospective provider can challenge the decision or objection.
Where applicable, the authority must communicate the grounds for its decision to the market surveillance authorities of other Member States in which the AI system has been tested under the testing plan.

77.unit_title: Powers of authorities protecting fundamental rights
77.text:
1. National public authorities or bodies which supervise or enforce the respect of obligations under Union law protecting fundamental rights, including the right to non-discrimination, in relation to the use of high-risk AI systems referred to in Annex III shall have the power to request and access any documentation created or maintained under this Regulation in accessible language and format when access to that documentation is necessary for effectively fulfilling their mandates within the limits of their jurisdiction. The relevant public authority or body shall inform the market surveillance authority of the Member State concerned of any such request.

2. By 2 November 2024, each Member State shall identify the public authorities or bodies referred to in paragraph 1 and make a list of them publicly available. Member States shall notify the list to the Commission and to the other Member States, and shall keep the list up to date.

3. Where the documentation referred to in paragraph 1 is insufficient to ascertain whether an infringement of obligations under Union law protecting fundamental rights has occurred, the public authority or body referred to in paragraph 1 may make a reasoned request to the market surveillance authority, to organise testing of the high-risk AI system through technical means. The market surveillance authority shall organise the testing with the close involvement of the requesting public authority or body within a reasonable time following the request.

4. Any information or documentation obtained by the national public authorities or bodies referred to in paragraph 1 of this Article pursuant to this Article shall be treated in accordance with the confidentiality obligations set out in Article 78.

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

77.summary_1: National public authorities or bodies responsible for enforcing fundamental rights in relation to high-risk AI systems (Annex III) may request access to any documentation under this Regulation in accessible language or format to effectively fulfill their mandates, and must inform the market surveillance authority of such requests. By 2 November 2024, Member States must identify these authorities, publish the list publicly, and notify the Commission and other Member States. If documentation is insufficient to determine an infringement, these authorities may request testing of the high-risk AI system by the market surveillance authority with close involvement of the requesting body, and any obtained information or documentation must be kept confidential under Article 78.
77.summary_2:
National public authorities or bodies that supervise or enforce Union law protecting fundamental rights (including non-discrimination) in relation to high-risk AI systems in Annex III may request and access any documentation created or maintained under this Regulation, in accessible language/format, when such access is necessary to fulfil their mandates within their jurisdiction. They must inform the relevant market surveillance authority of the request.
By 2 November 2024, each Member State must identify these authorities/bodies and publish a public list, notify the Commission and other Member States, and keep the list up to date.
If the documentation is insufficient to determine whether a rights-related infringement has occurred, the requesting authority may issue a reasoned request to the market surveillance authority to organize testing of the high-risk AI system through technical means, with the market surveillance authority coordinating the testing promptly and with the involved authority.
Any information or documentation obtained under this Article must be treated in accordance with the confidentiality obligations set out in Article 78.

notes.57: Directive (EU) 2016/943 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 8 June 2016 on the protection of undisclosed know-how and business information (trade secrets) against their unlawful acquisition, use and disclosure (OJ L 157, 15.6.2016, p. 1).

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

78.summary_1: It imposes confidentiality obligations on the Commission, market surveillance authorities, notified bodies, and others involved in applying the Regulation to protect intellectual property, confidential business information, trade secrets, and security interests during inspections, investigations, and audits. Data collected must be strictly necessary for assessing AI risk and exercising powers, with cybersecurity measures and deletion when no longer needed, in line with Regulation (EU) 2019/1020. It allows limited disclosure in specified circumstances, notably among national authorities or with the Commission for high-risk or law-enforcement needs, preserves sensitive operational data, requires access to technical documentation for certain law-enforcement providers, and authorizes confidential exchanges with third-country regulatory authorities under confidentiality arrangements.
78.summary_2:
The provision imposes strict confidentiality obligations on the Commission, market surveillance authorities, notified bodies, and others involved in applying the Regulation. Information and data obtained in carrying out tasks must be protected to safeguard intellectual property and confidential business information (including source code) unless disclosure is permitted by trade-secret provisions; the confidentiality also covers the effective implementation of the Regulation, public and national security interests, the conduct of criminal or administrative proceedings, and classified information.
Data collected or accessed may only be requested if strictly necessary for assessing AI system risk and exercising powers under this Regulation and Regulation (EU) 2019/1020. Entities must implement robust cybersecurity measures to safeguard security and confidentiality and must delete data once it is no longer needed for the stated purpose, in line with applicable Union or national law.
Confidential information exchanged between national competent authorities or with the Commission should not be disclosed without prior consultation when high-risk AI systems listed in Annex III are used by law enforcement, border control, immigration or asylum authorities and where disclosure could jeopardize public or national security. This exchange excludes sensitive operational data. If law enforcement, immigration or asylum authorities are themselves providers of such high-risk AI systems, the technical documentation remains on their premises; market surveillance authorities must be able to access or obtain copies of the documentation on request, with access limited to staff possessing the appropriate security clearance.
These confidentiality rules do not alter the rights or obligations of the Commission, Member States and their authorities, or notified bodies, to exchange information and issue warnings or to engage in cross-border cooperation, nor do they affect criminal-law information obligations. The Commission and Member States may also confidentially exchange information with regulatory authorities in third countries under relevant international or trade agreements that guarantee adequate confidentiality.

79.unit_title: Procedure at national level for dealing with AI systems presenting a risk
79.text:
1. AI systems presenting a risk shall be understood as a 'product presenting a risk' as defined in Article 3, point 19 of Regulation (EU) 2019/1020, in so far as they present risks to the health or safety, or to fundamental rights, of persons.

2. Where the market surveillance authority of a Member State has sufficient reason to consider an AI system to present a risk as referred to in paragraph 1 of this Article, it shall carry out an evaluation of the AI system concerned in respect of its compliance with all the requirements and obligations laid down in this Regulation. Particular attention shall be given to AI systems presenting a risk to vulnerable groups. Where risks to fundamental rights are identified, the market surveillance authority shall also inform and fully cooperate with the relevant national public authorities or bodies referred to in Article 77(1). The relevant operators shall cooperate as necessary with the market surveillance authority and with the other national public authorities or bodies referred to in Article 77(1).
Where, in the course of that evaluation, the market surveillance authority or, where applicable the market surveillance authority in cooperation with the national public authority referred to in Article 77(1), finds that the AI system does not comply with the requirements and obligations laid down in this Regulation, it shall without undue delay require the relevant operator to take all appropriate corrective actions to bring the AI system into compliance, to withdraw the AI system from the market, or to recall it within a period the market surveillance authority may prescribe, and in any event within the shorter of 15 working days, or as provided for in the relevant Union harmonisation legislation.
The market surveillance authority shall inform the relevant notified body accordingly. Article 18 of Regulation (EU) 2019/1020 shall apply to the measures referred to in the second subparagraph of this paragraph.

3. Where the market surveillance authority considers that the non-compliance is not restricted to its national territory, it shall inform the Commission and the other Member States without undue delay of the results of the evaluation and of the actions which it has required the operator to take.

4. The operator shall ensure that all appropriate corrective action is taken in respect of all the AI systems concerned that it has made available on the Union market.

5. Where the operator of an AI system does not take adequate corrective action within the period referred to in paragraph 2, the market surveillance authority shall take all appropriate provisional measures to prohibit or restrict the AI system's being made available on its national market or put into service, to withdraw the product or the standalone AI system from that market or to recall it. That authority shall without undue delay notify the Commission and the other Member States of those measures.

6. The notification referred to in paragraph 5 shall include all available details, in particular the information necessary for the identification of the non-compliant AI system, the origin of the AI system and the supply chain, the nature of the non-compliance alleged and the risk involved, the nature and duration of the national measures taken and the arguments put forward by the relevant operator. In particular, the market surveillance authorities shall indicate whether the non-compliance is due to one or more of the following:
(a) non-compliance with the prohibition of the AI practices referred to in Article 5;
(b) a failure of a high-risk AI system to meet requirements set out in Chapter III, Section 2;
(c) shortcomings in the harmonised standards or common specifications referred to in Articles 40 and 41 conferring a presumption of conformity;
(d) non-compliance with Article 50.

7. The market surveillance authorities other than the market surveillance authority of the Member State initiating the procedure shall, without undue delay, inform the Commission and the other Member States of any measures adopted and of any additional information at their disposal relating to the non-compliance of the AI system concerned, and, in the event of disagreement with the notified national measure, of their objections.

8. Where, within three months of receipt of the notification referred to in paragraph 5 of this Article, no objection has been raised by either a market surveillance authority of a Member State or by the Commission in respect of a provisional measure taken by a market surveillance authority of another Member State, that measure shall be deemed justified. This shall be without prejudice to the procedural rights of the concerned operator in accordance with Article 18 of Regulation (EU) 2019/1020. The three-month period referred to in this paragraph shall be reduced to 30 days in the event of non-compliance with the prohibition of the AI practices referred to in Article 5 of this Regulation.

9. The market surveillance authorities shall ensure that appropriate restrictive measures are taken in respect of the product or the AI system concerned, such as withdrawal of the product or the AI system from their market, without undue delay.

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

79.summary_1: National market surveillance evaluates AI systems deemed to present risk, with particular attention to vulnerable groups, and may require corrective action, withdrawal, or recall if non-compliance is found. If the non-compliance appears to be cross-border, authorities must inform the Commission and other Member States, and may impose provisional measures to restrict or remove the system from the market. Operators must ensure corrective action across all AI systems they have placed on the Union market, with reference to harmonisation standards and applicable provisions of Regulation 2019/1020.
79.summary_2:
AI systems that present risk are treated as products presenting a risk to health, safety, or fundamental rights, and national market surveillance authorities may evaluate their compliance with all applicable requirements and obligations. Special attention must be given to risks affecting vulnerable groups, and if fundamental rights are at stake, authorities must cooperate with relevant national public bodies. Operators must cooperate with authorities during the evaluation.
If non-compliance is found, authorities must require the operator to take corrective actions, withdraw the AI system from the market, or recall it within a period that is the shorter of 15 working days or the period laid down in harmonisation legislation. The authority must inform the relevant notified body, and the measures proceed in accordance with Regulation (EU) 2019/1020.
If non-compliance is not confined to one Member State, authorities must promptly inform the Commission and other Member States of the evaluation results and the actions required of the operator.
Operators must ensure corrective actions cover all AI systems they have placed on the Union market. If the operator does not act within the prescribed period, authorities may impose provisional measures to prohibit, restrict, withdraw, or recall the AI system, and must notify the Commission and other Member States. The notification must detail identification, origin and supply chain, nature and risk of non-compliance, duration of measures, and the operator's arguments, and must indicate whether non-compliance arises from prohibited practices, failure of a high-risk system to meet requirements, shortcomings in harmonised standards or common specifications, or non-compliance with transparency provisions. Other Member States' authorities must be informed and may raise objections; if no objection within the set period (three months, or 30 days for certain prohibitions), the measure is deemed justified, without prejudice to the operator's rights. Authorities must ensure restrictive measures are applied without undue delay, including withdrawal from the market where appropriate.

80.unit_title: Procedure for dealing with AI systems classified by the provider as non-high-risk in application of Annex III
80.text:
1. Where a market surveillance authority has sufficient reason to consider that an AI system classified by the provider as non-high-risk pursuant to Article 6(3) is indeed high-risk, the market surveillance authority shall carry out an evaluation of the AI system concerned in respect of its classification as a high-risk AI system based on the conditions set out in Article 6(3) and the Commission guidelines.

2. Where, in the course of that evaluation, the market surveillance authority finds that the AI system concerned is high-risk, it shall without undue delay require the relevant provider to take all necessary actions to bring the AI system into compliance with the requirements and obligations laid down in this Regulation, as well as take appropriate corrective action within a period the market surveillance authority may prescribe.

3. Where the market surveillance authority considers that the use of the AI system concerned is not restricted to its national territory, it shall inform the Commission and the other Member States without undue delay of the results of the evaluation and of the actions which it has required the provider to take.

4. The provider shall ensure that all necessary action is taken to bring the AI system into compliance with the requirements and obligations laid down in this Regulation. Where the provider of an AI system concerned does not bring the AI system into compliance with those requirements and obligations within the period referred to in paragraph 2 of this Article, the provider shall be subject to fines in accordance with Article 99.

5. The provider shall ensure that all appropriate corrective action is taken in respect of all the AI systems concerned that it has made available on the Union market.

6. Where the provider of the AI system concerned does not take adequate corrective action within the period referred to in paragraph 2 of this Article, Article 79(5) to (9) shall apply.

7. Where, in the course of the evaluation pursuant to paragraph 1 of this Article, the market surveillance authority establishes that the AI system was misclassified by the provider as non-high-risk in order to circumvent the application of requirements in Chapter III, Section 2, the provider shall be subject to fines in accordance with Article 99.

8. In exercising their power to monitor the application of this Article, and in accordance with Article 11 of Regulation (EU) 2019/1020, market surveillance authorities may perform appropriate checks, taking into account in particular information stored in the EU database referred to in Article 71 of this Regulation.

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

80.summary_1: If a market surveillance authority suspects that an AI system labeled non-high-risk by the provider is actually high-risk, it must evaluate the system using the conditions in Article 6(3) and the Commission guidelines. If deemed high-risk, the provider must take all necessary actions to bring the system into compliance within the period set by the authority, and, where use is not confined to the national territory, the authority must inform the Commission and other Member States; non-compliance may trigger fines under Article 99 and require corrective action for all affected AI systems, with Article 79(5)-(9) applying if corrective action remains inadequate. The authority may conduct checks and may reference information stored in the EU database under Article 71 of this Regulation during such evaluations.
80.summary_2:
If a market surveillance authority suspects that an AI system the provider labeled as non-high-risk is actually high-risk, it must evaluate the system against the high-risk criteria and Commission guidelines to determine whether the classification is correct. 
If the evaluation concludes the system is high-risk, the authority must promptly require the provider to take all necessary actions to bring the system into compliance with the regulation and to carry out corrective actions within a period set by the authority.
If the system's use is not limited to the national territory, the authority must inform the Commission and other Member States of the evaluation results and the actions required of the provider without undue delay.
The provider must implement all required actions to achieve compliance and, if it fails to do so within the prescribed period, face fines under Article 99. The provider must also ensure appropriate corrective action for all AI systems it has made available on the Union market.
If the provider does not take adequate corrective action within the period, the enforcement provisions of Article 79(5) to (9) apply. Misclassification by the provider to circumvent Chapter III, Section 2, may result in fines under Article 99. Market surveillance authorities may conduct checks as part of monitoring compliance, leveraging information from the EU database and in line with Regulation (EU) 2019/1020.

81.text:
1. Where, within three months of receipt of the notification referred to in Article 79(5), or within 30 days in the case of non-compliance with the prohibition of the AI practices referred to in Article 5, objections are raised by the market surveillance authority of a Member State to a measure taken by another market surveillance authority, or where the Commission considers the measure to be contrary to Union law, the Commission shall without undue delay enter into consultation with the market surveillance authority of the relevant Member State and the operator or operators, and shall evaluate the national measure. On the basis of the results of that evaluation, the Commission shall, within six months, or within 60 days in the case of non-compliance with the prohibition of the AI practices referred to in Article 5, starting from the notification referred to in Article 79(5), decide whether the national measure is justified and shall notify its decision to the market surveillance authority of the Member State concerned. The Commission shall also inform all other market surveillance authorities of its decision.

2. Where the Commission considers the measure taken by the relevant Member State to be justified, all Member States shall ensure that they take appropriate restrictive measures in respect of the AI system concerned, such as requiring the withdrawal of the AI system from their market without undue delay, and shall inform the Commission accordingly. Where the Commission considers the national measure to be unjustified, the Member State concerned shall withdraw the measure and shall inform the Commission accordingly.

3. Where the national measure is considered justified and the non-compliance of the AI system is attributed to shortcomings in the harmonised standards or common specifications referred to in Articles 40 and 41 of this Regulation, the Commission shall apply the procedure provided for in Article 11 of Regulation (EU) No 1025/2012.

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

81.summary_1: The text establishes a Union safeguard procedure for AI systems: if objections to a national measure arise or the Commission deems a measure contrary to Union law, the Commission must consult with the relevant Member State and the operator and decide within defined timeframes whether the measure is justified. If justified, other Member States must take restrictive actions or withdraw the AI system; if unjustified, the national measure must be withdrawn. If non-compliance is due to shortcomings in harmonised standards or common specifications, the procedure provided for in Regulation (EU) No 1025/2012 applies.
81.summary_2:
When objections are raised within three months (or within 30 days for non-compliance with the prohibition in Article 5) to a national measure, the Commission must promptly consult with the relevant Member State's market surveillance authority and the operator(s), and evaluate the national measure. Based on this evaluation, the Commission must decide within six months (or 60 days for Article 5 non-compliance) whether the national measure is justified and inform the concerned market surveillance authority and all other Member States of its decision.
If the Commission finds the national measure justified, all Member States must take appropriate restrictive actions against the AI system, such as withdrawing it from their markets, and inform the Commission. If the Commission finds the national measure unjustified, the Member State must withdraw the measure and inform the Commission.
If the justified national measure arises because of shortcomings in harmonised standards or common specifications, the Commission shall apply the standardisation procedure set out in Regulation (EU) No 1025/2012 (the Article 11 procedure).
The process ensures coordinated action across the Union: the Commission's decision is communicated to the relevant Member State and to other market surveillance authorities, and any withdrawal or other restrictive measure is implemented with a view to addressing cross-border non-compliance.

82.unit_title: Compliant AI systems which present a risk
82.text:
1. Where, having performed an evaluation under Article 79, after consulting the relevant national public authority referred to in Article 77(1), the market surveillance authority of a Member State finds that although a high-risk AI system complies with this Regulation, it nevertheless presents a risk to the health or safety of persons, to fundamental rights, or to other aspects of public interest protection, it shall require the relevant operator to take all appropriate measures to ensure that the AI system concerned, when placed on the market or put into service, no longer presents that risk without undue delay, within a period it may prescribe.

2. The provider or other relevant operator shall ensure that corrective action is taken in respect of all the AI systems concerned that it has made available on the Union market within the timeline prescribed by the market surveillance authority of the Member State referred to in paragraph 1.

3. The Member States shall immediately inform the Commission and the other Member States of a finding under paragraph 1. That information shall include all available details, in particular the data necessary for the identification of the AI system concerned, the origin and the supply chain of the AI system, the nature of the risk involved and the nature and duration of the national measures taken.

4. The Commission shall without undue delay enter into consultation with the Member States concerned and the relevant operators, and shall evaluate the national measures taken. On the basis of the results of that evaluation, the Commission shall decide whether the measure is justified and, where necessary, propose other appropriate measures.

5. The Commission shall immediately communicate its decision to the Member States concerned and to the relevant operators. It shall also inform the other Member States.

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

82.summary_1: After an evaluation under Article 79 and consultation with the relevant national public authority (Article 77(1)), if a high-risk AI system is compliant but still presents health, safety, fundamental rights, or public-interest risks, the market surveillance authority must require the operator to take all appropriate measures to ensure the risk is eliminated without undue delay and within a prescribed period. The provider must ensure corrective action across all AI systems it has placed on the Union market within the period set by the authority. The Member States must immediately inform the Commission and other Member States with details (identification, origin and supply chain, nature of the risk, and duration of the measures); the Commission will consult with the Member States and operators, assess the measures, decide whether they are justified, potentially propose further actions, and communicate its decision to all concerned.
82.summary_2:
When, after an evaluation and consultation with the relevant national public authority, the market surveillance authority finds that a high-risk AI system complies with the Regulation but still poses a risk to health or safety, fundamental rights, or other public-interest aspects, it shall require the operator to take all appropriate measures to ensure the AI system, when placed on the market or put into service, no longer presents that risk. Action must be taken without undue delay and within a period prescribed by the authority.
The provider or other relevant operator must ensure corrective action is taken for all AI systems it has made available on the Union market within the timeline prescribed by the market surveillance authority.
Member States must immediately inform the Commission and other Member States of the finding, including all available details necessary to identify the AI system, its origin and supply chain, the nature of the risk, and the nature and duration of the measures taken.
The Commission shall, without undue delay, consult with the Member States concerned and the relevant operators, evaluate the national measures taken, and decide whether the measure is justified; where necessary, it may propose other appropriate measures.
The Commission shall immediately communicate its decision to the Member States concerned and to the relevant operators, and shall also inform the other Member States.

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

83.summary_1: Market surveillance authorities must require providers to remedy defined conformity failures for high-risk AI systems, including improper or missing CE markings, incorrect or missing EU declarations of conformity, missing EU database registration, lack of an authorised representative, or unavailable technical documentation. If the non-compliance persists, they must take proportionate measures to restrict or prohibit the system on the market and, if needed, recall or withdraw it without delay. These provisions enable enforcement of conformity obligations to safeguard compliance and market integrity.
83.summary_2:
If a market surveillance authority finds one of several forms of non-compliance by a high-risk AI system or its provider--such as incorrect or missing CE marking, missing or incorrect EU declaration of conformity, missing registration in the EU database, absence of an authorised representative where required, or unavailable technical documentation--it must require the provider to remedy the non-compliance within a period it prescribes.
If the non-compliance persists, the authority must take appropriate and proportionate measures to restrict or prohibit the system from being placed on the market, or to ensure its recall or withdrawal, without delay.

84.text:
1. The Commission shall designate one or more Union AI testing support structures to perform the tasks listed under Article 21(6) of Regulation (EU) 2019/1020 in the area of AI.

2. Without prejudice to the tasks referred to in paragraph 1, Union AI testing support structures shall also provide independent technical or scientific advice at the request of the Board, the Commission, or of market surveillance authorities.

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

84.summary_1: Union AI testing support structures will be designated to perform the AI testing tasks set out under the framework of Regulation (EU) 2019/1020. In addition, these structures shall provide independent technical or scientific advice upon request by the Board, the Commission, or market surveillance authorities. This arrangement ensures both the execution of testing tasks and access to expert guidance for oversight bodies.
84.summary_2: The Commission designates one or more Union AI testing support structures to perform the tasks listed in Article 21(6) of Regulation (EU) 2019/1020 in the AI domain. In addition to those tasks, the structures shall provide independent technical or scientific advice upon request by the Board, the Commission, or market surveillance authorities, without prejudice to the tasks in paragraph 1.

85.unit_title: Right to lodge a complaint with a market surveillance authority
85.text:
Without prejudice to other administrative or judicial remedies, any natural or legal person having grounds to consider that there has been an infringement of the provisions of this Regulation may submit complaints to the relevant market surveillance authority.

In accordance with Regulation (EU) 2019/1020, such complaints shall be taken into account for the purpose of conducting market surveillance activities, and shall be handled in line with the dedicated procedures established therefor by the market surveillance authorities.

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

85.summary_1: The article provides that any natural or legal person who believes there has been an infringement may lodge a complaint with the relevant market surveillance authority, without prejudice to other remedies. Complaints must be taken into account for market surveillance activities under the regulation. They are to be handled in accordance with Regulation (EU) 2019/1020 and the procedures established by market surveillance authorities.
85.summary_2: Any natural or legal person who believes there has been an infringement of this Regulation may file a complaint with the relevant market surveillance authority, without prejudice to other administrative or judicial remedies. Such complaints must be taken into account for market surveillance purposes and shall be processed in accordance with the dedicated procedures established by the market surveillance authorities under Regulation (EU) 2019/1020.

86.unit_title: Right to explanation of individual decision-making
86.text:
1. Any affected person subject to a decision which is taken by the deployer on the basis of the output from a high-risk AI system listed in Annex III, with the exception of systems listed under point 2 thereof, and which produces legal effects or similarly significantly affects that person in a way that they consider to have an adverse impact on their health, safety or fundamental rights shall have the right to obtain from the deployer clear and meaningful explanations of the role of the AI system in the decision-making procedure and the main elements of the decision taken.

2. Paragraph 1 shall not apply to the use of AI systems for which exceptions from, or restrictions to, the obligation under that paragraph follow from Union or national law in compliance with Union law.

3. This Article shall apply only to the extent that the right referred to in paragraph 1 is not otherwise provided for under Union law.

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

86.summary_1: Affected persons have a right to clear and meaningful explanations from the deployer about the role of a high-risk AI system in the decision-making process and the main elements of the decision, for systems listed in Annex III that produce legal effects or significantly affect health, safety, or fundamental rights. This right does not apply to uses of AI that are exempt or restricted by Union or national law in line with Union law. It applies only to the extent the right is not otherwise provided by Union law.
86.summary_2: An affected person subject to a decision made by the deployer based on the output of a high-risk AI system listed in Annex III (excluding systems in point 2) that has legal effects or significantly affects them in a way that adversely impacts health, safety, or fundamental rights shall have the right to clear and meaningful explanations of the AI's role in the decision-making process and the main elements of the decision. The right does not apply to AI systems whose use is exempted or restricted by Union or national law in compliance with Union law. The applicability of this right is limited to the extent that it is not already provided for under Union law.

87.unit_title: Reporting of infringements and protection of reporting persons
87.text: Directive (EU) 2019/1937 shall apply to the reporting of infringements of this Regulation and the protection of persons reporting such infringements.

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

87.summary_1: Reporting of infringements of the Regulation and protection of reporting persons are governed by Directive (EU) 2019/1937. This ensures that the reporting process and whistleblower protections under that directive apply to this Regulation.
87.summary_2: Directive 2019/1937 applies to reporting infringements of this Regulation and to the protection of persons reporting such infringements, thereby importing its whistleblowing rights, procedures, and anti-retaliation protections into this Regulation.

88.unit_title: Enforcement of the obligations of providers of general-purpose AI models
88.text:
1. The Commission shall have exclusive powers to supervise and enforce Chapter V, taking into account the procedural guarantees under Article 94. The Commission shall entrust the implementation of these tasks to the AI Office, without prejudice to the powers of organisation of the Commission and the division of competences between Member States and the Union based on the Treaties.

2. Without prejudice to Article 75(3), market surveillance authorities may request the Commission to exercise the powers laid down in this Section, where that is necessary and proportionate to assist with the fulfilment of their tasks under this Regulation.

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

88.summary_1: The Commission has exclusive power to supervise and enforce Chapter V's general-purpose AI model obligations, with enforcement tasks delegated to the AI Office and subject to procedural guarantees under Article 94. It preserves the distribution of organizational and competences powers between Member States and the Union as defined by the Treaties. Market surveillance authorities may request the Commission to exercise the powers in this Section when necessary and proportionate to support enforcement, subject to Article 75(3).
88.summary_2:
The Commission holds exclusive authority to supervise and enforce Chapter V, while respecting the procedural guarantees set out in the Regulation. The day-to-day implementation of these tasks is delegated to the AI Office, and this delegation does not alter the Commission's organizational prerogatives or the distribution of competences between Member States and the Union as laid down in the Treaties.
Market surveillance authorities may request the Commission to exercise the powers described in this Section when necessary and proportionate to aid in fulfilling their tasks under the Regulation. This mechanism operates without prejudice to the existing rules governing such requests under the Regulation.

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

89.summary_1: The AI Office may monitor compliance by providers of general-purpose AI models, including adherence to approved codes of practice, to ensure effective implementation of the Regulation. Downstream providers have the right to lodge a reasoned complaint about infringements, detailing contact information, relevant facts, the applicable provisions, and any other pertinent information. This provision establishes the complaint mechanism as part of the enforcement framework.
89.summary_2:
The AI Office is empowered to take necessary monitoring actions to ensure the effective implementation and compliance of providers of general-purpose AI models with the Regulation, including their adherence to approved codes of practice. This enables enforcement and oversight of how GP AI models are deployed and governed.
Downstream providers have the right to lodge a complaint alleging a Regulation infringement. Complaints must be duly reasoned and include: (i) the contact point for the GP AI model provider concerned; (ii) a description of the relevant facts, the provisions implicated, and why infringement is claimed; and (iii) any other information the complainant considers relevant, including data gathered independently.

90.unit_title: Alerts of systemic risks by the scientific panel
90.text:
1. The scientific panel may provide a qualified alert to the AI Office where it has reason to suspect that:
(a) a general-purpose AI model poses concrete identifiable risk at Union level; or
(b) a general-purpose AI model meets the conditions referred to in Article 51.

2. Upon such qualified alert, the Commission, through the AI Office and after having informed the Board, may exercise the powers laid down in this Section for the purpose of assessing the matter. The AI Office shall inform the Board of any measure according to Articles 91 to 94.

3. A qualified alert shall be duly reasoned and indicate at least:
(a) the point of contact of the provider of the general-purpose AI model with systemic risk concerned;
(b) a description of the relevant facts and the reasons for the alert by the scientific panel;
(c) any other information that the scientific panel considers to be relevant, including, where appropriate, information gathered on its own initiative.

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

90.summary_1: The scientific panel may issue a qualified alert to the AI Office if it suspects that a general-purpose AI model poses a concrete risk at the Union level or meets the conditions in Article 51. If such an alert is issued, the Commission, through the AI Office and after informing the Board, may exercise the powers in this Section to assess the matter, with the AI Office informing the Board of any measures under Articles 91–94. A qualified alert must be reasoned and include the provider's contact point, a description of the relevant facts and reasons for the alert, and any other information the panel deems relevant.
90.summary_2:
The scientific panel may issue a qualified alert to the AI Office if it has reason to suspect that a general-purpose AI model (GP AI) either poses a concrete identifiable risk at the Union level or meets the systemic-risk conditions described in Article 51. This establishes a trigger for supervisory action based on potential risks or high-impact criteria.
Following a qualified alert, the Commission, via the AI Office and after informing the Board, may exercise the powers established in the relevant section to assess the matter. The AI Office is required to keep the Board informed of any measures taken under Articles 91 to 94, ensuring governance and oversight throughout the assessment process.
To be valid, a qualified alert must be duly reasoned and include at least: (a) the point of contact for the GP AI model provider associated with the systemic risk; (b) a description of the relevant facts and the reasons for the alert; and (c) any other information the scientific panel deems relevant, including information it has gathered independently.

91.unit_title: Power to request documentation and information
91.text:
1. The Commission may request the provider of the general-purpose AI model concerned to provide the documentation drawn up by the provider in accordance with Articles 53 and 55, or any additional information that is necessary for the purpose of assessing compliance of the provider with this Regulation.

2. Before sending the request for information, the AI Office may initiate a structured dialogue with the provider of the general-purpose AI model.

3. Upon a duly substantiated request from the scientific panel, the Commission may issue a request for information to a provider of a general-purpose AI model, where the access to information is necessary and proportionate for the fulfilment of the tasks of the scientific panel under Article 68(2).

4. The request for information shall state the legal basis and the purpose of the request, specify what information is required, set a period within which the information is to be provided, and indicate the fines provided for in Article 101 for supplying incorrect, incomplete or misleading information.

5. The provider of the general-purpose AI model concerned, or its representative shall supply the information requested. In the case of legal persons, companies or firms, or where the provider has no legal personality, the persons authorised to represent them by law or by their statutes, shall supply the information requested on behalf of the provider of the general-purpose AI model concerned. Lawyers duly authorised to act may supply information on behalf of their clients. The clients shall nevertheless remain fully responsible if the information supplied is incomplete, incorrect or misleading.

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

91.summary_1: The article authorizes the Commission to require documentation and information from providers of general-purpose AI models to assess compliance, including documentation produced under Articles 53 and 55 or other necessary data. It allows the AI Office to initiate a structured dialogue with the provider before formal requests. It also requires information requests—whether by the Commission on a substantiated panel request or to fulfill the panel’s tasks—to state the legal basis, purpose, information required, deadline, and penalties under Article 101 for false or incomplete data, with providers and their authorized representatives obliged to supply accurate information.
91.summary_2:
The authority may require the provider of a general-purpose AI model to supply documentation prepared by the provider under Articles 53 and 55, or any additional information necessary to assess compliance with the Regulation. Before issuing such a information request, the AI Office may engage in a structured dialogue with the provider.
Upon a duly substantiated request from the scientific panel, the Commission may issue a request for information to a provider when access to information is necessary and proportionate for the panel's tasks.
The information request must specify the legal basis and purpose, detail what information is required, set a deadline for provision, and indicate the fines provided for supplying incorrect, incomplete, or misleading information.
The provider or its representative must supply the requested information. In the case of legal persons, entities, or those with no legal personality, those legally authorized to represent them must supply the information, and lawyers may supply on behalf of their clients. The clients remain fully responsible for any incomplete, incorrect, or misleading information.

92.text:
1. The AI Office, after consulting the Board, may conduct evaluations of the general-purpose AI model concerned:
(a) to assess compliance of the provider with obligations under this Regulation, where the information gathered pursuant to Article 91 is insufficient; or
(b) to investigate systemic risks at Union level of general-purpose AI models with systemic risk, in particular following a qualified alert from the scientific panel in accordance with Article 90(1), point (a).

2. The Commission may decide to appoint independent experts to carry out evaluations on its behalf, including from the scientific panel established pursuant to Article 68. Independent experts appointed for this task shall meet the criteria outlined in Article 68(2).

3. For the purposes of paragraph 1, the Commission may request access to the general-purpose AI model concerned through APIs or further appropriate technical means and tools, including source code.

4. The request for access shall state the legal basis, the purpose and reasons of the request and set the period within which the access is to be provided, and the fines provided for in Article 101 for failure to provide access.

5. The providers of the general-purpose AI model concerned or its representative shall supply the information requested. In the case of legal persons, companies or firms, or where the provider has no legal personality, the persons authorised to represent them by law or by their statutes, shall provide the access requested on behalf of the provider of the general-purpose AI model concerned.

6. The Commission shall adopt implementing acts setting out the detailed arrangements and the conditions for the evaluations, including the detailed arrangements for involving independent experts, and the procedure for the selection thereof. Those implementing acts shall be adopted in accordance with the examination procedure referred to in Article 98(2).

7. Prior to requesting access to the general-purpose AI model concerned, the AI Office may initiate a structured dialogue with the provider of the general-purpose AI model to gather more information on the internal testing of the model, internal safeguards for preventing systemic risks, and other internal procedures and measures the provider has taken to mitigate such risks.

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

92.summary_1: Empowers the AI Office, after consulting the Board, to evaluate a general-purpose AI model to check provider compliance or to investigate systemic Union-level risks, including after a qualified alert from the scientific panel. The Commission may appoint independent experts (including from the scientific panel) to conduct evaluations, with access granted via APIs or other technical means (including source code) and penalties under Article 101 for noncompliance; implementing acts will set detailed arrangements under Article 98(2). Before access is requested, the AI Office may hold a structured dialogue with the provider to understand internal testing and risk-mitigation measures.
92.summary_2:
The AI Office, after consulting the Board, may conduct evaluations of a general-purpose AI model to verify compliance with the Regulation where information from other sources is insufficient, or to investigate systemic Union-level risks, particularly after a qualified alert by the scientific panel. These evaluations can target compliance by the provider or assess broader risks associated with the model.
The Commission may appoint independent experts to perform evaluations on its behalf, including experts drawn from the scientific panel. The appointed experts must meet the independence, expertise, and related criteria established in the governing rules.
To carry out evaluations, the Commission may request access to the general-purpose AI model through APIs or other suitable technical means, including access to source code. Access requests must specify the legal basis, the purpose and reasons, a deadline, and the penalties for non-compliance with access obligations.
Providers or their authorized representatives must supply the requested access and information. If the provider is a legal entity, those authorized to represent it by law or statute must provide access on its behalf. The Commission will adopt implementing acts detailing the arrangements for evaluations, including involvement and selection of independent experts, to be issued under the prescribed examination procedure.
Before requesting access, the AI Office may initiate a structured dialogue with the provider to gather information on internal testing, safeguards against systemic risks, and other internal measures taken to mitigate risks.

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

93.summary_1: The Commission may require providers to take measures to comply with Articles 53 and 54, implement mitigation measures for systemic Union-level risk, or restrict/withdraw a model. Before a measure is requested, the AI Office may initiate a structured dialogue with the provider. If the provider offers commitments during that dialogue to mitigate a systemic risk, the Commission may make those commitments binding by decision and conclude there are no grounds for further action.
93.summary_2:
The Commission may require providers to (i) take appropriate measures to comply with Articles 53 and 54, (ii) implement mitigation measures if an evaluation under Article 92 reveals serious and substantiated concerns of a systemic Union-level risk, and (iii) restrict the model's market availability, withdraw it, or recall it, where such action is necessary and appropriate.
Before any measure is requested, the AI Office may initiate a structured dialogue with the provider of the general-purpose AI model.
If, during that dialogue, the provider offers commitments to mitigate the systemic risk at Union level, the Commission may, by decision, make those commitments binding and declare that there are no further grounds for action.

94.unit_title: Procedural rights of economic operators of the general-purpose AI model
94.text: Article 18 of Regulation (EU) 2019/1020 shall apply mutatis mutandis to the providers of the general-purpose AI model, without prejudice to more specific procedural rights provided for in this Regulation.

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

94.summary_1: It provides that the procedural rights of providers of general-purpose AI models are governed by Article 18 of Regulation (EU) 2019/1020, applied mutatis mutandis. This is without prejudice to the more specific procedural rights laid out in this Regulation. In short, providers' procedures are harmonized with the 2019/1020 framework, while this Regulation may grant additional rights.
94.summary_2: Procedural rights applicable to providers of general-purpose AI models are to be drawn from Regulation (EU) 2019/1020 and applied with the necessary modifications to fit the context of AI model providers. These rights are to be understood mutatis mutandis, i.e., in substance but tailored as appropriate. They are provided without prejudice to any more specific procedural rights established elsewhere in this Regulation, which would take precedence if they overlap.

95.unit_title: Codes of conduct for voluntary application of specific requirements

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

95.summary_1: The AI Office and Member States are directed to promote and facilitate voluntary codes of conduct that apply to AI systems that are not high-risk, incorporating Chapter III, Section 2 requirements and current technical and industry practices. The rules cover voluntary applications for all AI systems, based on clear objectives and KPIs, including alignment with Union ethical guidelines for trustworthy AI, energy-efficient design, AI literacy, inclusive design, and protection of vulnerable groups. Codes may be developed by providers, deployers, or their representative bodies, with stakeholder involvement, and SMEs and start-ups' needs must be considered.

need_ref.value: Union ethical guidelines for trustworthy AI

95.summary_2:
The provision establishes a framework to promote voluntary codes of conduct for AI systems that are not high-risk. The AI Office and the Member States must actively encourage and facilitate the development of such codes, including governance mechanisms, to enable the voluntary application of the relevant requirements in Chapter III, Section 2, taking into account current technical solutions and industry best practices.
In addition, the AI Office and Member States should facilitate codes concerning the voluntary application of specific requirements to all AI systems, guided by clear objectives and measurable indicators (KPIs). These should cover elements such as adherence to Union ethical guidelines for trustworthy AI, environmental sustainability (including energy-efficient programming and design/training/use), AI literacy, inclusive and diverse design with stakeholder participation, and the prevention of negative impacts on vulnerable groups, including accessibility and gender equality.
Codes of conduct may be drafted by individual providers or deployers, or by organizations representing them, or by both, and may apply to one or more AI systems depending on the similarity of their intended purpose. The development process should involve interested stakeholders and their representative organizations, including civil society and academia.
The AI Office and the Member States must take into account the specific interests and needs of SMEs, including start-ups, when encouraging and facilitating the drawing up of codes of conduct.

96.unit_title: Guidelines from the Commission on the implementation of this Regulation

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

96.summary_1: The Commission is empowered to issue guidelines on the practical implementation of the Regulation, covering the application of requirements in Articles 8 to 15 and Article 25, the prohibited practices in Article 5, the practical handling of substantial modification, transparency obligations in Article 50, and the relationship with Annex I harmonisation legislation and other Union law, including the definition of an AI system in Article 3(1). Guidelines must consider the needs of SMEs and start-ups, local authorities, and affected sectors, and reflect the current state of the art and relevant harmonised standards (Articles 40 and 41). The Commission must update guidelines upon request by Member States or the AI Office or on its own initiative.
96.summary_2:
The Commission will issue practical guidelines to facilitate the implementation of the Regulation. These guidelines will address how to apply the Regulation's core requirements and obligations, guidance on prohibited practices, how to operationalize substantial modification rules, how transparency duties should be fulfilled, and how the Regulation interacts with Union harmonisation legislation and other Union law to ensure consistent enforcement. They will also clarify how the AI system definition is to be applied in practice.
The guidelines must align with the generally acknowledged state of the art and reference harmonised standards and common specifications cited in the Regulation, including any that arise under Union harmonisation law, to support coherent and technically sound application.
Special attention shall be given to the needs of SMEs and start-ups, local public authorities, and sectors most likely to be affected by the Regulation, ensuring guidance is accessible and practically usable across diverse contexts.
The Commission may update these guidelines at the request of Member States or the AI Office, or on its own initiative, whenever it considers such updates necessary.

97.text:
1. The power to adopt delegated acts is conferred on the Commission subject to the conditions laid down in this Article.

2. The power to adopt delegated acts referred to in Article 6(6) and (7), Article 7(1) and (3), Article 11(3), Article 43(5) and (6), Article 47(5), Article 51(3), Article 52(4) and Article 53(5) and (6) shall be conferred on the Commission for a period of five years from 1 August 2024. The Commission shall draw up a report in respect of the delegation of power not later than nine months before the end of the five-year period. The delegation of power shall be tacitly extended for periods of an identical duration, unless the European Parliament or the Council opposes such extension not later than three months before the end of each period.

3. The delegation of power referred to in Article 6(6) and (7), Article 7(1) and (3), Article 11(3), Article 43(5) and (6), Article 47(5), Article 51(3), Article 52(4) and Article 53(5) and (6) may be revoked at any time by the European Parliament or by the Council. A decision of revocation shall put an end to the delegation of power specified in that decision. It shall take effect the day following that of its publication in the Official Journal of the European Union or at a later date specified therein. It shall not affect the validity of any delegated acts already in force.

4. Before adopting a delegated act, the Commission shall consult experts designated by each Member State in accordance with the principles laid down in the Interinstitutional Agreement of 13 April 2016 on Better Law-Making.

5. As soon as it adopts a delegated act, the Commission shall notify it simultaneously to the European Parliament and to the Council.

6. Any delegated act adopted pursuant to Article 6(6) or (7), Article 7(1) or (3), Article 11(3), Article 43(5) or (6), Article 47(5), Article 51(3), Article 52(4) or Article 53(5) or (6) shall enter into force only if no objection has been expressed by either the European Parliament or the Council within a period of three months of notification of that act to the European Parliament and the Council or if, before the expiry of that period, the European Parliament and the Council have both informed the Commission that they will not object. That period shall be extended by three months at the initiative of the European Parliament or of the Council.

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

97.summary_1: Establishes the Commission's power to adopt delegated acts subject to a five-year term from 1 August 2024, with tacit extension unless Parliament or the Council objects. Requires consultation of experts designated by Member States under the 2016 Interinstitutional Agreement on Better Law-Making and notification to Parliament and the Council when a delegated act is adopted. Delegated acts enter into force only if neither Parliament nor the Council objects within three months (extendable by three months), and can be revoked by either institution.

need_ref.value: Interinstitutional Agreement of 13 April 2016 on Better Law-Making
Official Journal of the European Union

97.summary_2:
The Commission is empowered to adopt delegated acts for a defined set of provisions for a five-year period starting on 1 August 2024. This delegation may be tacitly extended for successive periods of the same length unless the European Parliament or the Council oppos(es) the extension at least three months before the end of the current period.
The delegation may be revoked at any time by the European Parliament or by the Council. A revocation ends the delegation from the date of publication or a later date specified in the decision and does not affect the validity of delegated acts already in force.
Before adopting any delegated act, the Commission must consult experts designated by each Member State in accordance with the Better Law-Making framework.
Once a delegated act is adopted, the Commission must notify Parliament and the Council simultaneously.
A delegated act takes effect only if neither Parliament nor the Council objects within three months of notification, or if both indicate that they will not object before the period expires. The three-month period may be extended by three months at the initiative of either the European Parliament or the Council.

98.text:
1. The Commission shall be assisted by a committee. That committee shall be a committee within the meaning of Regulation (EU) No 182/2011.

2. Where reference is made to this paragraph, Article 5 of Regulation (EU) No 182/2011 shall apply.

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

98.summary_1: The Commission is to be assisted by a committee defined under Regulation (EU) No 182/2011. Where reference is made to this paragraph, Article 5 of Regulation (EU) No 182/2011 applies. The provision thus ties the committee's operation to the comitology framework established in Regulation 182/2011.
98.summary_2: The Commission is assisted by a committee that is constituted under Regulation (EU) No 182/2011. When this paragraph is invoked, the committee's procedure is governed by Article 5 of Regulation (EU) No 182/2011.

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

99.summary_1: Establishes that Member States must set penalties and enforcement measures for infringements of the Regulation, ensuring penalties are effective, proportionate, and dissuasive, with special consideration for SMEs and start-ups. It defines maximum administrative fines for non-compliance with AI prohibitions (up to €35,000,000 or 7% of worldwide annual turnover) and for breaches of listed provider/obligations, or for providing incorrect information (up to €7,500,000 or 1% turnover), and prescribes procedural safeguards and Commission guidelines under Article 96. It also requires annual reporting on fines and related litigation and provides criteria for determining fines, including consideration of the nature and scope of the infringement and the operator's size and cooperation.
99.summary_2:
Member States must establish penalties and enforcement measures for infringements of this Regulation, including warnings and non-monetary remedies, and ensure they are implemented effectively in line with Commission guidelines. Penalties should be effective, proportionate, and dissuasive, with particular regard to the interests and economic viability of SMEs and start-ups.
National authorities must notify the Commission of the penalty rules and any amendments before or on entry into application. Fines for non-compliance with the prohibitions on high-risk AI practices can reach up to €35,000,000 or 7% of worldwide annual turnover, whichever is higher. For non-compliance with core operator- and notifier-related obligations (providers, authorised representatives, importers, distributors, deployers, and related bodies, including transparency duties), penalties can reach up to €15,000,000 or 3% of worldwide turnover, whichever is higher. Penalties for providing incorrect or misleading information to authorities can reach up to €7,500,000 or 1% of turnover, with lower amounts applying to SMEs.
When determining and sizing fines, authorities must consider factors such as the nature, gravity, and duration of the infringement and its consequences; prior fines by other authorities; the operator's size and market position; aggravating or mitigating circumstances; cooperation with authorities; the operator's responsibility and measures taken to mitigate harm; and whether the infringement was intentional. Public authorities may be subject to separate rules on administrative fines, and applicable procedural safeguards, including due process, must be respected.
There is an annual reporting obligation requiring Member States to inform the Commission of the administrative fines issued and related litigation. The framework allows for a range of enforcement modalities beyond monetary penalties, and ensures consistent application of penalties across the Union while preserving procedural rights.

100.unit_title: Administrative fines on Union institutions, bodies, offices and agencies

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

100.summary_1: Allows the European Data Protection Supervisor to impose administrative fines on Union institutions for AI-related infringements, with two tier caps: up to EUR 1,500,000 for prohibitions under Article 5 and up to EUR 750,000 for other obligations. Fines are guided by factors such as the nature, gravity and duration of the infringement, responsibility, mitigation efforts, cooperation, prior infringements, and awareness of the issue, and are subject to due process including a hearing and defence rights. Collected fines go to the general Union budget, and the EDPS reports annually to the Commission on fines and related litigation.
100.summary_2:
The European Data Protection Supervisor may impose administrative fines on Union institutions, bodies, offices and agencies for breaches under this Regulation, with the amount and decision guided by the specific circumstances of each case. The factors to be considered include the nature, gravity and duration of the infringement and its consequences (including the AI system's purpose and the number of affected persons and the damage suffered); the level of responsibility and any technical or organizational measures implemented; actions taken to mitigate damage; the degree of cooperation with the EDPS to remedy the infringement; any prior similar infringements; how the infringement became known and whether it was notified; and the annual budget of the institution involved. The assessment may also take into account compliance with measures previously ordered by the EDPS on the same subject matter.
Non-compliance with the prohibition of AI practices within this Regulation may attract administrative fines of up to 1,500,000 euros.
Non-compliance with any other requirements or obligations under the Regulation (other than the prohibition) may attract administrative fines of up to 750,000 euros.
Before issuing decisions, the EDPS must offer the implicated Union body the opportunity to be heard on the potential infringement, and the EDPS must base its decisions only on elements that have been commented on by the parties. Complainants, if any, should be closely associated with the proceedings, and the rights of defense of the parties must be fully respected, including access to the EDPS file subject to legitimate protections for personal data and business secrets.
Fines collected under this provision are to contribute to the general EU budget and must not impair the effective operation of the fined institution. The EDPS must annually inform the Commission of the administrative fines imposed and of any ongoing litigation or judicial proceedings.

101.unit_title: Fines for providers of general-purpose AI models
101.text:
1. The Commission may impose on providers of general-purpose AI models fines not exceeding 3 % of their annual total worldwide turnover in the preceding financial year or EUR 15000000, whichever is higher., when the Commission finds that the provider intentionally or negligently:
(a) infringed the relevant provisions of this Regulation;
(b) failed to comply with a request for a document or for information pursuant to Article 91, or supplied incorrect, incomplete or misleading information;
(c) failed to comply with a measure requested under Article 93;
