(d) failed to make available to the Commission access to the general-purpose AI model or general-purpose AI model with systemic risk with a view to conducting an evaluation pursuant to Article 92.
In fixing the amount of the fine or periodic penalty payment, regard shall be had to the nature, gravity and duration of the infringement, taking due account of the principles of proportionality and appropriateness. The Commission shall also into account commitments made in accordance with Article 93(3) or made in relevant codes of practice in accordance with Article 56.

2. Before adopting the decision pursuant to paragraph 1, the Commission shall communicate its preliminary findings to the provider of the general-purpose AI model and give it an opportunity to be heard.

3. Fines imposed in accordance with this Article shall be effective, proportionate and dissuasive.

4. Information on fines imposed under this Article shall also be communicated to the Board as appropriate.

5. The Court of Justice of the European Union shall have unlimited jurisdiction to review decisions of the Commission fixing a fine under this Article. It may cancel, reduce or increase the fine imposed.

6. The Commission shall adopt implementing acts containing detailed arrangements and procedural safeguards for proceedings in view of the possible adoption of decisions pursuant to paragraph 1 of this Article. Those implementing acts shall be adopted in accordance with the examination procedure referred to in Article 98(2).

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

101.summary_1: The Commission may impose fines on providers of general-purpose AI models up to 3% of their annual worldwide turnover or EUR 15,000,000 for intentional or negligent infringements, failure to comply with information requests, failure to comply with measures, or failure to grant access for evaluation. Before finalizing the decision, the Commission must share preliminary findings with the provider and offer a hearing. Fines must be effective, proportionate and dissuasive, with implementing acts under Article 98(2 detailing procedures and possible review by the Court of Justice.
101.summary_2:
The enforcement framework allows the Commission to impose significant fines on providers of general-purpose AI models for intentional or negligent violations of the Regulation, failure to comply with information or document requests, failure to implement required measures, or failure to grant access for evaluations. The fines may reach up to 3% of the provider's annual worldwide turnover or €15,000,000, whichever is higher, with the amount determined by the nature, gravity, and duration of the infringement and taking into account proportionality, appropriateness, and any commitments or codes of practice relevant to the case.
Before a decision imposing a fine is issued, the Commission must share its preliminary findings with the provider and provide an opportunity to be heard.
Fines under this framework must be effective, proportionate, and dissuasive. Information about any fines may also be communicated to the Board as appropriate.
The Court of Justice exercises unlimited jurisdiction to review Commission decisions on fines and may cancel, reduce, or increase the amount. The Commission must adopt implementing acts that set out detailed arrangements and procedural safeguards for proceedings, to be adopted through the required examining procedure.

102.text:
In Article 4(3) of Regulation (EC) No 300/2008, the following subparagraph is added:
'When adopting detailed measures related to technical specifications and procedures for approval and use of security equipment concerning Artificial Intelligence systems within the meaning of Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council(note: 58), the requirements set out in Chapter III, Section 2, of that Regulation shall be taken into account.

notes.58: Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 13 June 2024 laying down harmonised rules on artificial intelligence and amending Regulations (EC) No 300/2008, (EU) No 167/2013, (EU) No 168/2013, (EU) 2018/858, (EU) 2018/1139 and (EU) 2019/2144 and Directives 2014/90/EU, (EU) 2016/797 and (EU) 2020/1828 (Artificial Intelligence Act) (OJ L, 2024/1689, 12.7.2024, ELI: http://data.europa.eu/eli/reg/2024/1689/oj).'.

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

102.summary_1: Amends Regulation (EC) No 300/2008 to require alignment with the AI-related security equipment rules set out in Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 when adopting detailed measures for technical specifications and procedures for approval and use of such equipment. Specifically, it adds that the requirements in Chapter III, Section 2 of Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 must be taken into account. This ensures consistency with the AI governance framework across both regulations.
102.summary_2:
This amendment adds a new subparagraph to Article 4(3) of Regulation (EC) No 300/2008. It requires that when adopting detailed measures on technical specifications and procedures for the approval and use of security equipment related to AI systems (as defined in Regulation (EU) 2024/1689), the requirements in Chapter III, Section 2 of Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 must be taken into account.
The provision creates an alignment obligation, ensuring that any security equipment associated with AI systems adheres to the governance framework established in Regulation (EU) 2024/1689, specifically its Chapter III, Section 2.
Practically, the obligation is procedural: those drafting or implementing the measures must consider, and presumably harmonize with, the requirements of Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 Chapter III, Section 2 when establishing technical specs and approval/use procedures for AI-related security equipment. It does not itself impose new direct duties on operators beyond this mandated consideration.
In sum, the amendment ensures coherence between the AI-security equipment regime and the existing security equipment framework by mandating consideration of Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 Chapter III, Section 2 in the adoption of detailed measures.

103.text:
In Article 17(5) of Regulation (EU) No 167/2013, the following subparagraph is added:
'When adopting delegated acts pursuant to the first subparagraph concerning artificial intelligence systems which are safety components within the meaning of Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council(note: 59), the requirements set out in Chapter III, Section 2, of that Regulation shall be taken into account.

notes.59: Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 13 June 2024 laying down harmonised rules on artificial intelligence and amending Regulations (EC) No 300/2008, (EU) No 167/2013, (EU) No 168/2013, (EU) 2018/858, (EU) 2018/1139 and (EU) 2019/2144 and Directives 2014/90/EU, (EU) 2016/797 and (EU) 2020/1828 (Artificial Intelligence Act) (OJ L, 2024/1689, 12.7.2024, ELI: http://data.europa.eu/eli/reg/2024/1689/oj).'.

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

103.summary_1: Adds that when adopting delegated acts concerning AI systems that are safety components, the requirements of Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 Chapter III, Section 2 must be taken into account. This ensures alignment with the AI governance framework across the two regulations. It promotes consistent application of AI safety standards in delegated acts.
103.summary_2: An amendment to Regulation (EU) No 167/2013 adds a new subparagraph to Article 17(5). It provides that when delegated acts are adopted under the first subparagraph concerning artificial intelligence systems that are safety components under Regulation (EU) 2024/1689, the requirements in Chapter III, Section 2, of Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 must be taken into account.

104.text:
In Article 22(5) of Regulation (EU) No 168/2013, the following subparagraph is added:
'When adopting delegated acts pursuant to the first subparagraph concerning Artificial Intelligence systems which are safety components within the meaning of Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council(note: 60), the requirements set out in Chapter III, Section 2, of that Regulation shall be taken into account.

notes.60: Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 13 June 2024 laying down harmonised rules on artificial intelligence and amending Regulations (EC) No 300/2008, (EU) No 167/2013, (EU) No 168/2013, (EU) 2018/858, (EU) 2018/1139 and (EU) 2019/2144 and Directives 2014/90/EU, (EU) 2016/797 and (EU) 2020/1828 (Artificial Intelligence Act) (OJ L, 2024/1689, 12.7.2024, ELI: http://data.europa.eu/eli/reg/2024/1689/oj).'.

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

104.summary_1: Amendment to Regulation (EU) No 168/2013 requires that when adopting delegated acts for AI systems that are safety components, the requirements in Regulation (EU) 2024/1689, Chapter III, Section 2, must be taken into account. This ensures alignment with the AI governance framework across EU safety-regulation provisions.
104.summary_2: This amendment requires that when delegated acts are adopted under Article 22(5) of Regulation 168/2013 concerning Artificial Intelligence systems that are safety components (as defined in Regulation 2024/1689), the requirements laid out in Regulation 2024/1689, Chapter III, Section 2, must be taken into account. In effect, act-drafters must ensure that such delegated acts align with and reflect the safety framework for AI safety components established in Regulation 2024/1689. This creates a harmonized approach to regulating AI safety components across the two regulations by mandating consideration of the specified Chapter III, Section 2 requirements.

105.text:
In Article 8 of Directive 2014/90/EU, the following paragraph is added:
'5. For Artificial Intelligence systems which are safety components within the meaning of Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council(note: 61), when carrying out its activities pursuant to paragraph 1 and when adopting technical specifications and testing standards in accordance with paragraphs 2 and 3, the Commission shall take into account the requirements set out in Chapter III, Section 2, of that Regulation.

notes.61: Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 13 June 2024 laying down harmonised rules on artificial intelligence and amending Regulations (EC) No 300/2008, (EU) No 167/2013, (EU) No 168/2013, (EU) 2018/858, (EU) 2018/1139 and (EU) 2019/2144 and Directives 2014/90/EU, (EU) 2016/797 and (EU) 2020/1828 (Artificial Intelligence Act) (OJ L, 2024/1689, 12.7.2024, ELI: http://data.europa.eu/eli/reg/2024/1689/oj).'.

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

105.summary_1: Amends Directive 2014/90/EU to require that for AI systems that are safety components, the Commission, when setting technical specifications and testing standards, must take into account the requirements in Chapter III, Section 2 of Regulation (EU) 2024/1689, ensuring alignment of AI safety governance across regulations.
105.summary_2: For AI systems that qualify as safety components under Regulation 2024/1689, the Commission, in carrying out its duties under the applicable paragraph and when adopting technical specifications and testing standards under the subsequent paragraphs, must take into account the requirements set out in Chapter III, Section 2 of that Regulation, ensuring alignment of safety and risk-management standards across the instruments.

106.text:
In Article 5 of Directive (EU) 2016/797, the following paragraph is added:
'12. When adopting delegated acts pursuant to paragraph 1 and implementing acts pursuant to paragraph 11 concerning Artificial Intelligence systems which are safety components within the meaning of Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council(note: 62), the requirements set out in Chapter III, Section 2, of that Regulation shall be taken into account.

notes.62: Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 13 June 2024 laying down harmonised rules on artificial intelligence and amending Regulations (EC) No 300/2008, (EU) No 167/2013, (EU) No 168/2013, (EU) 2018/858, (EU) 2018/1139 and (EU) 2019/2144 and Directives 2014/90/EU, (EU) 2016/797 and (EU) 2020/1828 (Artificial Intelligence Act) (OJ L, 2024/1689, 12.7.2024, ELI: http://data.europa.eu/eli/reg/2024/1689/oj).'.

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

106.summary_1: Amends Directive (EU) 2016/797 to add a new paragraph requiring that, when adopting delegated and implementing acts concerning AI safety components as defined in Regulation (EU) 2024/1689, the provisions of Chapter III, Section 2 of that Regulation are to be followed. This creates cross-regulatory alignment for AI safety governance across the two instruments.
106.summary_2: When adopting delegated acts pursuant to paragraph 1 and implementing acts pursuant to paragraph 11 concerning Artificial Intelligence systems that are safety components within the meaning of Regulation (EU) 2024/1689, the requirements set out in Chapter III, Section 2, of that Regulation shall be taken into account. This ensures that the governance and safety obligations for AI safety components are incorporated into the act-making process under Directive (EU) 2016/797, aligning those acts with the EU-wide AI safety framework.

107.text:
In Article 5 of Regulation (EU) 2018/858 the following paragraph is added:
'4. When adopting delegated acts pursuant to paragraph 3 concerning Artificial Intelligence systems which are safety components within the meaning of Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council(note: 63), the requirements set out in Chapter III, Section 2, of that Regulation shall be taken into account.

notes.63: Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 13 June 2024 laying down harmonised rules on artificial intelligence and amending Regulations (EC) No 300/2008, (EU) No 167/2013, (EU) No 168/2013, (EU) 2018/858, (EU) 2018/1139 and (EU) 2019/2144 and Directives 2014/90/EU, (EU) 2016/797 and (EU) 2020/1828 (Artificial Intelligence Act) (OJ L, 2024/1689, 12.7.2024, ELI: http://data.europa.eu/eli/reg/2024/1689/oj).'.

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

107.summary_1: Adds a new paragraph to Regulation (EU) 2018/858, requiring that when adopting delegated acts concerning AI systems that are safety components, the requirements in Chapter III, Section 2 of Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 must be taken into account. This ensures alignment with the AI governance framework across EU safety and AI regulatory instruments. The amendment reinforces cross-regulatory consistency for AI safety component standards.
107.summary_2: An added paragraph to Article 5 of Regulation 2018/858 requires that, when adopting delegated acts under paragraph 3 relating to AI systems that qualify as safety components under Regulation 2024/1689, the requirements in Chapter III, Section 2 of Regulation 2024/1689 must be taken into account. This ensures that delegated acts governing AI safety components align with the AI safety requirements specified in 2024/1689.

notes.64: Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 13 June 2024 laying down harmonised rules on artificial intelligence and amending Regulations (EC) No 300/2008, (EU) No 167/2013, (EU) No 168/2013, (EU) 2018/858, (EU) 2018/1139 and (EU) 2019/2144 and Directives 2014/90/EU, (EU) 2016/797 and (EU) 2020/1828 (Artificial Intelligence Act) (OJ L, 2024/1689, 12.7.2024, ELI: http://data.europa.eu/eli/reg/2024/1689/oj).';

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

108.summary_1: Adds cross-regulatory alignment by requiring that when adopting implementing or delegated acts for AI systems that are safety components, the requirements of Chapter III, Section 2, of Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 must be taken into account. This applies to Articles 17, 19, 43, 47, 57, and 58 of Regulation (EU) 2018/1139. The measure reinforces consistency of AI safety governance across EU safety and AI regulatory instruments.
108.summary_2:
The amendments add a cross-reference to Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 across six provisions, requiring that when adopting implementing acts (Articles 17, 43, 57) or delegated acts (Articles 19, 47, 58) concerning Artificial Intelligence systems which are safety components, the requirements set out in Chapter III, Section 2 of Regulation 2024/1689 must be taken into account.
This cross-reference ensures alignment between Regulation (EU) 2018/1139 as amended and the AI Regulation, by embedding the specified Chapter III, Section 2 requirements into the aviation safety framework for AI safety components.
In practical terms, the relevant act-drafting processes must consider those AI Regulation requirements during the adoption of implementing or delegated acts, without altering the core procedural framework of Regulation 2018/1139.
The language is consistently applied to all six instances, signaling a uniform obligation to harmonize with the safety-component AI provisions of Regulation 2024/1689.

109.text:
In Article 11 of Regulation (EU) 2019/2144, the following paragraph is added:
'3. When adopting the implementing acts pursuant to paragraph 2, concerning artificial intelligence systems which are safety components within the meaning of Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council(note: 65), the requirements set out in Chapter III, Section 2, of that Regulation shall be taken into account.

notes.65: Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 13 June 2024 laying down harmonised rules on artificial intelligence and amending Regulations (EC) No 300/2008, (EU) No 167/2013, (EU) No 168/2013, (EU) 2018/858, (EU) 2018/1139 and (EU) 2019/2144 and Directives 2014/90/EU, (EU) 2016/797 and (EU) 2020/1828 (Artificial Intelligence Act) (OJ L, 2024/1689, 12.7.2024, ELI: http://data.europa.eu/eli/reg/2024/1689/oj).'.

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

109.summary_1: When adopting implementing acts regarding AI systems that are safety components, the requirements in Chapter III, Section 2 of Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 must be taken into account. This creates cross-regulatory alignment by linking AI safety governance to the related safety-regulation framework. It ensures consistent consideration of AI safety standards across EU regulatory acts.
109.summary_2: An added provision requires that when implementing acts concerning AI systems that are safety components under the EU AI Regulation are adopted, the safety requirements in Chapter III, Section 2 of that Regulation must be taken into account. This ensures regulatory coherence by embedding the AI safety component requirements in the act-drafting process, but it does not confer new rights on individuals.

110.text:
In Annex I to Directive (EU) 2020/1828 of the European Parliament and of the Council(note: 66), the following point is added:
'(68) Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 13 June 2024 laying down harmonised rules on artificial intelligence and amending Regulations (EC) No 300/2008, (EU) No 167/2013, (EU) No 168/2013, (EU) 2018/858, (EU) 2018/1139 and (EU) 2019/2144 and Directives 2014/90/EU, (EU) 2016/797 and (EU) 2020/1828 (Artificial Intelligence Act) (OJ L, 2024/1689, 12.7.2024, ELI: http://data.europa.eu/eli/reg/2024/1689/oj).'.

notes.66: Directive (EU) 2020/1828 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 25 November 2020 on representative actions for the protection of the collective interests of consumers and repealing Directive 2009/22/EC (OJ L 409, 4.12.2020, p. 1).

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

110.summary_1: Amends Directive (EU) 2020/1828 by adding a new point (68) in Annex I that references Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 (AI Act). This cross-reference embeds the AI governance framework into the directive, signaling alignment with harmonised AI rules for related safety provisions.
110.summary_2:
The amendment adds a new item (68) to Annex I of Directive (EU) 2020/1828, designating Regulation (EU) 2024/1689, the Artificial Intelligence Act, as part of the Union's harmonised rules. This inclusion confirms that the AI Act, which lays down harmonised rules on artificial intelligence and amends several existing Regulations and Directives, is integrated within the New Legislative Framework's harmonised-rule framework.
The reference provides the formal citation for the AI Act (OJ L 2024/1689, 12 July 2024) and notes its relation to the amended instruments listed in Annex I. By incorporating the AI Act, the directive ensures that its market-access and conformity requirements apply in parallel with other harmonised rules governing products and services across the EU.

111.unit_title: AI systems already placed on the market or put into service and general-purpose AI models already placed on the marked
111.text:
1. Without prejudice to the application of Article 5 as referred to in Article 113(3), point (a), AI systems which are components of the large-scale IT systems established by the legal acts listed in Annex X that have been placed on the market or put into service before 2 August 2027 shall be brought into compliance with this Regulation by 31 December 2030.
The requirements laid down in this Regulation shall be taken into account in the evaluation of each large-scale IT system established by the legal acts listed in Annex X to be undertaken as provided for in those legal acts and where those legal acts are replaced or amended.

2. Without prejudice to the application of Article 5 as referred to in Article 113(3), point (a), this Regulation shall apply to operators of high-risk AI systems, other than the systems referred to in paragraph 1 of this Article, that have been placed on the market or put into service before 2 August 2026, only if, as from that date, those systems are subject to significant changes in their designs. In any case, the providers and deployers of high-risk AI systems intended to be used by public authorities shall take the necessary steps to comply with the requirements and obligations of this Regulation by 2 August 2030.

3. Providers of general-purpose AI models that have been placed on the market before 2 August 2025 shall take the necessary steps in order to comply with the obligations laid down in this Regulation by 2 August 2027.

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

111.summary_1: AI components of large-scale IT systems established by specified acts must comply with the Regulation by the end of 2030, with compliance integrated into ongoing evaluations of those systems. For high-risk AI systems not covered by the first paragraph, the Regulation applies only if significant design changes occur before 2 August 2026, and the providers and deployers for public-authority use must achieve compliance by 2 August 2030. General-purpose AI models placed on the market before 2 August 2025 must reach compliance by 2 August 2027.
111.summary_2:
AI systems that are components of large-scale IT systems listed in Annex X and placed on the market or put into service before 2 August 2027 must be brought into compliance with the Regulation by 31 December 2030. Their conformity requirements are to be considered in the ongoing evaluation of those large-scale IT systems under the applicable acts, and those acts' replacement or amendment should incorporate the Regulation's requirements.
For other high-risk AI systems not covered by the first paragraph, placed on the market or put into service before 2 August 2026, the Regulation applies only if there are significant changes to their designs from that date forward. In any event, providers and deployers of high-risk AI intended for use by public authorities must implement the Regulation's obligations by 2 August 2030.
Providers of general-purpose AI models that were placed on the market before 2 August 2025 must take the necessary steps to comply with the Regulation by 2 August 2027.
These transitional obligations are without prejudice to the application of Article 5, meaning the restrictions and prohibitions set out there remain in force and are not overridden by these transitional timelines.

112.text:
1. The Commission shall assess the need for amendment of the list set out in Annex III and of the list of prohibited AI practices laid down in Article 5, once a year following the entry into force of this Regulation, and until the end of the period of the delegation of power laid down in Article 97. The Commission shall submit the findings of that assessment to the European Parliament and the Council.

2. By 2 August 2028 and every four years thereafter, the Commission shall evaluate and report to the European Parliament and to the Council on the following:
(a) the need for amendments extending existing area headings or adding new area headings in Annex III;
(b) amendments to the list of AI systems requiring additional transparency measures in Article 50;
(c) amendments enhancing the effectiveness of the supervision and governance system.

3. By 2 August 2029 and every four years thereafter, the Commission shall submit a report on the evaluation and review of this Regulation to the European Parliament and to the Council. The report shall include an assessment with regard to the structure of enforcement and the possible need for a Union agency to resolve any identified shortcomings. On the basis of the findings, that report shall, where appropriate, be accompanied by a proposal for amendment of this Regulation. The reports shall be made public.

4. The reports referred to in paragraph 2 shall pay specific attention to the following:
(a) the status of the financial, technical and human resources of the national competent authorities in order to effectively perform the tasks assigned to them under this Regulation;
(b) the state of penalties, in particular administrative fines as referred to in Article 99(1), applied by Member States for infringements of this Regulation;
(c) adopted harmonised standards and common specifications developed to support this Regulation;
(d) the number of undertakings that enter the market after the entry into application of this Regulation, and how many of them are SMEs.

5. By 2 August 2028, the Commission shall evaluate the functioning of the AI Office, whether the AI Office has been given sufficient powers and competences to fulfil its tasks, and whether it would be relevant and needed for the proper implementation and enforcement of this Regulation to upgrade the AI Office and its enforcement competences and to increase its resources. The Commission shall submit a report on its evaluation to the European Parliament and to the Council.

6. By 2 August 2028 and every four years thereafter, the Commission shall submit a report on the review of the progress on the development of standardisation deliverables on the energy-efficient development of general-purpose AI models, and asses the need for further measures or actions, including binding measures or actions. The report shall be submitted to the European Parliament and to the Council, and it shall be made public.

7. By 2 August 2028 and every three years thereafter, the Commission shall evaluate the impact and effectiveness of voluntary codes of conduct to foster the application of the requirements set out in Chapter III, Section 2 for AI systems other than high-risk AI systems and possibly other additional requirements for AI systems other than high-risk AI systems, including as regards environmental sustainability.

8. For the purposes of paragraphs 1 to 7, the Board, the Member States and national competent authorities shall provide the Commission with information upon its request and without undue delay.

9. In carrying out the evaluations and reviews referred to in paragraphs 1 to 7, the Commission shall take into account the positions and findings of the Board, of the European Parliament, of the Council, and of other relevant bodies or sources.

10. The Commission shall, if necessary, submit appropriate proposals to amend this Regulation, in particular taking into account developments in technology, the effect of AI systems on health and safety, and on fundamental rights, and in light of the state of progress in the information society.

11. To guide the evaluations and reviews referred to in paragraphs 1 to 7 of this Article, the AI Office shall undertake to develop an objective and participative methodology for the evaluation of risk levels based on the criteria outlined in the relevant Articles and the inclusion of new systems in:
(a) the list set out in Annex III, including the extension of existing area headings or the addition of new area headings in that Annex;
(b) the list of prohibited practices set out in Article 5; and
(c) the list of AI systems requiring additional transparency measures pursuant to Article 50.

12. Any amendment to this Regulation pursuant to paragraph 10, or relevant delegated or implementing acts, which concerns sectoral Union harmonisation legislation listed in Section B of Annex I shall take into account the regulatory specificities of each sector, and the existing governance, conformity assessment and enforcement mechanisms and authorities established therein.

13. By 2 August 2031, the Commission shall carry out an assessment of the enforcement of this Regulation and shall report on it to the European Parliament, the Council and the European Economic and Social Committee, taking into account the first years of application of this Regulation. On the basis of the findings, that report shall, where appropriate, be accompanied by a proposal for amendment of this Regulation with regard to the structure of enforcement and the need for a Union agency to resolve any identified shortcomings.

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

112.summary_1: It mandates annual assessments of the Annex III lists and the prohibited AI practices (Article 5), with findings submitted to the European Parliament and the Council. It establishes staggered review cycles with deadlines in 2028 (and every four years) for Annex III adjustments, Article 50 transparency updates, and governance; in 2029 (and every four years) for enforcement structure and potential Union agency; in 2028 (and every three years) for voluntary codes of conduct; and in 2031 for an overarching enforcement assessment, all with public reporting. It also tasks the AI Office with developing an evaluation methodology, requires information from national authorities, and supports cross-regulatory alignment with sector-specific rules and the possible upgrade of enforcement capacity.
112.summary_2:
The Commission must annually assess whether the lists of high-risk AI systems (Annex III) and the prohibited AI practices (Art 5) should be amended, and must report its findings to the European Parliament and the Council. By 2 August 2028 and then every four years, it shall also evaluate and report on (i) extending or creating new area headings in Annex III, (ii) amendments to the transparency requirements for AI systems in Article 50, and (iii) improvements to the effectiveness of supervision and governance.
- By 2 August 2029 and every four years thereafter, the Commission shall produce a comprehensive report evaluating and reviewing the Regulation, including an assessment of enforcement structures and the possible need for a Union agency to address any shortcomings, with a view to proposing amendments where appropriate. All such reports shall be made public.
- The periodic reports must specifically address: the financial, technical and human resources of national competent authorities; the level and application of penalties, including administrative fines; the adoption of harmonised standards and common specifications; and the number of market entrants, with particular attention to SMEs. The Commission will also evaluate the functioning and powers of the AI Office and whether upgrades are needed, and monitor energy-efficient standardisation for general-purpose AI models, including possible binding measures. It shall assess voluntary codes of conduct for non-high-risk AI systems, including environmental sustainability, on a triannual basis.
- The Board, Member States and national competent authorities must provide the Commission with information upon request and without undue delay, and the Commission must consider the positions and findings of the Board, the European Parliament, the Council, and other relevant bodies in its evaluations and reviews.
- Any amendments prompted by these evaluations, including sector-specific harmonisation considerations, shall take into account the regulatory particularities and existing governance and enforcement mechanisms in each sector. By 2031 the Commission shall assess overall enforcement and report to the Parliament, the Council and the European Economic and Social Committee, potentially proposing amendments to address enforcement structure and the possible creation of a Union agency.

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

113.summary_1: Entry into force occurs on the twentieth day after publication, and the Regulation becomes applicable in staged dates. Chapters I and II apply from 2 February 2025, while Chapter III Section 4, Chapter V, Chapter VII, Chapter XII, and Article 78 apply from 2 August 2025 (Article 101 is an exception). Article 6(1) and the corresponding obligations apply from 2 August 2027.
113.summary_2:
The Regulation enters into force on the twentieth day after its publication in the Official Journal, and it generally applies from 2 August 2026.
There is staged applicability: Chapters I and II apply from 2 February 2025; Chapters III Section 4, Chapter V, Chapter VII, Chapter XII and Article 78 apply from 2 August 2025, with the exception of Article 101.
Article 6(1) and the corresponding obligations apply from 2 August 2027.

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

I.summary_1: Provides a catalog of Union harmonisation legislation under the New Legislative Framework, split into Section A (core safety and market-surveillance instruments) and Section B (additional harmonisation rules across transport and related sectors). It lists specific directives and regulations by number and date, spanning machinery, toys, recreational crafts, lifts and safety components, hazardous-area equipment, radio equipment, pressure equipment, PPE, gas appliances, medical devices and in vitro diagnostics, as well as civil aviation security, vehicle approvals, rail interoperability, and motor-vehicle type-approval regimes. Together, the items illustrate the breadth of cross-sector product-safety and market-access harmonisation within the Union.
I.summary_2:
Annex I classifies Union harmonisation legislation under the New Legislative Framework into two sections. Section A lists core harmonisation instruments, including directives and regulations that cover machinery (Directive 2006/42/EC), toys (Directive 2009/48/EC), recreational craft (Directive 2013/53/EU), lifts (Directive 2014/33/EU), equipment for potentially explosive atmospheres (Directive 2014/34/EU), radio equipment (Regulation (EU) 2014/53/EU), pressure equipment (Regulation (EU) 2014/68/EU), cableway installations (Regulation (EU) 2016/424), PPE (Regulation (EU) 2016/425), gas appliances (Regulation (EU) 2016/426), medical devices (Regulation (EU) 2017/745), and in vitro diagnostic medical devices (Regulation (EU) 2017/746).
Section B lists other Union harmonisation legislation in sectors such as civil aviation security (Regulation (EC) No 300/2008), two- and three-wheel vehicles and quadricycles (Regulation (EU) No 168/2013), agricultural and forestry vehicles (Regulation (EU) No 167/2013), marine equipment (Directive 2014/90/EU), rail interoperability (Directive (EU) 2016/797), motor vehicles and trailers (Regulation (EU) 2018/858 and Regulation (EU) 2019/2144), and civil aviation common rules including unmanned aircraft (Regulation (EU) 2018/1139).
The annex identifies the instruments that underpin harmonised market rules and the associated conformity, market surveillance, and CE-marking framework across sectors.
Practitioners should use this list to locate the specific instrument governing rights, restrictions, and obligations for a given product or sector; the annex itself does not detail those obligations but directs to the instrument texts for precise requirements.

II.unit_title: List of criminal offences referred to in Article 5(1), first subparagraph, point (h)(iii)
II.text:
Criminal offences referred to in Article 5(1), first subparagraph, point (h)(iii):
-- terrorism,
-- trafficking in human beings,
-- sexual exploitation of children, and child pornography,
-- illicit trafficking in narcotic drugs or psychotropic substances,
-- illicit trafficking in weapons, munitions or explosives,
-- murder, grievous bodily injury,
-- illicit trade in human organs or tissue,
-- illicit trafficking in nuclear or radioactive materials,
-- kidnapping, illegal restraint or hostage-taking,
-- crimes within the jurisdiction of the International Criminal Court,
-- unlawful seizure of aircraft or ships,
-- rape,
-- environmental crime,
-- organised or armed robbery,
-- sabotage,
-- participation in a criminal organisation involved in one or more of the offences listed above.

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

II.summary_1: It lists the criminal offences referred to in Article 5(1), first subparagraph, point (h)(iii). The offences include terrorism, trafficking in human beings, sexual exploitation of children and child pornography, illicit trafficking in drugs and psychotropic substances, illicit trafficking in weapons, murder and grievous bodily harm, illicit trade in human organs, illicit trafficking in nuclear or radioactive materials, kidnapping, ICC-jurisdiction crimes, unlawful seizure of aircraft or ships, rape, environmental crime, organized or armed robbery, sabotage, and participation in a criminal organization involved in these offences. The enumeration clarifies which acts fall within the scope of Article 5 for regulatory or enforcement purposes.
II.summary_2:
Annex II sets out the specific criminal offences that trigger reference under Article 5(1), first subparagraph, point (h)(iii). The listed offences are: terrorism; trafficking in human beings; sexual exploitation of children and child pornography; illicit trafficking in narcotic drugs or psychotropic substances; illicit trafficking in weapons, munitions or explosives; murder and grievous bodily injury; illicit trade in human organs or tissue; illicit trafficking in nuclear or radioactive materials; kidnapping, illegal restraint or hostage-taking; crimes within the jurisdiction of the International Criminal Court; unlawful seizure of aircraft or ships; rape; environmental crime; organised or armed robbery; sabotage; and participation in a criminal organisation involved in one or more of the above offences.
The inclusion of "participation in a criminal organisation involved in one or more of the offences listed above" ensures liability coverage for complicity or organized involvement across the same offences. This broadens the scope to capture both direct offenders and associates within criminal networks.
For practitioners, this annex defines the scope of offences that may influence the application of the Regulation's safeguards and prohibitions in Article 5(1)(h)(iii). It signals the thresholds and categories of serious crime that may drive enhanced controls, risk assessments, or restrictions when AI systems intersect with activities connected to these offences.

III.unit_title: High-risk AI systems referred to in Article 6(2)

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

III.summary_1: Annex III lists high-risk AI systems as defined in Article 6(2), covering biometric systems (remote identification, categorization, emotion recognition), AI in critical infrastructure, education and vocational training, employment and access to self-employment, essential services, law enforcement, migration, and administration of justice. The designation highlights that these categories are subject to higher regulatory scrutiny due to potential impacts on safety, rights, and democratic processes. It enumerates subcategories under each domain to specify when an AI system falls into high-risk.
III.summary_2:
High-risk AI systems referred to in Article 6(2) encompass the following areas and use cases, with deployment restricted to contexts permitted by relevant Union or national law in many cases. These categories are designated high-risk and subject to the regulation's stringent requirements governing risk management, data governance, transparency, and human oversight.
Biometrics: remote biometric identification systems are high-risk where permitted by law, excluding AI systems whose sole purpose is biometric verification to confirm a person's identity; biometric categorisation and emotion recognition are also high-risk. The scope and legality of use depend on compliance with applicable legal frameworks.
Critical infrastructure: AI used as safety components in the management and operation of critical digital infrastructure, road traffic, or the supply of water, gas, heating, or electricity is high-risk. Education and vocational training: high-risk uses include determining access/admission to institutions, evaluating learning outcomes, assessing the appropriate level of education, and monitoring/detecting prohibited behavior of students during tests.
Employment, services, and law enforcement: high-risk uses include recruitment/selection and decision-making affecting work-related terms or progression, task allocation based on individual behavior or traits, and monitoring performance. In essential services, high-risk uses cover evaluating eligibility for public assistance, creditworthiness (with an exception for fraud detection), life/health insurance risk assessment/pricing, and emergency call evaluation/triage and dispatch. In law enforcement, high-risk uses include risk assessment of victims, polygraphs or similar tools, evaluating evidence reliability, assessing risk of offending beyond profiling, and profiling in detection, investigation, or prosecution; and in migration/border control, high-risk uses include polygraphs, risk assessment of entrants, asylum/visa eligibility examinations, and assisting with applications and evidence reliability, excluding travel document verification.
Administration of justice and democratic processes: high-risk AI may assist judicial authorities in researching facts and applying the law, or be used to influence election outcomes or voting behavior, with the caveat that outputs not directly exposing individuals may include administrative or logistical political-campaign tools.

IV.unit_title: Technical documentation referred to in Article 11(1)

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

IV.summary_1: Annex IV sets out the mandatory content and structure of the technical documentation for AI systems, detailing general descriptions, deployment context, interfaces, hardware and software versions, and user-facing information. It requires a thorough description of development methods, design choices, data requirements, system architecture, validation/testing, cybersecurity, and monitoring, including risk management and human oversight. It also mandates information on changes across the lifecycle, harmonised standards or alternative solutions, and a post-market monitoring plan, plus a copy of the EU declaration of conformity.

need_ref.value: Official Journal of the European Union

IV.summary_2:
The technical documentation must provide a comprehensive description of the AI system and its development, including the intended purpose, provider name, and versioning, how it interacts with hardware, software, and other systems, relevant software/firmware versions and update requirements, all forms in which the system is placed on the market or put into service, the hardware it runs on, any product features shown externally, and a basic deployer-facing user interface with accompanying use instructions.
It must also contain a detailed description of how the system was developed and designed, covering the development methods and steps (including any pre-trained components or third-party tools and how they were used), design specifications and rationale for key design choices and trade-offs, system architecture and the computational resources used in development, training, testing and validation, data requirements with provenance and characteristics of training data and labeling/ cleaning procedures, an assessment of required human oversight and interpretability measures, descriptions of any predetermined changes to maintain compliance, validation and testing procedures with datasets, metrics for accuracy, robustness and non-discrimination, and test logs/reports, as well as the cybersecurity measures implemented.
The documentation must include detailed information on monitoring, functioning, and control, addressing the system's capabilities and limitations, expected accuracy for intended users or user groups, foreseeable unintended outcomes and health/safety/fundamental-rights risks, and the human oversight measures needed, including data input specifications where relevant.
There must be a description of the appropriateness of the performance metrics used for the specific system, and a detailed description of the risk management system implemented in accordance with Article 9, including how changes to the system during its lifecycle are documented, and the description of the system in place to evaluate performance in the post-market phase as required by Article 72, including the post-market monitoring plan.
The documentation must list harmonised standards applied in full or in part (with official references) or, if none, provide a detailed description of how the Chapter III, Section 2 requirements are met through other standards and technical specifications. It must include a copy of the EU declaration of conformity referred to in Article 47, and describe the system for evaluating post-market performance, including the post-market monitoring plan required in Article 72(3).

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

V.summary_1: This annex specifies the information that must be included in the EU declaration of conformity for AI systems under Article 47, including identification, provider details, and a statement of responsibility. It requires conformity with this Regulation and any applicable Union law, plus any data-protection compliance when personal data are processed, and it mandates references to harmonised standards and details of the conformity assessment, including the notified body and certificate where applicable. It also requires the declaration to include place and date of issue and the signatory information.
V.summary_2:
Annex V requires the EU declaration of conformity to identify the AI system and its maker: the AI system name and type (plus any additional unambiguous reference for identification and traceability) and the provider's name and address or their authorized representative.
The declaration must state that it is issued under the sole responsibility of the provider and that the AI system conforms to this Regulation, and, where applicable, to any other relevant Union law that requires such a declaration.
If the AI system processes personal data, the declaration must affirm compliance with Regulation (EU) 2016/679 (GDPR), Regulation (EU) 2018/1725, and Directive (EU) 2016/680.
The declaration must reference any relevant harmonised standards or other common specifications used for conformity, and, where applicable, include the name and identification number of the notified body, a description of the conformity assessment procedure performed, and the identification of the certificate issued.
Finally, the declaration must provide the place and date of issue, the name and function of the person who signed it, and an indication of on whose behalf the person signed, accompanied by a signature.

VI.unit_title: Conformity assessment procedure based on internal control
VI.text:
1. The conformity assessment procedure based on internal control is the conformity assessment procedure based on points 2, 3 and 4.
2. The provider verifies that the established quality management system is in compliance with the requirements of Article 17.
3. The provider examines the information contained in the technical documentation in order to assess the compliance of the AI system with the relevant essential requirements set out in Chapter III, Section 2.
4. The provider also verifies that the design and development process of the AI system and its post-market monitoring as referred to in Article 72 is consistent with the technical documentation.

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

VI.summary_1: Annex VI outlines an internal-control conformity assessment in which the provider must ensure the quality management system complies with Article 17, review the technical documentation to verify compliance with the essential requirements in Chapter III, and confirm that the AI system’s design, development process, and post-market monitoring align with the technical documentation and Article 72.
VI.summary_2: The conformity assessment procedure based on internal control is defined as the procedure comprising the steps in points 2, 3 and 4. The provider must verify that the established quality management system complies with the requirements of Article 17. The provider must examine the information in the technical documentation to assess the AI system's compliance with the relevant essential requirements set out in Chapter III, Section 2. The provider must also verify that the AI system's design and development process and its post-market monitoring, as referred to in Article 72, are consistent with the technical documentation.

VII.unit_title: Conformity based on an assessment of the quality management system and an assessment of the technical documentation
VII.text:
1. Introduction
Conformity based on an assessment of the quality management system and an assessment of the technical documentation is the conformity assessment procedure based on points 2 to 5.
2. Overview
The approved quality management system for the design, development and testing of AI systems pursuant to Article 17 shall be examined in accordance with point 3 and shall be subject to surveillance as specified in point 5. The technical documentation of the AI system shall be examined in accordance with point 4.
3. Quality management system
3.1. The application of the provider shall include:(a) the name and address of the provider and, if the application is lodged by an authorised representative, also their name and address;
(b) the list of AI systems covered under the same quality management system;
(c) the technical documentation for each AI system covered under the same quality management system;
(d) the documentation concerning the quality management system which shall cover all the aspects listed under Article 17;
(e) a description of the procedures in place to ensure that the quality management system remains adequate and effective;
(f) a written declaration that the same application has not been lodged with any other notified body.
3.2. The quality management system shall be assessed by the notified body, which shall determine whether it satisfies the requirements referred to in Article 17.The decision shall be notified to the provider or its authorised representative.
The notification shall contain the conclusions of the assessment of the quality management system and the reasoned assessment decision.
3.3. The quality management system as approved shall continue to be implemented and maintained by the provider so that it remains adequate and efficient.
3.4. Any intended change to the approved quality management system or the list of AI systems covered by the latter shall be brought to the attention of the notified body by the provider.The proposed changes shall be examined by the notified body, which shall decide whether the modified quality management system continues to satisfy the requirements referred to in point 3.2 or whether a reassessment is necessary.
The notified body shall notify the provider of its decision. The notification shall contain the conclusions of the examination of the changes and the reasoned assessment decision.
4. Control of the technical documentation.
4.1. In addition to the application referred to in point 3, an application with a notified body of their choice shall be lodged by the provider for the assessment of the technical documentation relating to the AI system which the provider intends to place on the market or put into service and which is covered by the quality management system referred to under point 3.
4.2. The application shall include:(a) the name and address of the provider;
(b) a written declaration that the same application has not been lodged with any other notified body;
(c) the technical documentation referred to in Annex IV.
4.3. The technical documentation shall be examined by the notified body. Where relevant, and limited to what is necessary to fulfil its tasks, the notified body shall be granted full access to the training, validation, and testing data sets used, including, where appropriate and subject to security safeguards, through API or other relevant technical means and tools enabling remote access.
4.4. In examining the technical documentation, the notified body may require that the provider supply further evidence or carry out further tests so as to enable a proper assessment of the conformity of the AI system with the requirements set out in Chapter III, Section 2. Where the notified body is not satisfied with the tests carried out by the provider, the notified body shall itself directly carry out adequate tests, as appropriate.
4.5. Where necessary to assess the conformity of the high-risk AI system with the requirements set out in Chapter III, Section 2, after all other reasonable means to verify conformity have been exhausted and have proven to be insufficient, and upon a reasoned request, the notified body shall also be granted access to the training and trained models of the AI system, including its relevant parameters. Such access shall be subject to existing Union law on the protection of intellectual property and trade secrets.
4.6. The decision of the notified body shall be notified to the provider or its authorised representative. The notification shall contain the conclusions of the assessment of the technical documentation and the reasoned assessment decision.Where the AI system is in conformity with the requirements set out in Chapter III, Section 2, the notified body shall issue a Union technical documentation assessment certificate. The certificate shall indicate the name and address of the provider, the conclusions of the examination, the conditions (if any) for its validity and the data necessary for the identification of the AI system.
The certificate and its annexes shall contain all relevant information to allow the conformity of the AI system to be evaluated, and to allow for control of the AI system while in use, where applicable.
Where the AI system is not in conformity with the requirements set out in Chapter III, Section 2, the notified body shall refuse to issue a Union technical documentation assessment certificate and shall inform the applicant accordingly, giving detailed reasons for its refusal.
Where the AI system does not meet the requirement relating to the data used to train it, re-training of the AI system will be needed prior to the application for a new conformity assessment. In this case, the reasoned assessment decision of the notified body refusing to issue the Union technical documentation assessment certificate shall contain specific considerations on the quality data used to train the AI system, in particular on the reasons for non-compliance.
4.7. Any change to the AI system that could affect the compliance of the AI system with the requirements or its intended purpose shall be assessed by the notified body which issued the Union technical documentation assessment certificate. The provider shall inform such notified body of its intention to introduce any of the abovementioned changes, or if it otherwise becomes aware of the occurrence of such changes. The intended changes shall be assessed by the notified body, which shall decide whether those changes require a new conformity assessment in accordance with Article 43(4) or whether they could be addressed by means of a supplement to the Union technical documentation assessment certificate. In the latter case, the notified body shall assess the changes, notify the provider of its decision and, where the changes are approved, issue to the provider a supplement to the Union technical documentation assessment certificate.
5. Surveillance of the approved quality management system.
5.1. The purpose of the surveillance carried out by the notified body referred to in Point 3 is to make sure that the provider duly complies with the terms and conditions of the approved quality management system.
5.2. For assessment purposes, the provider shall allow the notified body to access the premises where the design, development, testing of the AI systems is taking place. The provider shall further share with the notified body all necessary information.
5.3. The notified body shall carry out periodic audits to make sure that the provider maintains and applies the quality management system and shall provide the provider with an audit report. In the context of those audits, the notified body may carry out additional tests of the AI systems for which a Union technical documentation assessment certificate was issued.

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

VII.summary_1: Conformity is assessed by verifying an approved quality management system for design and development of AI, together with an examination of the technical documentation by a notified body, and is followed by ongoing surveillance. The provider must supply details about the QA system, all AI systems covered, and the relevant documentation, and the notified body may require additional evidence or access to training data to verify conformity, including data used for high‑risk systems. If compliant, a Union technical documentation assessment certificate is issued; if not, a reasoned refusal is provided, and any changes to the AI system or QA setup may require reassessment or a certificate supplement.

need_ref.value: Union law on the protection of intellectual property and trade secrets

VII.summary_2:
This conformity path combines two linked assessments: conformity based on an approved quality management system (QMS) for the design, development and testing of AI systems, and conformity based on the assessment of the AI system's technical documentation. The QMS assessment is conducted under the procedure in Article 17 and is subject to ongoing surveillance, while the technical documentation for the AI system is assessed separately under the same framework. The outcome is a notified-body decision on both the QMS and the technical documentation.
For the QMS (3.1-3.4), the provider must submit: (a) name and address (and authorized representative details if applicable); (b) the list of AI systems covered by the same QMS; (c) the technical documentation for each system within the QMS; (d) documentation describing all QMS aspects referred to in Article 17; (e) procedures ensuring the QMS remains adequate and effective; and (f) a declaration that the same application has not been lodged with another notified body. The notified body will assess whether the QMS satisfies Article 17 and inform the provider of its decision with conclusions and reasoning. The approved QMS must be implemented and maintained, and any proposed changes to the QMS or the list of AI systems must be notified and assessed to determine if reassessment is required, with the notified body issuing its conclusions.
For the technical documentation (4.1-4.7), the provider must lodge an additional application with a notified body for the AI system's technical documentation covered by the QMS, including provider details, a declaration of no other lodgement, and the technical documentation referenced in Annex IV. The notified body will examine the documentation and may require access to training, validation and testing data (including remote access where appropriate). It can request further evidence or testing; if necessary, the body may conduct its own tests. In cases of high-risk AI, access to training data and trained models (with IP/trade secrets protections) may be granted upon reasoned request. If conformity is established, the notified body issues a Union technical documentation assessment certificate detailing conclusions, validity conditions, and identifiers; if not, it will refuse with reasons. If data quality issues are identified, retraining may be required before reapplication, with the decision explaining data-related non-compliance. Any changes to the AI system that affect compliance or purpose must be assessed by the same notified body; the provider must inform the body, which will decide whether a new conformity assessment or a certificate supplement is appropriate, and issue the supplement if approved.
Surveillance of the approved QMS (5) ensures ongoing compliance with the approved terms. The provider must permit access to facilities where design, development, and testing occur and share necessary information. The notified body conducts periodic audits to verify maintenance and application of the QMS and may perform additional tests on AI systems with an issued Union technical documentation assessment certificate, issuing an audit report and, where warranted, further testing.

VIII.unit_title: Information to be submitted upon the registration of high-risk AI systems in accordance with Article 49

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

VIII.summary_1: Annex VIII prescribes the information requirements for registering high-risk AI systems under Article 49, organizing them into Section A (providers), Section B (not-high-risk under Article 49(2)), and Section C (deployers under Article 49(3)). It mandates identification, intended purpose, data and operating logic, system status, and, where applicable, the certificate details and EU declaration of conformity, plus an electronic instructions for use (with exemptions in law enforcement or migration areas) and a URL for additional information, all to be kept up to date and submitted to the EU database. It additionally requires Section C to include deployer details, the EU database entry URL, and summaries of the fundamental rights impact assessment and data protection impact assessment.
VIII.summary_2:
Section A requires providers of high-risk AI systems to register and continuously update a comprehensive set of identifying and compliance information. This includes the provider's name and contact details (and those of any third party submitting on behalf, and any authorised representative), the AI system's trade name and traceability reference, the intended purpose and the key components and functions, and a concise description of the data/inputs and the system's operating logic. It also covers the system's current status (on the market or in service, or withdrawn/ recalled), the certification details (type, number, expiry) and the issuing notified body (with a copy of the certificate, if available), the Member States where the system has been placed or made available, and the EU declaration of conformity. In addition, electronic instructions for use must be provided (with an exemption for certain law enforcement or migration-related systems), and an optional URL for additional information; information submitted by an intermediary and the need for translation into EU languages where authorities operate are also addressed. The provider bears responsibility for conformity, and the information must be kept up to date; if the system is subject to other Union harmonisation acts requiring a declaration, a single EU declaration suffices and the information must be accessible to authorities on request.
Section B sets out the information for AI systems that are registered as not-high-risk under Article 49(2). It requires the same core identifying data and trade name, the intended purpose, and, crucially, the conditions under Article 6(3) that lead to the not-high-risk classification, plus a short summary of the grounds for this classification. The section also requires the current status of the AI system and the Member States where it has been placed on the market, put into service, or made available in the Union. This creates an ongoing obligation to document and disclose why a system is treated as not-high-risk and to keep that assessment up to date as circumstances change.
Section C governs information for deployers of high-risk AI systems under Article 49(3). Deployers must provide their own contact details and those of the person submitting on their behalf, supply the EU database entry URL created by the provider, and include a summary of the fundamental rights impact assessment conducted under Article 27, along with a summary of any applicable data protection impact assessment pursuant to Regulation (EU) 2016/679 or Directive 2016/680 as specified in Article 26(8). The information must also be kept up to date, and it applies to the deployment phase rather than initial registration, ensuring ongoing accountability for rights-related and data protection considerations.

IX.unit_title: Information to be submitted upon the registration of high-risk AI systems listed in Annex III in relation to testing in real world conditions in accordance with Article 60

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

IX.summary_1: Annex IX requires registering high-risk AI systems listed in Annex III for testing in real-world conditions, including a Union-wide unique testing identification number. It also requires the names and contact details of the provider, prospective provider, and deployers; a brief description of the AI system and its intended purpose; and a summary of the main characteristics of the testing plan. Finally, it requires information on the suspension or termination of the testing and mandates that these details be kept up to date.
IX.summary_2:
Annex IX requires that for high-risk AI systems listed in Annex III undergoing testing in real-world conditions, the testing must be registered under Article 60, and a Union-wide unique identification number must be assigned. The information submitted must be kept up to date.
The information to be provided includes: (1) the Union-wide unique identification number; (2) the name and contact details of the provider or prospective provider and of the deployers involved in the testing; (3) a brief description of the AI system, its intended purpose, and other identifying information; (4) a summary of the main characteristics of the testing plan.
Additionally, the registration must include information on suspension or termination of the testing. All submitted information must be maintained and updated as testing proceeds.

X.unit_title: Union legislative acts on large-scale IT systems in the area of Freedom, Security and Justice
X.text:
1. Schengen Information System
(a) Regulation (EU) 2018/1860 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 28 November 2018 on the use of the Schengen Information System for the return of illegally staying third-country nationals (OJ L 312, 7.12.2018, p. 1).
(b) Regulation (EU) 2018/1861 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 28 November 2018 on the establishment, operation and use of the Schengen Information System (SIS) in the field of border checks, and amending the Convention implementing the Schengen Agreement, and amending and repealing Regulation (EC) No 1987/2006 (OJ L 312, 7.12.2018, p. 14).
(c) Regulation (EU) 2018/1862 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 28 November 2018 on the establishment, operation and use of the Schengen Information System (SIS) in the field of police cooperation and judicial cooperation in criminal matters, amending and repealing Council Decision 2007/533/JHA, and repealing Regulation (EC) No 1986/2006 of the European Parliament and of the Council and Commission Decision 2010/261/EU (OJ L 312, 7.12.2018, p. 56).
2. Visa Information System
(a) Regulation (EU) 2021/1133 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 7 July 2021 amending Regulations (EU) No 603/2013, (EU) 2016/794, (EU) 2018/1862, (EU) 2019/816 and (EU) 2019/818 as regards the establishment of the conditions for accessing other EU information systems for the purposes of the Visa Information System (OJ L 248, 13.7.2021, p. 1).
(b) Regulation (EU) 2021/1134 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 7 July 2021 amending Regulations (EC) No 767/2008, (EC) No 810/2009, (EU) 2016/399, (EU) 2017/2226, (EU) 2018/1240, (EU) 2018/1860, (EU) 2018/1861, (EU) 2019/817 and (EU) 2019/1896 of the European Parliament and of the Council and repealing Council Decisions 2004/512/EC and 2008/633/JHA, for the purpose of reforming the Visa Information System (OJ L 248, 13.7.2021, p. 11).
3. Eurodac
Regulation (EU) 2024/1358 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 14 May 2024 on the establishment of 'Eurodac' for the comparison of biometric data in order to effectively apply Regulations (EU) 2024/1315 and (EU) 2024/1350 of the European Parliament and of the Council and Council Directive 2001/55/EC and to identify illegally staying third-country nationals and stateless persons and on requests for the comparison with Eurodac data by Member States' law enforcement authorities and Europol for law enforcement purposes, amending Regulations (EU) 2018/1240 and (EU) 2019/818 of the European Parliament and of the Council and repealing Regulation (EU) No 603/2013 of the European Parliament and of the Council (OJ L, 2024/1358, 22.5.2024, ELI: http://data.europa.eu/eli/reg/2024/1358/oj).
4. Entry/Exit System
Regulation (EU) 2017/2226 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 30 November 2017 establishing an Entry/Exit System (EES) to register entry and exit data and refusal of entry data of third-country nationals crossing the external borders of the Member States and determining the conditions for access to the EES for law enforcement purposes, and amending the Convention implementing the Schengen Agreement and Regulations (EC) No 767/2008 and (EU) No 1077/2011 (OJ L 327, 9.12.2017, p. 20).
5. European Travel Information and Authorisation System
(a) Regulation (EU) 2018/1240 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 12 September 2018 establishing a European Travel Information and Authorisation System (ETIAS) and amending Regulations (EU) No 1077/2011, (EU) No 515/2014, (EU) 2016/399, (EU) 2016/1624 and (EU) 2017/2226 (OJ L 236, 19.9.2018, p. 1).
(b) Regulation (EU) 2018/1241 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 12 September 2018 amending Regulation (EU) 2016/794 for the purpose of establishing a European Travel Information and Authorisation System (ETIAS) (OJ L 236, 19.9.2018, p. 72).
6. European Criminal Records Information System on third-country nationals and stateless persons
Regulation (EU) 2019/816 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 17 April 2019 establishing a centralised system for the identification of Member States holding conviction information on third-country nationals and stateless persons (ECRIS-TCN) to supplement the European Criminal Records Information System and amending Regulation (EU) 2018/1726 (OJ L 135, 22.5.2019, p. 1).
7. Interoperability
(a) Regulation (EU) 2019/817 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 20 May 2019 on establishing a framework for interoperability between EU information systems in the field of borders and visa and amending Regulations (EC) No 767/2008, (EU) 2016/399, (EU) 2017/2226, (EU) 2018/1240, (EU) 2018/1726 and (EU) 2018/1861 of the European Parliament and of the Council and Council Decisions 2004/512/EC and 2008/633/JHA (OJ L 135, 22.5.2019, p. 27).
(b) Regulation (EU) 2019/818 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 20 May 2019 on establishing a framework for interoperability between EU information systems in the field of police and judicial cooperation, asylum and migration and amending Regulations (EU) 2018/1726, (EU) 2018/1862 and (EU) 2019/816 (OJ L 135, 22.5.2019, p. 85).

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

X.summary_1: It catalogs the EU's large-scale IT information systems used in freedom, security and justice, including SIS, EES, ETIAS, Eurodac, the Visa Information System, and ECRIS-TCN. The annex lists the governing regulations and amendments that establish, operate, and interconnect these systems, as well as interoperability frameworks. Collectively, these acts underpin cross-border data sharing for border control, migration, and law enforcement.
X.summary_2:
The portion lists the Union legislative acts that establish and govern large-scale IT systems used for freedom, security and justice across the EU. It groups these into seven clusters: the Schengen Information System (SIS), the Visa Information System (VIS), Eurodac, the Entry/Exit System (EES), the European Travel Information and Authorisation System (ETIAS), the European Criminal Records Information System on third-country nationals and stateless persons (ECRIS-TCN), and the interoperability framework linking these systems. Each cluster references the specific implementing regulations and amendments necessary to create, operate, and interlink the systems and their data flows.
SIS comprises three regulations (2018/1860, 1861, 1862) addressing different domains: the return of illegally staying third-country nationals, border checks, and police/judicial cooperation in criminal matters. Collectively, SIS provides a shared data platform for border management, law enforcement cooperation, and related judicial processes, with the activities of the system described as establishing, operating and using SIS for these purposes.
VIS, with regulations 2021/1133 and 2021/1134, governs access to other EU information systems for visa-related purposes and reforms associated with the VIS architecture. This structure enables visa authorities and member states to query and interact with a broader information ecosystem to support visa screening, decision-making, and related security checks.
The annex also covers Eurodac (Reg 2024/1358), which establishes biometric data comparison to identify illegally staying third-country nationals and stateless persons and to support law enforcement and compliance with related directives/regulations; EES (Reg 2017/2226) for registering entry and exit data and refusals of entry; ETIAS (Reg 2018/1240 and 2018/1241) for a travel information and authorization regime; and ECRIS-TCN (Reg 2019/816) for centralized identification of conviction information on third-country nationals and stateless persons. Finally, interoperability regulations (Reg 2019/817 for borders/visa, and Reg 2019/818 for police/judicial cooperation, asylum and migration) establish the cross-system framework needed to interconnect these information systems, including amendments to numerous preceding regulations.
In sum, the text identifies the core EU-wide, large-scale IT systems and the corresponding regulatory instruments that establish, operate, and interlink them to support border control, visa processing, criminal justice cooperation, and enforcement across Member States, with explicit provisions for access conditions and cross-system interoperability.

XI.unit_title: Technical documentation referred to in Article 53(1), point (a) -- technical documentation for providers of general-purpose AI models

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

XI.summary_1: Sets content requirements for the technical documentation of general-purpose AI models, requiring a general description (tasks, use policies, release date, architecture, inputs/outputs, license) and a detailed account of development elements (training data, design choices, required integration means, computational resources, and energy use). It adds Section 2 obligations for models with systemic risk, mandating evaluation strategies, results, adversarial testing measures, model alignment/fine-tuning details, and a description of the architecture and component integration. The overall aim is to enable assessment of risk, deployment suitability, and traceability across the model's lifecycle.
XI.summary_2:
All providers of general-purpose AI models must keep up-to-date technical documentation that covers at least: a general description of the model including the tasks it is intended to perform, the type and nature of AI systems it can be integrated into, acceptable use policies, date of release and methods of distribution, the model's architecture and number of parameters, the modality and input/output formats, and the license governing use.
The documentation must also provide a detailed description of the model's development and deployment elements, including the technical means required to integrate the model into AI systems, design specifications and training processes (methods, key design choices, rationale, and what the model is designed to optimize for), information on data used for training, testing, and validation (type, provenance, curation methods, data point counts, selection methods, and bias-detection measures), and the computational resources used for training (resources, training time) and the model's energy consumption (with guidance if the energy use is unknown).
For providers of general-purpose AI models with systemic risk, additional information is required: a detailed description of evaluation strategies with results based on available public evaluation protocols or other methodologies, including evaluation criteria, metrics, and how limitations are identified; measures for internal and/or external adversarial testing (e.g., red teaming), model adaptations such as alignment and fine-tuning; and a detailed description of the system architecture explaining how software components are built or fed into each other and integrated into the overall processing.

XII.unit_title: Transparency information referred to in Article 53(1), point (b) -- technical documentation for providers of general-purpose AI models to downstream providers that integrate the model into their AI system
