context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

XII.summary_1: Annex XII prescribes transparency requirements for providers of general-purpose AI models supplied to downstream providers, detailing the information that must be disclosed about the model and its development. It requires a comprehensive general description (tasks, policies, release date, interaction with non-model components, software versions, architecture, inputs/outputs, and license) and a description of development elements (integration means, input/output modalities and sizes, and data provenance and curation). The aim is to enable downstream users to assess usage, risk, and compliance across the model’s lifecycle.
XII.summary_2:
The obligation requires providers of general-purpose AI models to furnish downstream integrators with a mandatory transparency package. This package must include a general description of the model, detailing the tasks it is intended to perform, the types of AI systems into which it can be integrated, acceptable use policies, the release date and distribution methods, how it interacts with non-model hardware or software, relevant software versions, the architecture and parameter count, the model's modality and input/output formats, and the license governing the model.
In addition, the package must describe the model's development and constituent elements, including the technical means needed to integrate the model (such as instructions, infrastructure, and tools), the inputs and outputs modalities and formats with their maximum sizes, and information on data used for training, testing, and validation.
Specific attention must be given to the data aspects: providers must disclose the type and provenance of training, testing, and validation data, as well as any data curation methodologies employed. This information is intended to enable downstream integrators to assess compatibility with their systems, compliance with acceptable use policies, licensing terms, and safety considerations.
Overall, the transparency information creates a baseline of technical and governance details that downstream providers can rely on when evaluating integration, use, and risk management of the general-purpose AI model.

XIII.unit_title: Criteria for the designation of general-purpose AI models with systemic risk referred to in Article 51

context.regulation: (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council

XIII.summary_1: Annex XIII sets the criteria for designating general-purpose AI models with systemic risk under Article 51, focusing on model scale, data quality, compute, input/output modalities, benchmarks, market reach, and user base to determine high-impact potential. It specifies seven factors (parameters, data quality/size, training compute, modalities and thresholds, evaluation benchmarks, market impact, and registered end-users) that guide designation. The criteria emphasize quantifiable metrics and market exposure to identify models requiring higher regulatory oversight.
XIII.summary_2:
To determine that a general-purpose AI model has capabilities or an impact equivalent to those in Article 51(1)(a), the Commission shall consider seven criteria. First, the number of parameters of the model. Second, the quality or size of the data set, for example measured through tokens. Third, the amount of computation used for training the model, measured in floating point operations or indicated by related proxies such as estimated training cost, time, or energy consumption. Fourth, the input and output modalities of the model and the state-of-the-art thresholds for determining high-impact capabilities for each modality, including examples like text-to-text, text-to-image, multi-modality, and inputs/outputs such as biological sequences.
Additionally, the Commission assesses the model's benchmarks and evaluations of capabilities, including the number of tasks it can perform without additional training, its adaptability to learn new tasks, its level of autonomy and scalability, and the tools it has access to. It also considers whether the model has a high impact on the internal market due to its reach, which shall be presumed when it is made available to at least 10,000 registered business users established in the Union. Finally, it takes into account the number of registered end-users.
